Q 8

Solution

We have {tex}\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{14}{3} \div \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{4}{7} \times\left(\frac{14}{3} \times \frac{3}{2}\right){/tex}

Q 9

Using appropriate properties, find {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{-5}{7}+\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{-2}{7}{/tex}.

Solution

{tex}\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-5}{7}\right)+\frac{7}{3}+\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-2}{7}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-5}{7} \times \frac{2}{3}-\frac{2}{7} \times \frac{2}{3}+\frac{7}{3}{/tex}(by commutativity)
{tex}=\left(\frac{-5}{7}-\frac{2}{7}\right) \times \frac{2}{3}+\frac{7}{3}{/tex}(by distributivity)
{tex}=-\frac{2}{3}+\frac{7}{3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{3}{/tex}

Q 10

Let O, P and Z represent the numbers 0, 3 and -5 respectively on the number line. Points Q, R and S are between O and P such that OQ = QR = RS = SP.
What are the rational numbers represented by the points Q, R and S. Next choose a point T between Z and O so that ZT = TO. Which rational number does T represent?

Solution


As it is given that OQ = QR = RS = SP
and OQ + QR + RS + SP = OP
therefore Q, R and S divide OP into four equal parts.
So, R is the mid-point of OP, i.e. {tex}R=\frac{0+3}{2}=\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Q is the mid-point of OR, i.e. {tex}​​​​\mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{2}\left(0+\frac{3}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
and S is the mid-point of RO i.e., {tex}\mathrm{Q}=\frac{1}{2}\left(0+\frac{3}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
therefore, {tex}Q=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}, {tex}R=\frac{3}{2}{/tex} and {tex}S=\frac{9}{4}{/tex}
Also ZT = TO
So, T is the mid-point of OZ, i.e. {tex}T=\frac{0+(-5)}{2}=\frac{-5}{2}{/tex}.

Q 11

A farmer has a field of area {tex}49 \frac{4}{5}{/tex} ha. He wants to divide it equally among his one son and two daughters. Find the area of each one’s share. (ha means hectare; 1 hectare = 10,000 m2).

Solution

We have {tex}49 \frac{4}{5} h a=\frac{249}{5} h a{/tex}
Each share = {tex}(\frac{249}{5} \div{3} ) \mathrm{ha}{/tex}  = {tex}( \frac{249}{5} \times\frac{1}{3} )\mathrm{ha}{/tex}= {tex}16 \frac{3}{5} \mathrm{ha}{/tex}.

Q 12

Let a, b, c be the three rational numbers where a = {tex}\frac 23{/tex}, b = {tex}\frac 45{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac {-5}{ 6}{/tex} then verify that a + (b + c) = (a + b) + c (Associative property of addition).

Solution

Taking L.H.S = a + (b + c)
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}+\left[\frac{4}{5}+\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}+\left[\frac{24-25}{30}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}+\left(\frac{-1}{30}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{20-1}{30}=\frac{19}{30}{/tex}
Now taking R.H.S. = (a + b) + c
{tex}=\left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\left(\frac{10+12}{15}\right)+\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{22}{15}-\frac{5}{6}=\frac{44-25}{30}=\frac{19}{30}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{2}{3}+\left[\frac{4}{5}+\left(\frac{-5}{2}\right)\right]=\left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{4}{5}\right)+\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right) {/tex}
Hence, verified.

Q 13

Let a, b, c be the three rational numbers where a = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, b = {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{-5}{6}{/tex} then verify that a {tex}\times{/tex} (b {tex}\times{/tex} c) = (a {tex}\times{/tex} b) {tex}\times{/tex} c (Associative property of multiplication)

Solution

Taking L.H.S = a {tex}\times{/tex} (b {tex}\times{/tex} c)
{tex}=\frac{2}{3} \times\left[\frac{4}{5} \times\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-20}{30}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2 \times(-2)}{3 \times 3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-4}{9}{/tex}
Taking R.H.S. = (a {tex}\times{/tex} b) {tex}\times{/tex} c
{tex}=(\frac{2}{3}\times \frac{4}{5}) \times \frac{-5}{6}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{15} \times\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-40}{90}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-4}{9}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times\left[\frac{4}{5} \times\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right)\right]=\left[\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{4}{5}\right] \times\left(\frac{-5}{6}\right){/tex}

Q 14

Solve the following questions and write your observations.

  1. {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} + 0 =?
  2. {tex}\frac{-2}{5}{/tex} + 0 = ?
  3. {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} + 0 = ?
  4. {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times 1{/tex} = ?
  5. {tex}\frac{-6}{7} \times 1{/tex} = ?
  6. {tex}\frac{9}{8} \times 1{/tex} = ?
Solution

We have 

  1. {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex} + 0 = {tex}\frac{5}{3}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{-2}{5}{/tex} + 0 = {tex}\frac{-2}{5}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex} + 0 = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times 1=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
  5. {tex}\frac{-6}{7} \times 1=\frac{-6}{7}{/tex}
  6. {tex}\frac{9}{8} \times 1=\frac{9}{8}{/tex}
Q 15

Write any 5 rational numbers between {tex}\frac{-5}{6}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex}.

Solution

We have {tex}\frac{-5}{6}=\frac{-5 \times 4}{6 \times 4}=\frac{-20}{24}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{7}{8}=\frac{7 \times 3}{8 \times 3}=\frac{21}{24}{/tex}
Thus, rational numbers {tex}\frac{-19}{24}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-18}{24}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-17}{24}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{19}{24}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{20}{24}{/tex} lie between {tex}-5 \over 6{/tex} and {tex}7 \over 8{/tex}.

Q 16

Identify the rational number which is different from the other three :{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{-4}{5}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}. Explain your reasoning.

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{-4}{5}{/tex} is the rational number which is different from the other three rational numbers, as it lies on the left side of zero while others lie on the right side of zero on the number line.

Q 17

The product of two rational numbers is –7. If one of the numbers is -10, find the other.

Solution

Understand and explore the given problem:

  1. What information is given in the question?
    One of the two rational numbers
    Product of two rational numbers
  2. What are you finding? The other rational number.

Plan a strategy: Let the unknown rational number be x. Form an equation with the conditions given. Then solve the equation.
Solution:
According to given condition, 
- 10 {tex}\times{/tex} x = - 7
x = {tex}\frac{-7}{-10}{/tex}, 
{tex}x=\frac{7}{10}{/tex}
Check:
{tex}-10 \times \frac{7}{10}=-7{/tex} 
Therefore, the result is correct.

Q 28

Between the numbers {tex}\frac{15}{20} {/tex} and {tex}\frac {35}{40}{/tex}, the greater number is ________.

Solution

{tex}(\frac{35}{40}){/tex}

Given numbers are {tex}\frac{15}{20} \;and\; \frac {35}{40}{/tex}.

LCM of 20 and 40 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 40
Now, {tex}\frac{15}{20}=\frac{15}{20} \times \frac{2}{2}{/tex} [on multiplying and dividing by 2]
= {tex}\frac{30}{40}{/tex}
On comparing,
{tex}\frac{35}{40}>\frac{30}{40}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{35}{40}>\frac{15}{20}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{35}{40}{/tex} is greater than {tex}\frac{15}{20}{/tex}.

Q 102

Using suitable rearrangement find the sum: {tex}\frac{4}{7}+\left(\frac{-4}{9}\right)+\frac{3}{7}+\left(\frac{-13}{9}\right){/tex}

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{4}{7}+\left(\frac{-4}{9}\right)+\frac{3}{7}+\left(\frac{-13}{9}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{4}{7}+\frac{3}{7}+\left(\frac{-4}{9}\right)+\left(\frac{-13}{9}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{7}{7}-\frac{17}{9}{/tex} 
= {tex}1-\frac{17}{9}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{9-17}{9}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-8}{9}{/tex}

Q 103

Using suitable rearrangement find the sum: {tex}-5+\frac{7}{10}+\frac{3}{7}+(-3)+\frac{5}{14}+\frac{-4}{5}{/tex}

Solution

We have, {tex}-5+\frac{7}{10}+\frac{3}{7}+(-3)+\frac{5}{14}+\left(\frac{-4}{5}\right){/tex} 
= -5 + (-3) + {tex}\frac{7}{10}+\left(\frac{-4}{5}\right)+\frac{3}{7}+\frac{5}{14}{/tex} 
= -8 + {tex}\frac{7-8}{10}+\frac{6+5}{14}{/tex} = -8 - {tex}\frac{1}{10}+\frac{11}{14}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-560-7+55}{70}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-512}{70}=\frac{-256}{35}{/tex}

Q 104

Verify – (– x) = x for x = {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}

Solution

We have, x = {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -x = {tex}\frac{-3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(-x) = {tex}-\left(\frac{-3}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(-x) = {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -(-x) = x
hence proved.

hence proved.

 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}-(-x) = x
hence proved.

Q 107

Give one example each to show that the rational numbers are closed under addition, subtraction and multiplication. Are rational numbers closed under division? Give two examples in support of your answer.

Solution

We know that, rational numbers are closed under addition, subtraction and multiplication. We can understand this from the following examples.
Rational numbers are closed under addition
e.g. {tex}\frac{4}{7}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{8+7}{14}=\frac{15}{14}{/tex}, which is a rational number.
Subtraction
e.g. {tex}\frac{4}{7}-\frac{1}{2}=\frac{8-7}{14}=\frac{1}{14}{/tex}, which is a rational number.
Multiplication
e.g. {tex}\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{4}{14}=\frac{2}{7}{/tex}, which is a rational number.
But rational are not closed under division. If zero is excluded from the collection of rational numbers, then we can say that rational numbers are closed under division.
Now, we see the examples given below:
{tex}\frac{4}{7}\div\frac{1}{2}=\frac{4}{7} \times 2=\frac{8}{7}{/tex}, which is a rational number.
But {tex}\frac{4}{7}\div0=\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{1}{0}{/tex},
Which is not defined and so, it is not a rational number.
Also, {tex}\frac{1}{2}\div0=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{0}{/tex},
which is not defined and so, it is not a rational number.

Q 108

Verify the property x + y = y + x of rational number by taking {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}y=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}y=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Then, LHS = x + y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex}
RHS = y + x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS
Hence, x + y = y + x 

Q 109

Verify the property x + y = y + x of rational number by taking {tex}x=\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}y=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x=\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}y=\frac{-5}{6}{/tex}
Then, LHS = x + y = {tex}\frac{-2}{3}+\frac{-5}{6}=\frac{-2}{3}-\frac{5}{6}=\frac{-4-5}{6}=\frac{-9}{6}{/tex}
and RHS = y + x = {tex}\frac{-5}{6}+\frac{-2}{3}=\frac{-5}{6}-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{-5-4}{6}=\frac{-9}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS
Hence, x + y = y + x

Q 119

Q 120

Verify the property x {tex}\times{/tex} (y {tex}\times{/tex} z) = (x {tex}\times{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z of rational number by using x = 1, y = {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and What is the name of this property?

and RHS = (x {tex}\times{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z = {tex}\left(1 \times \frac{-1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{-1}{2} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{-1}{8}{/tex}

Hence,  x {tex}\times{/tex} (y {tex}\times{/tex} z) =  (x {tex}\times{/tex} y) {tex}\times{/tex} z

Q 121

= LHS = RHS

Q 124

Verify the property x {tex}\times{/tex} ( y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z of rational number where {tex}x=\frac{-1}{2}, y=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x=\frac{-1}{2}, y=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Now, LHS = x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \times\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}\right)=\frac{-1}{2} \times \frac{4}{4}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
and RHS = (x {tex}\times{/tex} y) + (x {tex}\times{/tex} z) = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \times \frac{3}{4}+\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{-3}{8}-\frac{1}{8}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-3-1}{8}=\frac{-4}{8}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS
Hence,   x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z

Q 125

Verify the property x {tex}\times{/tex} ( y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z of rational number where {tex}x=\frac{-1}{2}, y=\frac{2}{3}{/tex} and {tex} z=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x=\frac{-1}{2}, y=\frac{2}{3} {/tex} and {tex} z=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
Now, LHS = x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \times\left(\frac{2}{3}+\frac{3}{4}\right)=\frac{-1}{2} \times\left(\frac{8+9}{12}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-17}{24}{/tex}
and RHS =  (x {tex}\times{/tex} y) + (x {tex}\times{/tex} z) = {tex}\frac{-1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3}+\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right) \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-1}{3}-\frac{3}{8}=\frac{-8-9}{24}=\frac{-17}{24}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS
Hence, x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z

Q 126

Verify the property x {tex}\times{/tex} ( y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z of rational number where {tex}x=\frac{-2}{3}, y=\frac{-4}{6} {/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{-7}{9}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x=\frac{-2}{3}, y=\frac{-4}{6} \text { and } z=\frac{-7}{9}{/tex}
Now, LHS = x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = {tex}\frac{-2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-4}{6}+\frac{-7}{9}\right){/tex}{tex}=\frac{-2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-4}{6}-\frac{7}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-12-14}{18}\right)=\frac{-2}{3} \times \frac{-26}{18}=\frac{26}{27}{/tex}
and RHS = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z = {tex}\frac{-2}{3} \times\left(\frac{-4}{6}\right)+\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right) \times\left(\frac{-7}{9}\right){/tex}{tex}=\frac{4}{9}+\frac{14}{27}=\frac{12+14}{27}=\frac{26}{27}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS
Hence,  x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z

Q 127

Verify the property x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z of rational number where {tex}x=\frac{-1}{5}, y=\frac{2}{15}{/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{-3}{10}{/tex}

Solution

We have given, {tex}x=\frac{-1}{5}, y=\frac{2}{15}{/tex} and {tex}z=\frac{-3}{10}{/tex}
Now taking L.H.S. = x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) 
= {tex}\frac{-1}{5} \times\left(\frac{2}{15}+\frac{-3}{10}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{5} \times\left(\frac{2}{15}-\frac{3}{10}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-1}{5} \times\left(\frac{4-9}{30}\right){/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{-1}{5} \times \frac{-5}{30}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{30}{/tex}
Now taking R.H.S. = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z 
= {tex}\frac{-1}{5} \times \frac{2}{15}+(\frac{-1}{5})\times(\frac{-3}{10}){/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{-2}{75}+\frac{3}{50}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac {{14}+{9}}{150}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac {5}{150}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac {1}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} L.H.S. = R.H.S.
Hence,  x {tex}\times{/tex} (y + z) = x {tex}\times{/tex} y + x {tex}\times{/tex} z

Q 137

The cost of {tex}\frac {19}{4}{/tex}m of wire is ₹{tex}\frac{171}{2}{/tex}. Find the cost of one metre of the wire.

Solution

The cost of {tex}\frac {19}{4}{/tex}m of wire = ₹{tex}\frac{171}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of 1 m of wire = {tex}\frac{171}{2}\div\frac{19}{4}=\frac{171}{2} \times \frac{4}{19}{/tex}= 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = ₹18

Q 138

A train travels {tex}\frac {1445}{2}{/tex}km in {tex}\frac {17}{2}{/tex}h. Find the speed of the train in km/h.

Solution

Here, distance travelled by trian = {tex}\frac {1445}{2}{/tex} km.
Time taken by trian =  {tex}\frac {17}{2}{/tex}h
{tex} \because{/tex} Speed of train = {tex}\frac {distance\; travelled \;by \;train}{Time \;taken \;by\;train}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{1445}{2}}{\frac{17}{2}}=\frac{1445}{2} \times \frac{2}{17} {/tex} = 85 km/h
Hence, the speed of the train is 85 km/h.

Q 139

If 16 shirts of equal size can be made out of 24m of cloth, how much cloth is needed for making one shirt?

Solution

If 16 shirts are to be made by cloth of 24 m
Then, 1 shirt is to be made by cloth of = {tex}\frac{24}{16} =\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = 1.5 m

Q 140

{tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex} of all the money in Hamid's bank account is ₹ 77000. How much money does Hamid have in his bank account?

Solution

Let money in Hamid’s bank account be ₹ x.
Given, ​​{tex}\frac {7}{11}{/tex} of all the money in Hamid’s bank account = ₹ 77000
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{7}{11} \times x=77000{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{77000 \times 11}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=11000 \times 11{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 121000
Hence, Hamid has ₹121000 in his bank account

Q 141

A {tex}117\frac{1}{3}{/tex}m  long rope is cut into equal pieces measuring {tex}7\frac{1}{3}{/tex}m each. How many such small pieces are these?

Solution

We have, length of rope = {tex}117\frac{1}{3}{/tex}m {tex}=\frac{117 \times 3+1}{3} =\frac{352}{3}{/tex}
Length of each piece = {tex}7 \frac{1}{3}=\frac{22}{3}{/tex}
So, the number of pieces of the rope = {tex}\frac {Total\; length \;of\; the \;rope}{Length\; of \;each \;piece}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{352}{3}}{\frac{22}{3}}=\frac{352}{3} \times \frac{3}{22}=16{/tex}
Hence, the number of small pieces cut from the {tex}117\frac{1}{3}{/tex}m long rope is 16.

Q 142

{tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} of the class students are above average, {tex}\frac {1}{4}{/tex} are average and rest are below average. If there are 48 students in all, how many students are below average in the class?

Solution

Number of above average students = {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} of the class students
Number of average students = {tex}\frac {1}{6}{/tex} of the class students
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of below average students = 1 - {tex}\left[\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{4}\right]{/tex} of the class students
{tex}=1-\left[\frac{2+3}{12}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}1-\frac{5}{12}=\frac{7}{12}{/tex} of the class students
Since, number of students in the class = 48 [given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of below average students = {tex}\frac{7}{12} \times 48{/tex} = 28 
So, number of below average students are 28.

Q 143

{tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} of total number of students of a school come by car while {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} of students come by bus to school. All the other students walk to school, of which {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} walk on their own and the rest are escorted by their parents. If 224 students come to school walking on their own, how many students study in that school?

Solution

We have,
Let the number of students who study in school be x
Number of students come by car = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \times x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{5} x{/tex}
Number of students come by bus = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \times x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4} x{/tex}
Remaining students walk to school = x - {tex}\left(\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{4} x\right){/tex}
{tex}=x-\left(\frac{8 x+5 x}{20}\right){/tex}
= {tex}x-\frac{13 x}{20}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{20 x-13 x}{20}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7 x}{20}{/tex}
Now, the number of students walk to school on their own = {tex}\frac{1}{3} {/tex} of {tex}\frac{7 x}{20}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7 x}{60}{/tex}
Since, 224 students come to school on their own.
According to the question, 
{tex}\frac{7 x}{60}=224{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{224 \times 60}{7}{/tex}
= 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 60
= 1920
Therefore, 1920 students study in that school.

Q 144

Huma, Hubna and Seema received a total of ₹2016 as monthly allowance from their mother such that Seema gets {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} of what Hubna gets and Huma gets {tex}1\frac {2}{3}{/tex} times Seema's share. How much money do the three sisters get individually?

Solution

We have given that:
Seema gets allowance = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}of Huma's share
and Hubna gets allowance = {tex}1\frac {2}{3}{/tex}of Seema's share
= {tex}\frac {5}{3}{/tex} of Seema's share
= {tex}\frac {5}{3}{/tex} of {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} of Huma's share [{tex}\because{/tex} Seema's share = {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} of Huma's share]
= {tex}\frac {5}{3}\times \frac {1}{2}{/tex} of Huma's share
= {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex} of Huma's share
Also, it is given that Huma, Hubna and Seema received total monthly allowance from their mother = ₹2016
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Huma’s share + Hubna’s share + Seema’s share = ₹2016
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1 of Huma's share + {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex} of Huma's share + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} of Huma's share = ₹2016
So, {tex}\left(1+\frac{5}{6}+\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} of Huma's share = ₹2016
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{14}{6}{/tex}of Huma's share = ₹2016
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Huma's share {tex}=₹\; 2016 \times \frac{6}{14}{/tex}
{tex}=144 \times 6{/tex}
 = ₹ 864
So, Seema’s share = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} of {tex}864{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 864{/tex} 
= ₹432
and Hubna’s share = {tex}\frac {5}{6}{/tex} of 864
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 144
= ₹ 720
Hence, Huma, Hubna and Seema get ₹864, ₹720 and ₹432 respectively.

Q 145

 A mother and her two daughters got a room constructed for ₹62000. The elder daughter contributes {tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex} of her mother's contribution while the 1 younger daughter contributes {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} of her mother's share. How much do the three contribute individually?

Solution

We have,
Let the mother’s share be ₹x
Now, elder daughter’s share = ₹{tex}\frac{3}{8}{/tex}x
and younger daughter's share = ₹{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x
According to the question, 
{tex}x+\frac{3}{8} x+\frac{x}{2}{/tex} = 62000
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{8 x+3 x+4 x}{8}{/tex} = 62000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 15x = 62000 {tex}\times{/tex} 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {62000\times8}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {12400\times8}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{99200}{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}x = 33066.6{/tex}
So, mother's share = ₹33066.6
Elder daughter's share = {tex}\frac{3}{8} \times \frac{99200}{3}{/tex} 
= ₹12400
Younger daughter’s share = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{99200}{3}{/tex}
= ₹16533.3
Therefore, mother and her two daughters contributed ₹ 33066.6, ₹12400 and ₹16533.3, respectively.

Q 146

Tell which property allows you to compare {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times\left[\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{5}{7}\right]{/tex} and {tex}\left[\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{7}\right] \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{2}{3} \times\left[\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{5}{7}\right]=\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{5}{7} \times \frac{3}{4}\right){/tex} [by commutative property over multiplication]
{tex}=\left(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{7}\right) \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} [by associative property over multiplication]
Hence, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \times\left(\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{5}{7}\right){/tex} can be compared with {tex}\left(\frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{7}\right) \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex} with the help of associative and commutative property.

Q 154

Arrange the numbers {tex}\frac{1}{4}, \frac{13}{16}, \frac{5}{8}{/tex} in the descending order.

Solution

Given numbers are ​​{tex}\frac{1}{4}, \frac{13}{16}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex}.
First, we convert the number as like denominator.

Taking LCM of 4, 16, 8 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 16
Now, {tex}\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{4}{4}=\frac{4}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{2}{2}=\frac{10}{16}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{13}{16}>\frac{10}{16}>\frac{4}{16}{/tex}
i.e. {tex}\frac{13}{16}>\frac{5}{8}>\frac{1}{4}{/tex}

Q 155

The product of two rational numbers is {tex}\frac{-14}{27}{/tex}. If one of the numbers be {tex}\frac{7}{9}{/tex}, find the other.

Solution

It is given, one number = {tex}\frac{7}{9}{/tex} 
Let other number be x.
According to the question,
One number {tex}\times{/tex} Other numbers = Product of two numbers 
{tex}\frac{7 x}{9}=\frac{-14}{27}{/tex} 
x = {tex}\frac{-14}{27} \times \frac{9}{7}{/tex} 
x = {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex} 
Hence, the other number is {tex}\frac{-2}{3}{/tex}

Q 156

x = -13 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 = - 39

Q 162

From a rope 40 m long, pieces of equal size are cut. If the length of one piece is {tex}\frac {10}{3}{/tex}m, find the number of such pieces.

Solution

Total length of rope = 40 m
Length of one piece = {tex}\frac {10}{3}{/tex} m
Let the number of pieces be x.
Then, according to the question, {tex}\frac {10x}{3} = 40{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ​​{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {10\times4\times3}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ​​{/tex} x = 12
Hence, the number of pieces cut from the rope are 12.

Q 163

{tex}5\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m long rope is cut into 12 equal pieces. What is the length of each piece?

Solution

Total length of the rope = {tex}5\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m = {tex}\frac{11}{2}{/tex}m
Total number of pieces = 12
Let the length of each piece be x m.
According to the question, {tex}12 x=\frac{11}{2}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{11}{2 \times 12}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{11}{24} \mathrm{m}{/tex} 
Hence, the length of each piece is {tex}\frac{11}{24}{/tex}m.

Q 164

Write the following rational numbers in the descending order.
{tex}\frac{8}{7}, \frac{-9}{8}, \frac{-3}{2}, 0, \frac{2}{5}{/tex}

Solution

Given numbers are {tex}\frac{8}{7}, \frac{-9}{8}, \frac{-3}{2}, 0, \frac{2}{5}{/tex}
First, we convert the given numbers as like denominators.
LCM of 7, 8, 2, 5 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 280
Now, {tex}\frac{8}{7}=\frac{8}{7} \times \frac{40}{40}=\frac{320}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-9}{8}=\frac{-9}{8} \times \frac{35}{35}=\frac{-315}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{-3}{2}=\frac{-3}{2} \times \frac{140}{140}=\frac{-420}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{2}{5}=\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{56}{56}=\frac{112}{280}{/tex}
In descending order,
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{320}{280}>\frac{112}{280}>0>\frac{-315}{280}>\frac{-420}{280}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{8}{7}>\frac{2}{5}>0>\frac{-9}{8}>\frac{-3}{2}{/tex}

Find: {tex}0 \div \frac{2}{3}{/tex}

Q 167

On a winter day the temperature at a place in Himachal Pradesh was -16°C. Convert it in degree Fahrenheit (°F) by using the formula {tex}\frac{c}{5}=\frac{F-32}{9}{/tex}

Solution

Given, temperature of Himachal Pradesh = - 16 °C
{tex}\because \quad \frac{C}{5}=\frac{F-32}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{-16}{5}=\frac{F-32}{9}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad F-32=-\frac{144}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad F=32-\frac{144}{5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad F=\frac{160-144}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{16}{5}=3.2^{\circ} \mathrm{F}{/tex}

Q 168

Find the sum of additive inverse and multiplicative inverse of 7.

Solution

The additive inverse of 7 = -7
The multiplicative inverse of 7 = {tex}\frac {1}{7}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required sum = - 7 + {tex}\frac{1}{7}=\frac{-49+1}{7}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-48}{7}=-6 \frac{6}{7}{/tex}

Q 169

Find the product of additive inverse and multiplicative inverse of {tex}\frac {-1}{3}{/tex}.

Solution

The additive inverse of {tex}\frac{-1}{3}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
The multiplicative inverse of {tex}\frac{-1}{3}=-3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required product = {tex}\frac{1}{3}\times-3{/tex} = -1

Q 170

The diagram shows the wingspans of different species of birds. Use the diagram to answer the question given below.

  1. How much longer is the wingspan of an Albatross than the wingspan of a Sea gull?
  2. How much longer is the wingspan of a Golden eagle than the wingspan of a Blue jay?
Solution

We have,

  1. Length of the wingspan of Albatross = {tex}3 \frac{3}{5} m{/tex}
    and length of the wingspan of a Sea gull = {tex}1 \frac{7}{10} m{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Difference = {tex}3 \frac{3}{5}-1 \frac{7}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{18}{5}-\frac{17}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{36-17}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{19}{10} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    Hence, the wingspan of an Albatross is {tex}\frac{19}{10}{/tex} m longer than the wingspan of a Sea gull.
  2. Length of the wingspan of Golden eagle = {tex}2 \frac{1}{2} m{/tex}
    and length of the wingspan of a Blue jay = {tex}\frac{41}{100} m{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Difference = {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}- \frac{41}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5}{2}-\frac{41}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{250-41}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{209}{100} m{/tex}
    Therefore, the wingspan of a Golden eagles is {tex}\frac{209}{100}{/tex} m longer than the wingspan of a Blue jay.
Q 171

Shalini has to cut out circles of diameter {tex}1\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm from an aluminium strip of dimensions {tex}8\frac{3}{4}{/tex}cm by {tex}1\frac{1}{4}{/tex} cm. How many full circles can Shalini cut? Also, calculate the wastage of the aluminium strip.

Solution

We have,
Breadth of the circle = Diameter of one circle {tex}1\frac{1}{4}{/tex}cm
= {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} cm
Length of aluminium strip = {tex}8\frac{3}{4}{/tex} cm
= {tex}\frac{35}{4}{/tex} cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of full circles cut from the aluminium strip {tex}=\frac{35}{4}\div\frac{5}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{35}{4} \times \frac{4}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=7{/tex}
Hence, the number of circle 7.

Now, the diameter of circle = {tex}\frac{5}{4}{/tex} cm
Radius of circle = {tex}\frac{5}{4 \times 2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{8} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Now, area to be cut by one circle = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{22}{7} \times\left(\frac{5}{8}\right)^{2}{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{25}{64} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area to be cut by 7 full circles = {tex}7 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{25}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{22 \times 25}{64}\mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Also, area of the aluminium strip = Length x Breadth {tex}=\frac{35}{4} \times \frac{5}{4} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Wastage of aluminium strip = {tex}=\left(\frac{35}{4} \times \frac{5}{4}\right)-\left(\frac{22 \times 25}{64}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{175}{16}-\frac{550}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{700-550}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{150}{64}{/tex}
{tex}=2 \frac{11}{32} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Therefore, the wastage of aluminium strip = {tex}2\frac{11}{32}{/tex} cm2

Q 172

One fruit salad recipe requires {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cup of sugar. Another recipe for the same fruit salad requires 2 tablespoons of sugar. If 1 tablespoon is equivalent to {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex} cup, how much more sugar does the first recipe require?

Solution

Given, sugar required for one fruit salad = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} cup
Sugar required for another salad = {tex}2 \times \frac{1}{16}=\frac{2}{16}{/tex}cup
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required sugar = {tex}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{16}=\frac{8-2}{16}=\frac{6}{16}=\frac{3}{8} {/tex} cup

Q 173

Four friends had a competition to see how far could they hop on one foot. The table given shows the distance covered by each.

Name Distance covered (in km)
Seema {tex}\frac {1}{25}{/tex}
Nancy {tex}\frac {1}{32}{/tex}
Megha {tex}\frac {1}{40}{/tex}
Soni {tex}\frac {1}{20}{/tex}
  1. How farther did Soni hop than Nancy?
  2. What is the total distance covered by Seema and Megha?
  3. Who walked farther, Nancy or Megha?
Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{1}{25}, \frac{1}{32}, \frac{1}{40}, \frac{1}{20}{/tex}
At first, we convert the numbers as like denominators.

Taking LCM of 25, 32, 40 and 20 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 800
we get,
{tex}\frac{1}{25}=\frac{1 \times 32}{25 \times 32}=\frac{32}{800}, \frac{1}{32}=\frac{1 \times 25}{32 \times 25}=\frac{25}{800} ; \frac{1}{40}=\frac{1 \times 20}{40 \times 20}=\frac{20}{800}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1 \times 40}{20 \times 40}=\frac{40}{800}{/tex}

  1. Soni hop more than Nancy {tex}=\frac{40}{800}-\frac{25}{800}=\frac{40-25}{800}=\frac{15}{800}=\frac{3}{160} {/tex}
  2. Total distance covered by Seema and Megha {tex}=\frac{32}{800}+\frac{20}{800}=\frac{32+20}{800}=\frac{52}{800}=\frac{13}{200} {/tex}
  3. It is clear that Nancy walked farther than Megha.

Q 175

The table shows the portion of some common materials that are recycled.

Material Recycled
Paper {tex}\frac {5}{11}{/tex}
Aluminium cans {tex}\frac {5}{8}{/tex}
Glass {tex}\frac {2}{5}{/tex}
Scrap {tex}\frac {3}{4}{/tex}
  1. Is the rational number expressing the amount of paper recycled more than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} or less than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}?
  2. Which items have a recycled amount less than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}?
  3. Is the quantity of aluminium cans recycled more (or less) than half of the quantity of aluminium cans?
  4. Arrange the rate of recycling the materials from the greatest to the smallest.
Solution

We have, 

  1. Here, {tex}\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{11}{11}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{11}{22}{/tex}
    and {tex}\frac{5}{11}=\frac{5}{11} \times \frac{2}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{10}{22}{/tex}
    Hence, paper recycled is less than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}
  2. Similarly, {tex}\frac {5}{8}{/tex} is greater than {tex}\frac {1}{2}=(\frac{4}{8}){/tex}
    Also {tex}\frac{2}{5}=\frac{2 \times 2}{5 \times 2}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{4}{10}<\frac{1}{2}\left(i.e.=\frac{5}{10}\right){/tex}
    And {tex}\frac{3}{4}>\frac{1}{2}\left(i.e.=\frac{2}{4}\right){/tex}
    Hence, the quantity of paper and glass recycled is less than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}
  3. Quantity of aluminium cans {tex}=\frac{5}{8}\left(i.e.=\frac{10}{16}\right){/tex} is more than {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex} of the quantity of aluminium cans {tex}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{5}{16}{/tex}
  4. Taking LCM of 11,8, 5, and 4 = 440
    Now, {tex}\frac{5}{11}=\frac{5}{11} \times \frac{40}{40}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{200}{440}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\frac{5}{8}=\frac{5}{8} \times \frac{55}{55}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{275}{440}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\frac{2}{5}=\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{88}{88}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{176}{440}{/tex}
    Now, {tex}\frac{3}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{110}{110}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{330}{440}{/tex}
    As, {tex}\frac{330}{440}>\frac{275}{440}>\frac{200}{440}>\frac{176}{440}{/tex}
    i.e, {tex}\frac{3}{4}>\frac{5}{8}>\frac{5}{11}>\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
    Therefore, the rate of recycling the materials from the greatest to the smallest is given as:
    Scrap > Aluminium cans > Paper > Glass
Q 176

The overall width in cm of several wide-screen televisions is 97.28 cm, {tex}98\frac{4}{9}{/tex}cm, {tex}98\frac{1}{25}{/tex}cm and 97.94 cm. Express these numbers as rational numbers in the form {tex}\frac{p}{q}{/tex} and arrange the widths in ascending order.

Solution

We have,
The width of the televisions screen are 97.28 cm,  {tex}98\frac{4}{9}{/tex}cm, {tex}98\frac{1}{25}{/tex}cm and 97.94 cm.
Then, at first, we convert all widths in the rational numbers.

  1. {tex}97.28 \mathrm{cm}=\frac{9728}{100}{/tex}[After removing decimal]
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{p}{q}=\frac{2432}{25} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [numerator and denominator both dividing by 4]
  2. {tex}98 \frac{4}{9} \mathrm{cm}=\frac{886}{9} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [convert mixed fraction into simple fraction]
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{p}{q}=\frac{886}{9} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
  3. {tex}98 \frac{1}{25} \mathrm{cm}=\frac{2451}{25} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [convert mixed fraction into simple fraction]
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{p}{q}=\frac{2451}{25} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
  4. {tex}97.94 \mathrm{cm}=\frac{9794}{100}{/tex}[after removing decimal]
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{p}{q}=\frac{4897}{50} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [numerator and denominator both dividing by 2]
    To arrange in ascending order, firstly we convert all the denominators same, then we get

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} LCM of 25, 9, 25, 50 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 9
    = 450
    S0, {tex}\frac{2432}{25}=\frac{2432 \times 18}{25 \times 18}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{43776}{450}{/tex}
    Also, {tex} \frac{886}{9}=\frac{886 \times 50}{9 \times 50}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{44300}{450}{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\frac{2451}{25}=\frac{2451 \times 18}{25 \times 18}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{44118}{450}{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\frac{4897}{50}=\frac{4897 \times 9}{50 \times 9}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{44073}{450}{/tex}
    In ascending order, {tex}\frac{43776}{450}>\frac{44073}{450}>\frac{44118}{450}>\frac{44300}{450}{/tex}
    i.e. 97.28 cm > 97.94 cm < {tex}98 \frac{1}{25} \mathrm{cm}<98 \frac{4}{9} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Q 177

Roller coaster at an amusement park is {tex}\frac {2}{3}{/tex} m high. If a new roller coaster is built that is {tex}\frac {3}{5}{/tex} times the height of the existing coaster, what will be the height of the new roller coaster?

Solution

Given, height of the existing roller coaster = {tex}\frac {2}{3}{/tex} m
Height of new roller coaster = {tex}\frac {3}{5}{/tex} of height of the existing roller coaster {tex}=\frac{3}{5} \times \frac{2}{3}=\frac{2}{5} {/tex}m

Months Above/Below normal (in cm)
May 2.6924
June 0.6096

    = {tex}\frac{26924}{10000}{/tex} [After removing decimal]
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{p}{q}=\frac{6731}{2500} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [after dividing numerator and denominator by 4]
  2. June = 0.6096
    = {tex}\frac{6096}{10000}{/tex} [After removing decimal]
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{P}{q}=\frac{381}{625} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [after dividing numerator and denominator by 16]
  3. July = - 6.9088
    = {tex}-\frac{69088}{10000}{/tex} [After removing decimal]
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{p}{q}=-\frac{4318}{625} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [after dividing numerator and denomiator by 16]
  4. August = -8.636
    = {tex}-\frac{8636}{1000}{/tex} [After removing decimal]
    {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{p}{q}=-\frac{2159}{250} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} [after dividing numerator and denominator by 4]

Q 180

A skirt that is {tex}35 \frac{7}{8}{/tex}cm long has a hem of {tex}3 \frac{1}{8}{/tex}cm. How long will the skirt be if the hem is let down?

Solution

We have, Length of the skirt =  {tex}35 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} cm = {tex}\frac{287}{8}{/tex}cm
Dimension of hem =  {tex}3 \frac{1}{8}{/tex} cm = {tex}\frac{25}{8}{/tex}cm
Length of skirt, if hem is let down = {tex}\left(\frac{287}{8}-\frac{25}{8}\right) \mathrm{cm}=\frac{262}{8} \mathrm{cm}{/tex} 
= 32.75 cm
Hence, the length of the skirt if the hem is let down = 32.75 cm

Q 181

Manavi and Kuber each receive an equal allowance. The table shows the fraction of their allowance each deposit into his/her saving account and the fraction each spends at the mall. If the allowance of each is ₹1260, find the amount left with each.

Where money goes Fraction of allowance
  Manavi Kuber
Saving account {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Spend at mall {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
Left over ? ?
Solution

Let the total cost be ₹1.
For Manavi,
Left over = Total cost - All spends
= 1- {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1-\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Amount left over for Manavi = {tex}1260 \times \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}= ₹ \:315{/tex}
For Kuber,
Left over = Total cost - All spends
= 1 - {tex}\left(\frac{1}{3}+\frac{3}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1-\frac{14}{15}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Amount left over for Kuber = {tex}1260 \times \frac{1}{15}{/tex}
{tex}=₹\;84{/tex}
Alternate Method:
Allowance given to Manavi = ₹1260
Left over amount of Manavi = Allowance - Saving - Spend at mall
{tex}=1260-\frac{1}{2} \times 1260-\frac{1}{4} \times 1260{/tex}
{tex}=1260\left(1-\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260 \times\left(\frac{4-2-1}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260 \times\left(\frac{4-3}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260 \times \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
{tex}= ₹\; 315{/tex}
Hence, the amount left with Manavi is ₹315.
Allowance given to Kuber = ₹ 1260
Left over amount of Kuber = Allowance - Saving - Spend at mall
{tex}=1260-\frac{1}{3} \times 1260-\frac{3}{5} \times 1260{/tex}
{tex}=1260\left(1-\frac{1}{3}-\frac{3}{5}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260\left(\frac{15-5-9}{15}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260\left(\frac{15-14}{15}\right){/tex}
{tex}=1260 \times \frac{1}{15}{/tex}
{tex}= ₹84{/tex}
Hence, the amount left with Kuber is ₹84.

Q 7

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{4}+\frac{x}{5}{/tex} + 10000 = {tex}x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{x}{4}+\frac{x}{5}{/tex} - x = -10000
{tex}\frac{10 x+5 x+4 x-20 x}{20}{/tex} = -10000
{tex}\frac{19 x-20 x}{20}{/tex} = -10000
{tex}\frac{-x}{20}{/tex} = -10000
{tex}x{/tex} = 200000

Q 8

The present age of father is four times the age of his son. After 10 years, age of father will become three times the age of his son. Find their present ages.

Solution

Let the present age of son be x years
{tex}\therefore{/tex} the present age of father = 4x years
After 10 years,
Age of son = (x + 10) years
and age of father = (4x + 10) years
According to the given condition,
4x + 10 = 3(x + 10)
4x + 10 = 3x + 30
4x - 3x = 30 - 10
x = 20
Hence, the present age of son = 20 years
and present age of father = 4x
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 20
= 80 years

Q 9

A steamer goes downstream from one point to another in 7 hours. It covers the same distance upstream in 8 hours. If the speed of the stream is 2 km/hr, find the speed of the steamer in still water and the distance between the points.

Solution

Let speed of steamer in still water = x km/hr
Speed of stream = 2 km/hr
Speed downstream = (x + 2) km/hr
Speed upstream = (x – 2) km/hr
Distance covered in 7 hours while downstream = 7(x + 2)[{tex}\because{/tex}Distance = speed x time]
Distance covered in 8 hours while upstream = 8(x - 2)
According to the condition,
7(x + 2) = 8(x - 2)
7x + 14 = 8x - 16
7x - 8x = -16 -14
-x = -30
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}x = 30 km/hr
Total distance between two points = 7(x + 2) km
= 7(30 + 2) km
= 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 km
= 224 km

Q 10

Distance between two stations A and B is 690 km. Two cars start simultaneously from A and B towards each other, and the distance between them after 6 hours is 30 km. If the speed of one car is less than the other by 10 km/hr, find the speed of each car.

Solution

Let speed of faster car = x km/hr
then, speed of other = (x - 10) km/hr
Let faster one starts from A and other from B.
let, M and N be their positions after 6 hours.

so, distace AM = 6x, and distance BN = 6(x -10)
According to condition,
6x + 6(x -10) +30 = 690
6x + 6x - 60 + 30 = 690
12x = 690 + 30
12x = 720
x = 60 km/hr
Therefore, speed of one car = 60 km/hr
Speed of other car = (60 - 10) = 50 km/hr

Q 11

Application on problem solving strategy
A home-owner is installing a fence around the square garden. The garden has a perimeter of 6480 cm. Write and solve the equation to find the garden’s dimensions.

Solution

Understand and explore the problem:

  • What do you know?
    The perimeter of square garden = 6480 cm

To find: Side of the garden?
Plan a strategy:

  • To visualize that fencing around a garden means fencing its perimeter.
  • Recall that a square has four equal sides, say a each.

Solution:
Fence around square garden = Perimeter of square garden
a + a + a + a = 6480 cm
4a = 6480 cm
a = 1620 cm
Thus, side of garden = 1620 cm
Check:
Verify your answer by adopting some other plan.
e.g. Here in this problem instead of taking perimeter as the sum of its sides, use the formula
4a = 6480 cm
a = 1620 cm
Hence verified.
