Q 132

The diagonals of a rhombus are 8 cm and 15 cm. Find its side.

Solution

Given, AC = 15 cm, BD = 8cm
Since the diagonals of a rhombus bisect each other at 90°, therefore in the {tex}\triangle{/tex}AOB, we have

AB2 = OA2 + OB2
{tex}\Rightarrow AB^{2}=\left(\frac{15}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{8}{2}\right)^{2}{/tex} = (7.5)2 + (4)2 = 56.25 + 16
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB2 = 72.25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt{72.25}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = 8.5 cm
Since it is a rhombus, the length of each side is 8.5 cm.

Q 133

Two adjacent angles of a parallelogram are in the ratio 1 : 3. Find its angles.

Solution

Let the adjacent angles of a parallelogram be x and 3x
Then, we have x + (3x) = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} adjacent angles of parallelogram are supplementary]
Thus, the angles are 45°, 135°
Hence, the angles are 45°, 135, 45°, 135° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles in a parallelogram are equal]

Q 134

Of the four quadrilaterals - square, rectangle, rhombus and trapezium-one is somewhat different from the others because of its design. Find it and give justification.

Solution

In square, rectangle and rhombus, opposite sides are parallel and equal. Also, opposite angles are equal, i.e. they all are parallelograms.
But in trapezium, there is only one pair of parallel sides, i.e. it is not a parallelogram.
Therefore, trapezium has different design.

Q 135

In a rectangle ABCD, AB = 25 cm and BC = 15 cm. In what ratio, does the bisector of {tex}\angle{C}{/tex} divide AB?

Solution

It is given that AB = 25 cm and BC = 15 cm
Now, in rectangle ABCD

CO is the bisector of {tex}\angle{C}{/tex} and it divides AB.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OCB = {tex}\angle{/tex}OCD = 45°
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle O C B=\angle O C D=45^{\circ}{/tex}
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}OCB, we have
{tex}\angle \mathrm{CBO}+\angle \mathrm{OCB}+\angle \mathrm{COB}=180^{\circ}{/tex}  [angle sum property of triangle]
90°+ 45° + {tex}\angle \mathrm{COB}{/tex} = 180°
{tex}\angle \mathrm{COB}{/tex} = 180°- 90°+ 45°
{tex}\angle \mathrm{COB}{/tex} = 180°- 135° = 45°
Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}OCB,
{tex}\angle O C B=\angle C O B{/tex}
Then, OB = BC
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} OB = 15 cm
CO divides AB in the ratio AO : OB
Let AO be x, then
OB = AB - x = 25 - x
Hence, AO : OB = x : 25 - x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10 : 15 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 : 3
Hence, the required ratio = 2 : 3

Q 136

PQRS is a rectangle. The perpendicular ST from S on PR divides {tex}\angle{/tex}S in the ratio 2 : 3. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}TPQ.

Solution

Given ST is perpendicular on PR and ST divides {tex}\angle{/tex}S in the ratio 2 : 3
So, sum of ratio = 2 + 3 = 5

Now, {tex}\angle T S P=\frac{2}{5} \times 90^{\circ}=36^{\circ}, \angle T S R=\frac{3}{5} \times 90^{\circ}=54^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\angle T P S=180^{\circ}-(\angle S T P+\angle T S P){/tex} [by the angle sum property of a triangle]
= 180°-(90°+ 36°) =54°
We know that, {tex}\angle S P Q=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \angle T P S+\angle T P Q=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 54^{\circ}+\angle T P Q=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle T P Q=90^{\circ}-54^{\circ}=36^{\circ}{/tex}

Q 137

A photo frame is in the shape of a quadrilateral, with one diagonal longer than the other. Is it a rectangle? Why or why not?

Solution

No, it cannot be a rectangle, as in rectangle, both the diagonals are of equal lengths.

Q 138

The adjacent angles of a parallelogram are (2x - 4)° and (3x - 1)°. Find the measures of all angles of the parallelogram.

Solution

Since, the adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (2x - 4)° + (3x - 1)° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x - 5° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x = 185° 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{185^{\circ}}{5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x=37^{\circ}{/tex}
Thus, the adjacent angles are
x = 37°
2x - 4 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 37°- 4 = 74 - 4 = 70°
and 3x -1 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 37 °- 1 = 111 - 1 = 110°
Hence, the angles are 70°, 110°, 70°, 110°
[{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles in a parallelogram are equal]

Q 139

The point of intersection of diagonals of a quadrilateral divides one diagonal in the ratio 1 : 2. Can it be a parallelogram? Why or why not?

Solution

No, it can never be a parallelogram, as the diagonals of a parallelogram intersect each other in the ratio 1 : 1

Q 140

The ratio between exterior angle and interior angle of a regular polygon is 1 : 5. Find the number of sides of the polygon.

Solution

Let the exterior angle and interior angle be x and 5x, respectively.
Then, x + 5x = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} exterior angle and corresponding interior angle are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6x = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{180^{\circ}}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 30°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The number of sides = {tex}\frac{360^{\circ}}{\text { Exterior angle }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{360^{\circ}}{30^{\circ}}=12{/tex}

Q 141

Two sticks each of length 5 cm are crossing each other such that they bisect each other. What shape is formed by joining their endpoints? Give reason.

Solution

Sticks can be taken as the diagonals of a quadrilateral. Now, since they are bisecting each other, therefore the shape formed by joining their end points will be a parallelogram. Hence, it may be a rectangle or a square depending on the angle between the sticks.

Q 142

Two sticks each of length 7 cm are crossing each other such that they bisect each other at right angles. What shape is formed by joining their endpoints? Give reason.

Solution

Sticks can be treated as the diagonals of a quadrilateral.
Now, since the diagonals (sticks) are bisecting each other at right angles, therefore the shape formed by joining their end points will be a rhombus.

Q 143

A playground in the town is in the form of a kite. The perimeter is 106 m. If one of its sides is 23 m, what are the lengths of other three sides?

Solution

Let the length of other non-consecutive side be x cm.
Then, we have, perimeter of playground = 23 + 23 + x + x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 106 = 2 ( 23 + x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 46 + 2x = 106
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 106 - 46
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 60
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 30 m
Hence, the lengths of other three sides are 23m, 30m and 30m. As a kite has two pairs of equal consecutive sides.

Q 144

In rectangle READ, Find {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR, {tex}\angle{/tex}RAD and {tex}\angle{/tex}ROD

Solution

Given a rectangle READ, in which {tex}\angle{/tex}ROE = 60o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EOA = 180o – 60o = 120o [linear pair]
Now in triangle EOA, {tex}\angle{/tex}OEA = {tex}\angle{/tex}OAE = 30o [{tex}\because{/tex} OE = OA and equal sides make equal angles]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR = 30o, {tex}\angle{/tex}RAD = 90o - {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR = 60o and {tex}\angle{/tex}ROD = {tex}\angle{/tex}EOA = 120o

Q 145

In rectangle PAIR, find {tex}\angle{/tex}ARI, {tex}\angle{/tex}RMI and {tex}\angle{/tex}PMA.

Solution

Given, {tex}\angle R A I=35^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle P R A=35^{\circ}{/tex} [PR || A I and AR is transversal]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle A R I=90^{\circ}-\angle P R A{/tex} = 90° - 35° = 55
{tex}\because \quad A M=I M, \angle M I A=\angle M A I=35^{\circ}{/tex}
In {tex}\triangle A M I, \angle R M I=\angle M A I+\angle M I A=70^{\circ}{/tex} [exterior angle]
Also, {tex}\angle R M I=\angle P M A{/tex}  
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle P M A=70^{\circ}{/tex} [vertically opposite angles]

Q 146

In parallelogram ABCD, find {tex}\angle{/tex}B, {tex}\angle{/tex}C and {tex}\angle{/tex}D.

Solution

In a parallelogram, the opposite angles are equal, therefore {tex}\angle{/tex}C = {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 80°
Also, adjacent angles are supplementary.
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle A+\angle B=180^{\circ}{/tex}
80°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180°- 80° = {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 100°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 100°

Q 147

In parallelogram PQRS, O is the mid-point of SQ. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}S, {tex}\angle{/tex}R, PQ, QR, and diagonal PR.

Solution

Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}RQY = 60°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RQP = 120° [linear pair]
{tex}\angle{/tex}S = 120° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles are equal in a parallelogram]
By the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, {tex}\angle{/tex}P + {tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\angle{/tex}S + {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle P+\angle R+120^{\circ}+120^{\circ}=360^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \angle P+\angle R=120^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}P = 120°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 60° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles are equal in parallelogram]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}P = {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 60°
Also, SR = 15 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} PQ = 15 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal]
And PS = 11 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} QR = 11 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal]
and PR = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} PO = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 12 [{tex}\because{/tex} diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other]

Q 148

In rhombus BEAM, find {tex}\angle{/tex}AME and {tex}\angle{/tex}AEM.

Solution

Given, {tex}\angle{BAM}{/tex} = 70°
We know that, in rhombus, diagonals bisect each other at right angles.
{tex}\therefore \angle B O M=\angle B O E=\angle A O M=\angle A O E=90^{\circ}{/tex}
Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}AOM;
{tex}\angle \mathrm{AOM}+\angle \mathrm{AMO}+\angle \mathrm{OAM}=180^{\circ}{/tex} [angle sum property of triangle]
90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}AMO + 70°= 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AMO = 180°- 90° = 70°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AMO = 20°
Also, AM = BM = BE = EA
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}AME, we have,
AM = EA
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AME = {tex}\angle{/tex}AEM = 20° [{tex}\because{/tex} equal sides make equal angles]

Q 149

In parallelogram FIST, find {tex}\angle{/tex}SFT, {tex}\angle{/tex}OST and {tex}\angle{/tex}STO.

Solution


Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}FIS = 60°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}FTS = {tex}\angle{/tex}FIS = 60° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]
{tex}\angle{/tex}FTO = {tex}\angle{/tex}SIO = 25° [{tex}\because{/tex} alternate interior angles]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}STO = {tex}\angle{/tex}FTS - {tex}\angle{/tex}FTO = 60° - 25° = 35°
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}FOT + {tex}\angle{/tex}SOT = 180° [linear pair]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 110°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}SOT = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SOT = 180°-110° = 70°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}TOS, {tex}\angle{/tex}TSO + {tex}\angle{/tex}OTS + {tex}\angle{/tex}TOS =180° [by angle sum property of triangle]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}OST = 180°- (70° + 35°) = 75°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}FOT, {tex}\angle{/tex}FOT + {tex}\angle{/tex}FTO + {tex}\angle{/tex}OFT = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SFT = {tex}\angle{/tex}OFT = 180°- ({tex}\angle{/tex}FOT + {tex}\angle{/tex}FTO) = 180° - (110° + 25°) = 45°

Q 150

In the given parallelogram YOUR, {tex}\angle{/tex}RUO = 120o and OY is extended to point S, such that {tex}\angle{/tex}SRY = 50o. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}YSR.

Solution

Given, {tex}\angle{/tex}RUO = 120° and {tex}\angle{/tex}SRY = 50°
{tex}\angle{/tex}RYO = {tex}\angle{/tex}RUO = 120° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles of a parallelogram]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}SYR = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}RYO [linear pair]
= 180°-120°= 60°
In {tex}\triangle{/tex}SRY,
By the angle sum property of a triangle, {tex}\angle{/tex}SRY + {tex}\angle{/tex}RYS + {tex}\angle{/tex}YSR = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50°+ 60° + {tex}\angle{/tex}YSR = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}YSR = 180° - (50° + 60°) = 70°

Q 151

In kite EARW, {tex}\angle{/tex}WEA = 70° and {tex}\angle{/tex}ARW = 80°. Find the remaining two angles.

Solution

Given, in a kite EARW, {tex}\angle{/tex}WEA = 70°, {tex}\angle{/tex}ARW = 80°
{tex}\angle{/tex}RWE + {tex}\angle{/tex}WEA + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR + {tex}\angle{/tex}ARW = 360°[by the interior angle sum property of a quadrilateral]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RWE + 70° + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR + 80° = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RWE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR = 360° - 150°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RWE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EAR =210°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}RWA = {tex}\angle{/tex}RAW [{tex}\because{/tex} RW = RA]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}AWE = {tex}\angle{/tex}WAE [{tex}\because{/tex} WE = AE]
On adding Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\angle{/tex}RWA + {tex}\angle{/tex}AWE = {tex}\angle{/tex}RAW + {tex}\angle{/tex}WAE
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RWE = {tex}\angle{/tex}RAE
From Eq. (i),
2{tex}\angle{/tex}RWE = 210°
{tex}\angle{/tex}RWE = 105° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RWE = {tex}\angle{/tex}RAE = 105°

Q 152

A rectangle MORE is shown below.

Answer the following questions by giving an appropriate reason.

  1. Is RE = OM
  2. Is {tex}\angle{/tex}MYO = {tex}\angle{/tex}RXE?
  3. Is {tex}\angle{/tex}MOY = {tex}\angle{/tex}REX?
  4. Is {tex}\triangle{/tex}MYO {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}RXE?
  5. Is MY = RX?
Solution

A rectangle MORE is shown below:
 

  1. Yes, RE = OM
    Given, MORE is a rectangle. Therefore, the opposite sides are equal.
  2. Yes, {tex}\angle{/tex}MYO = {tex}\angle{/tex}RXE
    Here, MY and RX are perpendicular to OE.
    Since, {tex}\angle{/tex}RXO = 90° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}RXE = 90° and {tex}\angle{/tex}MYE = 90° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}MYO = 90°
  3. Yes, {tex}\angle{/tex}MOY = {tex}\angle{/tex}REX
    {tex}\because{/tex} RE || OM and EO is a transversal.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}MOE = {tex}\angle{/tex}OER [alternate interior angles]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}MOY = {tex}\angle{/tex}REX
  4. Yes, {tex}\Delta{/tex}MYO {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}RXE
    In {tex}\Delta{/tex}MYO and {tex}\Delta{/tex}RXE
    MO = RE [proved in (i)]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}MOY = {tex}\angle{/tex}REX [proved in (iii)]
    {tex}\angle{/tex}MYO = {tex}\angle{/tex}RXE [proved in (ii)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}MYO {tex}\cong{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}RXE [by AAS]
  5. Yes, MY = RX
    Since, these are corresponding parts of congruent triangles.
Q 153

In parallelogram LOST, SN{tex}\perp{/tex}OL and SM{tex}\perp{/tex}LT. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}STM, {tex}\angle{/tex}SON and {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM.

Solution

It is given that {tex}\angle{/tex}MST = 40°
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}MST,
{tex}\angle{/tex}TMS + {tex}\angle{/tex}MST + {tex}\angle{/tex}STM = 180° [By the angle sum property of a triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}STM = 180° - (90° + 40°) = 50° [{tex}\because{/tex} SM{tex}\perp{/tex} LT, {tex}\angle{/tex}TMS = 90°]
{tex}\angle{/tex}SON = {tex}\angle{/tex}STM = 50° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]
Now, in the {tex}\Delta{/tex}ONS,
{tex}\angle{/tex}ONS + {tex}\angle{/tex}OSN + {tex}\angle{/tex}SON - 180° [angle sum property of triangle]
{tex}\angle{/tex}OSN = 180° - (90° + 50°)
= 180° - 140° = 40°
Moreover, {tex}\angle{/tex}SON + {tex}\angle{/tex}TSO = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}SON + {tex}\angle{/tex}TSM + {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM + {tex}\angle{/tex}OSN = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50° + 40° + {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM + 40° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90°+ 40°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 130° + {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}NSM = 180° - 130° = 50°

Q 154

In trapezium HARE, EP and RP are bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}E and {tex}\angle{/tex}R, respectively. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}HAR and {tex}\angle{/tex}EHA.

Solution

It is given that {tex}\angle{/tex}PER = 25°
and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRE = 30°
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}PEH = 25°
and {tex}\angle{/tex}PRA = 30°[{tex}\because{/tex}EP and PR are angle bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}REH, and {tex}\angle{/tex}ARE respectively]
Since, HARE is a trapezium,
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}E + {tex}\angle{/tex}H = 180°[co-interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}PER + {tex}\angle{/tex}PEH + {tex}\angle{/tex}H = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 25° + 25° + {tex}\angle{/tex}H = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}H = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}H = 130°
Similarly, {tex}\angle{/tex}R + {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180°[co-interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}ERP + {tex}\angle{/tex}PRA + {tex}\angle{/tex}RAH = 180°
 30° + 30° + {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180°
60°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 120°
Therefore, {tex}\angle{/tex}EHA = 130°
and {tex}\angle{/tex}HAR = 120°

Q 155

In parallelogram MODE, the bisectors of {tex}\angle{/tex}M and {tex}\angle{/tex}O meet at Q. Find the measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}MQO.

Solution

Let MODE be a parallelogram and Q be the point of intersection of the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}M and {tex}\angle{/tex}O.

Since, MODE is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EMO + {tex}\angle{/tex}DOM = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} adjacent angles are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \angle E M O+\frac{1}{2} \angle D O M=90^{\circ}{/tex} [dividing both sides by 2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}QMO + {tex}\angle{/tex}QOM = 90° ...(i)
Now, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}MOQ,
{tex}\angle{/tex}QOM + {tex}\angle{/tex}QMO + {tex}\angle{/tex}MQO = 180° [angle sum property of triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}MQO = 180°  [from Eq. (i)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}MQO = 180° - 90°= 90°

Q 156

A playground is in the form of a rectangle ATEF. Two players are standing at the points F and B, where EF = EB. Find the values of x and y.

Solution

Given, a rectangle ATEF in which EF = EB. Then, {tex}\Delta{/tex}FEB is an isosceles triangle.
Therefore,
{tex}\angle{/tex}EFB + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBF + {tex}\angle{/tex}FEB = 180°[by the angle sum property of a triangle],
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EFB + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBF + 90° = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} in a rectangle, each angle is of 90°]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}EFB = 90° [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}EFB = {tex}\angle{/tex}EBF]
{tex}\angle{/tex}EFB = 45°
so, {tex}\angle{/tex}EBF = 45°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}x +  {tex}\angle{/tex}EBF = 180°[linear pair]
 {tex}\angle{/tex}x = 180° - 45° = 135° 
and {tex}\angle{/tex}EFB + {tex}\angle{/tex}y= 90° [{tex}\because{/tex} in a rectangle, each angle is of 90°]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}y = 90° - 45° = 45°

Q 157

In the following figure of a ship, ABDH and CEFG are two parallelograms. Find the value of x.

Solution

We have, two parallelograms ABDH and CEFG.
Now, in ABDH,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = {tex}\angle{/tex}AHD = 130° [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]
and {tex}\angle{/tex}GHD = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}AHD = 180° - 130° [linear pair]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 50° = {tex}\angle{/tex}GHO
Also, {tex}\angle{/tex}EFG + {tex}\angle{/tex}FGC = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 30° + {tex}\angle{/tex}FGC= 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}FGC = 180°- 30° = 150°
and {tex}\angle{/tex}HGC + {tex}\angle{/tex}FGC = 180° [linear pair]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}HGC = 180° - {tex}\angle{/tex}FGC = 180° - 150° = 30° = {tex}\angle{/tex}HGO
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}HGO, by using angle sum property, {tex}\angle{/tex}OHG +{tex}\angle{/tex}HGO + {tex}\angle{/tex}HOG = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}50° + 30° + x = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 180° - 80 ° =100°

Q 158

A rangoli has been drawn on the floor of a house. ABCD and PQRS both are in the shape of a rhombus. Find the radius of semi-circle drawn on each side of rhombus ABCD.

Solution

In rhombus ABCD,
AO = OP + PA = 2 + 2 = 4 units and OB = OQ + QB = 2 + 1 = 3 units
We knew that, diagonals of rhombus bisect each other at 90°.
Now,
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}OAB, (AB)2 = (OA)2 + (OB)2 [by Pythagoras theorem]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (AB)2 = (4)2 + (3)2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = {tex}\sqrt{25}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = 5 units'
Since, AS is diameter of semi-circle.
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { radius }=\frac{\text { Diameter }}{2}=\frac{A B}{2}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} = 2.5 units
Hence, radius of the semi-circle is 2.5 units.

Q 159

ABCDE is a regular pentagon. The bisector of angle A meets the sides CD at M. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC.

Solution

Given, a pentagon ABCDE. The line segment AM is the bisector of the {tex}\angle{/tex}A.
Now, since the measure of each interior angle of a regular pentagon is 108°.
{tex}\therefore \quad \angle B A M=\frac{1}{2} \times 108^{\circ}=54^{\circ}{/tex}
By the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, we have (in quadrilateral ABCM)
{tex}\angle{/tex}BAM +{tex}\angle{/tex}ABC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCM + {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC = 360°
= 54° + 108° + 108° + {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC = 360° - 270° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AMC = 90°

Q 160

Quadrilateral EFGH is a rectangle in which J is the point of intersection of the diagonals. Find the value of x, if JF = 8x + 4 and EG = 24x - 8.

Solution

Given, EFGH is a rectangle in which diagonals are intersecting at the point J.

We know that, the diagonals of a rectangle bisect each other and are equal.
Then, EG = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} JF
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 24x - 8 = 2(8x +4)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 24x - 8 = 16x + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 24x -16x = 8 + 8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2

Q 161

Find the values of x and y in the following parallelogram.

Solution

In a parallelogram, adjacent angles are supplementary.
120°+ (5x + 10)° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x + 10° + 120° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x = 180° - 130°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5x = 50°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x= 10°
Also, opposite angles are equal in a parallelogram.
Therefore, 6y = 120° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 20°

Q 162

Find the values of x and y in the following kite.

Solution

The given figure is a kite
In a kite, one pair of opposite angles are equal
∴ y = 110°
Now, by the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, we have
110° + 60° + 110° + x = 360°
⇒ x = 360° – 280°
⇒ x = 80°

Q 163

Find the value of x in the trapezium ABCD given below.

Solution

Given, a trapezium ABCD in which {tex}\angle{/tex}A = (x - 20)°, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = (x + 40)°
Since, in a trapezium, the angles on either side of the base are supplementary, therefore
(x - 20) + (x+ 40) = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x - 20 + x + 40 = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 20° = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = (180° - 20°) = 160° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 80°

Q 164

Two angles of a quadrilateral are each of measure 75° and the other two angles are equal. What is the measure of these two angles? Name the possible figures so formed.

Solution

Let ABCD be a quadrilateral,
where {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 75° and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = x [say]

Then, by the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}C + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 75° + x + 75° + x = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 360°-150°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x=210°{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 105°
Thus, other two angles are of 105° each.
Since, opposite angles are equal, therefore the quadrilateral is a parallelogram.

Q 165

In a quadrilateral PQRS, {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 50o, {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = 50o, {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 60o. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}S. Is this quadrilateral convex or concave?

Solution

Given a quadrilateral PQRS, where
{tex}\angle{/tex}P = 50o, {tex}\angle{/tex}Q = 50o, {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 60o
Now, by the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, we have
{tex}\angle{/tex}P +{tex}\angle{/tex}Q + {tex}\angle{/tex}R+ {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 360o
⇒ 50o +  50o + 60o + {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 360o
⇒ 160o + {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 360o
⇒ {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 360o –  160o
⇒ {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 200o
Since one interior angle of the given quadrilateral is obtuse, therefore the quadrilateral is concave.

Q 166

Both the pairs of opposite angles of a quadrilateral are equal and supplementary. Find the measure of each angle.

Solution

Let ABCD be a quadrilateral, such that
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D and also {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°, {tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 180°
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}A= 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\angle{/tex}A = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 90°
Similarly, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 90°
Hence, each angle is a right angle.

Q 167

Find the measure of each angle of a regular octagon.

Solution

Number of sides(n) in octagon = 8
Now, the sum of interior angles of a regular octagon = (n - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180°
= (8 - 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 180°
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 180° = 1080°
Since, the octagon is regular, measure of each angle = {tex}\frac{1080^{\circ}}{8}{/tex}= 135°

Q 168

Find the measure of an exterior angle of a regular pentagon and an exterior angle of a regular decagon. What is the ratio between these two angles?

Solution

We know that, number of sides in pentagon is 5 and in decagon is 10.
Now, exterior angle of a regular pentagon = 360o/5 = 72o
Exterior angle of a regular decagon = 360o/10 = 36o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required ratio = 72/36 = 2: 1
So, the ratio between these two angles is 2:1.

Q 169

In the figure, find the value of x.

Solution

Now, we know that, sum of all the exterior angles of a polygon is 360°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 92° + 20° + 85° + x° + 89° = 360°
286° + x° = 360°
x° = 360° - 286°
= 74°

Q 170

Three angles of a quadrilateral are equal. Fourth angle is of measure 120°. What is the measure of equal angles?

Solution

Let the measures of equal angles be x° each.
Then, by the angle sum property of a quadrilateral, we have
x°+ x° + x°+ 120°= 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x° + 120° = 360°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x° = 240°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x° = 80°

Q 171

ABCD is a parallelogram. Find the values of x, y, and z.

Solution

Given, a parallelogram ABCD.
In the {tex}\triangle{/tex}OBC, we have
y + 30° = 100° [exterior angle property of triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 70°
By the angle sum property of a triangle,
we have, x + y + 30 = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x + 70°+30° = 180° {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 180° - 100° = 80°
Now, since AD||BC and BD is transversal, therefore
{tex}\angle{/tex}ADO = {tex}\angle{/tex}OBC [alternate interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 30°

Q 172

Diagonals of a quadrilateral are perpendicular to each other. Is such a quadrilateral always a rhombus? Give a figure to justify your answer.

Solution

No, if the diagonals of the quadrilateral are perpendicular to each other then such quadrilateral is not always a rhombus. It may be a square or kite. As we know conditions of rhombus:

  1. it should be a parallelogram.
  2. all sides should be equal.
  3. diagonals are perpendicular to each other.

but here given only "diagonals of the quadrilateral are perpendicular to each other."
hence, we can't say given quadrilateral is always rhombus.

Q 173

ABCD is a trapezium such that AB ∥ CD, {tex}\angle{/tex}A: {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 2: 1, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 7: 5. Find the angles of the trapezium.

Solution

Let ABCD be a trapezium, where AB || CD.

Let the angles A and D be of measures 2x and x, respectively
then 2x + x = 180o
[{tex}\because{/tex} in trapezium, the angles on either side of the base are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 60o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 2x = 60o = 120o, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 60o
Again, let the angles B and C be 7x and 5x respectively. Then 7x + 5x = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x = 180o
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15o
Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 105o and {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 75o

Q 174

A line l is parallel to line m and a transversal p intersects them at X, Y respectively. Bisectors of interior angles at X and Y intersect at P and Q. Is PXQY a rectangle? Give reason.

Solution

It is given that l || m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DXY = {tex}\angle{/tex}XYA [alternate interior angles]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\angle D X Y}{2}=\frac{\angle X Y A}{2}{/tex} [dividing both the sides by 2]

Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 [{tex}\because{/tex} XP and YQ are bisectors]
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 [{tex}\because{/tex}Alternate angles are equal]
XP||QY ..(i)
Similarly, XQ||PY ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
PXQY is a parallelogram ...(iii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DXY + {tex}\angle{/tex}XYB = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} interior angles on the same side of transversal are supplementary]
{tex}\frac{\angle D X Y}{2}+\frac{\angle X Y B}{2}=\frac{180^{\circ}}{2}{/tex} [dividing both the sides by 2]
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 + {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = 90° ...(iv)
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}XYP,
{tex}\angle{/tex}1+ {tex}\angle{/tex}3 + {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 180°
90° + {tex}\angle{/tex}P = 180° [from Eq. (iv)]
{tex}\angle{/tex}P = 90° ...(v)
From Eqs. (iii) and (v), PXQY is a rectangle.

Q 175

ABCD is a parallelogram. The bisector of angle A intersects CD at X and bisector of angle C intersects AB at Y. Is AXCY a parallelogram? Give reason.

Solution

Given, ABCD is a parallelogram.
So, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles of a parallelogram are equal]

{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\angle A}{2}=\frac{\angle C}{2}{/tex} [dividing both the sides by 2]
{tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2 [alternate angles]
But {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 [{tex}\because{/tex} AB || CD and CY is the transversal]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3
But they are pair of corresponding angles.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AX || YC ....(i)
AY || XC [{tex}\because{/tex} AB || DC] ...(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
AXCY s a parallelogram.

Q 176

A diagonal of a parallelogram bisects an angle. Will it also bisect the other angle? Give reason.

Solution

Consider a parallelogram ABCD

Given: {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
Since, ABCD is a parallelogram.
AB || CD and AC is the transversal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 [alternate interior angles] ... (i)
Similarly,  {tex}\angle{/tex}2 = {tex}\angle{/tex}3 [alternate interior angles]... (ii)
But given, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 [from Eqs. (i) and (ii)]
Hence proved.

Q 177

The angle between the two altitudes of a parallelogram through the vertex of an obtuse angle of the parallelogram is 45°. Find the angles of the parallelogram.

Solution

Let ABCD be a parallelogram, where BE and BE are the perpendiculars through the vertex B to the sides DC and AD, respectively.

Let {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = x and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D = y [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles are equal in parallelogram]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} adjacent angles of a parallelogram are supplementary]
 x + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABF + {tex}\angle{/tex}FBE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBC = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}B =  {tex}\angle{/tex}ABF + {tex}\angle{/tex}FBE + {tex}\angle{/tex}EBC ]
x + 90° - x + 45° + 90° - x = 180° [{tex}\because{/tex} In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABF, {tex}\angle{/tex}ABF = 90°-x and in {tex}\Delta{/tex}BBC, {tex}\angle{/tex}EBC = 90°- x]
 - x = 180° -225°
 x = 45°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45°
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = 45° + 45°+ 45° = 135°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 135°
Hence, the angles are 45°, 135°, 45°, 135°.

Q 178

ABCD is a rhombus such that the perpendicular bisector of AB passes through D. Find the angles of the rhombus.
Hint: Join BD. Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABD is equilateral.

Solution

It is given that ABCD is a rhombus in which DE is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

Join BD. Then, in {tex}\Delta{/tex}AED and {tex}\Delta{/tex}BED, we have
AE = EB [given]
ED = ED [common side]
{tex}\angle{/tex}AED = {tex}\angle{/tex}DEB [each = 90°]
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\Delta{/tex}AED ≅ {tex}\Delta{/tex}BED [ {tex}\because{/tex} SAS congruence]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AD = DB = AB [{tex}\because{/tex} ABCD is a rhombus, So, AD = AB]
Thus, {tex}\Delta{/tex}ADB is an equilateral triangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB = {tex}\angle{/tex}DBA = {tex}\angle{/tex}ADB = 60°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DCB = 60° [opposite angles of a rhombus are equal]
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}DAB + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 180° [adjacent angles of a rhombus are supplementary]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60° + {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60°+ 60°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 60°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD + {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 60° + 60° = 120°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ADC = 120° [opposite angles of a rhombus are equal]
Hence, the angles of the rhombus arc 60°, 120°, 60°, 120°

Q 179

ABCD is a parallelogram. Point P and Q are taken on the sides AB and AD, respectively and the parallelogram PRQA is formed. If {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45°, Find {tex}\angle{/tex}R.

Solution

Let ABCD be a parallelogram,
where  {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45°


Since ABCD is a parallelogram,
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C...(i)[opposite angles of parallelogram are equal]
Again, since PRQA is a parallelogram,
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}R...(ii)[opposite angles of parallelogram are equal]
from eqn (i) and eqn (ii), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 45°[{tex}\because{/tex}{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 45°]

Q 180

In parallelogram ABCD, the angle bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A bisects BC. Will angle bisector of B also bisect AD? Give reason.

Solution

Given, ABCD is a parallelogram, bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}A, bisects BC at E, i.e, {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2,CF = FB. Draw EE II BA.
therefore, ABFE is a parallelogram by construction [{tex}\because{/tex} FE || BA]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}6 [alternate angle]

But {tex}\angle{/tex}1 = {tex}\angle{/tex}2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}2 - {tex}\angle{/tex}6 [given]
AB = FB [opposite sides to equal angles are equal] ...(i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ABFE is a rhombus.
Now, in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABO and {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOF 
AB = BF  [from Eq. (i)]
BO = BO [common]
AO = FO [diagonals of rhombus bisect each other]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABO ≅ {tex}\triangle{/tex}BOF [by SSS congruence)]
{tex}\angle{/tex}3 = {tex}\angle{/tex}4 [by CPCT]
Now, {tex}B F=\frac{1}{2} B C{/tex} [given]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \quad B F=\frac{1}{2} A D{/tex} [BC = AD]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A E=\frac{1}{2} A D{/tex} [BF = AE]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E is the mid-point of AD.

Q 181

A regular pentagon ABCDE and a square ABFG are formed on opposite sides of AB. Find {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF?

Solution

Given, is a regular pentagon.

Then, the measure of each interior angle of the regular pentagon
= {tex}\frac{\text { Sum of interior angles }}{\text { Number of sides }}=\frac{(x-2) \times 180^{\circ}}{5}{/tex} [Where x is number of sides]
= {tex}\frac{(5-2) \times 180^{\circ}}{5}=\frac{540^{\circ}}{5}=108^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}CBA = 108°
Join CF.
Now, {tex}\angle{/tex}FBC = 360° - (90° + 108°) = 360° - 198° = 162°
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}FBC
{tex}\angle{/tex}FBC + {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF + {tex}\angle{/tex}BFC = 180° [by the angle sum property] 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF + {tex}\angle{/tex}BFC = 180° - 162°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF + {tex}\angle{/tex}BFC = 18°
Since, {tex}\Delta{/tex}FBC is an isosceles triangle and BF = BC
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}BCF = {tex}\angle{/tex}BFC = 9°

Q 182

Find the maximum number of acute angles which a convex, a quadrilateral, a pentagon and a hexagon can have. Observe the pattern and generalise the result for any polygon.

Solution

We know that if the interior angle is acute, then the corresponding exterior angle is greater than 90o.
Now, suppose a convex polygon has four or more acute angles. Since the polygon is convex, all the exterior angles are positive, so the sum of the exterior angle is at least the sum of the interior angles.
Now, supplementary of the four acute angles, which is greater than 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 90o = 360o
However, it is impossible.
Since the sum of the exterior angle of a polygon must equal to 360o and cannot be greater than it.
It follows that the maximum number of an acute angle in a convex polygon is 3.

Q 183

In the following figure, FD || BC || AE and AC || ED. Find the value of x.

Solution

Produce DF such that it intersects AB at G.

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC,
{tex}\angle{/tex}A+ {tex}\angle{/tex}B+ {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° [angle sum property of triangle]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 52° + 64° + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° - (52° + 64°) = 180° - 116° = 64°
Now, we see that, DG II BC and DG II AE
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}ACB = {tex}\angle{/tex}AFG
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 64° = {tex}\angle{/tex}AFG
Also, GFD is a straight line.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}GFA + {tex}\angle{/tex}AFD = 180° [linear pair]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 64° + {tex}\angle{/tex}AFD- 180°
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AFD = 180° - 64° = 116°
Also, FD||AE and AF||ED
So, AEDF is a parallelogram.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AFD = {tex}\angle{/tex}AED [{tex}\because{/tex} opposite angles in a parallelogram are equal]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}AED = x = 116°

Q 184

In the following figure, AB || DC and AD = BC. Find the value of x.

Solution

Given, an isosceles trapezium, where AB || DC and AD = BC and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 60o.
Then, {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 60o.
Draw a line parallel to BC through D which intersects the line AB at E. 
Then, DEBC is a parallelogram, where
BE = CD = 20 cm and DE = BC = 10 cm
now {tex}\angle{/tex}DEB + {tex}\angle{/tex}CBE = 180o
[adjacent angles are supplementary in parallelogram]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\angle{/tex}DEB =180o – 60o = 120o
{tex}\therefore{/tex} in {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADE, {tex}\angle{/tex}ADE = 60o  [exterior angle]
Also {tex}\angle{/tex}DEA = 60o [{tex}\because{/tex} AD = DE = 10 cm and {tex}\angle{/tex}DAE =  60o]
Then {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADE is an equilateral triangle.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} AE = 10 cm
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} AB = AE + EB = 10 + 20 = 30 cm
Hence x = 30 cm

Q 185

Find the measure of each angle of a regular polygon of 20 sides?

sections.label: Examples
sections.html:
Q 1

Construct a quadrilateral ABCD in which AB = AD = 5cm, BC = CD = 7cm and BD = 6cm. What type of quadrilateral is this?

Solution

Looking at the rough figure, draw a line segment BD = 6cm. Taking B and D as centres and 5 cm radius, draw arcs to intersect at the point A, then taking B and D as centres and 7 cm radius, draw arcs in the opposite side of A to intersect at the point C. Join AB, AD and BC, DC. Then ABCD is the required quadrilateral. It is a kite.

Q 2

Application on the problem-solution strategy:
Construct a rhombus with side 4.5cm and diagonal 6cm.

Solution

Understand and explore the problem What do you know?
Here, side of rhombus = 4.5 cm.
Diagonal of rhombus = 6 cm.
What do we need to make rhombus?
4 sides and its one diagonal
Plan a strategy

  1. Use the property of rhombus - all sides are equal.
  2. Make a free hand rough sketch and name it ABCD.


Solve:
Step-1. Draw AB = 4.5 cm.

Step-2. With A as centre and radius 6 cm draw an arc above AB.

Step-3. With B as centre draw an arc to cut the arc drawn in step 2 at pt C.
Step-4. Join AC and BC.

Step-5. With A and C as centre and radius 4.5 cm draw arcs to intersect each other at D.

Step-6. ABCD is required rhombus.
Checking:
Verify your figure by adopting some other property of rhombus.
Step 1. Join BD to intersect AC at O.
Step 2. Measure {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB. Is it 90o?
Step 3. Measure OA and OC. Are they equal?
Step 4. Measure OB and OD. Are they equal?
If your answer to 2, 3, 4 is yes it means what you have constructed is a right angle.

sections.label: Chapter-end
sections.html:
Q 1

Construct a trapezium ABCD in which AB || DC, {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 105°, AD = 3 cm, AB = 4 cm and CD = 8 cm.

Solution

We know that.
{tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}D =180 [{tex}\because{/tex} sum of adjacent angle of a trapezium is 180°]
105°+ {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 180°
{tex}\angle{/tex}D = 75°

Steps of Construction
Step I: Draw AB = 4 cm.
Step II: Draw {tex}\overline{A X}{/tex} such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAX= 105°.
Step III: Mark a point D on AX such that AD = 3cm.
Step IV: Draw {tex}\overline{D Y}{/tex} such that {tex}\angle{/tex}ADY = 75°.
Step V: Mark a point C such that CD = 8cm.
Step VI: Join BC.
Hence, ABCD is the required trapezium.

Q 2

Construct a parallelogram ABCD in which AB = 4 cm, BC = 5 cm and ∠B = 60°.

Solution
  1. Draw AB = 4 cm
  2. Draw ray BX such that ∠ABX = 60°
  3. Mark a point C such that BC = 5cm
  4. With C and A as centre, draw arcs of 4 cm & 5 cm respectively intersecting at a point D respectively
  5. ABCD is the required parallelogram.
Q 3

Construct a rhombus whose side is 5 cm and one angle is of 60o.

Solution
  1. {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 60o (suppose) then {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 120o (sum of co-interior angles is 180o)
  2. Draw AB = 5cm
  3. Draw ray AY such that {tex}\angle{/tex}BAY = 120o
  4. Mark a point D such that AD = 5cm
  5. Draw ray BX such that {tex}\angle{/tex}ABX = 60o
  6. Mark a point C such that BC = 5cm
  7. Joint C and D. ABCD is the required rhombus
Q 4

Construct a rectangle whose one side is 3 cm and a diagonal equal to 5 cm.

Solution
  1. Diagonals of a rectangle are equal. So, AC = BD = 5 cm
  2. Draw AB = 3 cm
  3. Draw a ray BC such that ∠ABC = 90°
  4. Draw an are such that AC = 5 cm
  5. With B as centre, draw an arc of radius 5 cm. With C as centre draw another arc of radius 3 cm which intersect first arc at a point, suppose D.
  6. Join CD and AD
  7. ABCD is the required rectangle.
Q 5

Construct a square of side 4 cm.

Solution
  1. Draw AB = 4 cm
  2. Make two angles of 90° at points A & B.
  3. Mark two points C & D on these angles such that BC = AD = 4 cm.
  4. Join DC. ABCD is the required square.
Q 6

Construct a rhombus CLUE in which CL = 7.5 cm and LE = 6 cm.

Solution
  1. All sides of rhombus are equal, So CL = LU = UE = EC = 7.5 cm and diagonal LE = 6 cm.
  2. Draw a line segment LC = 7.5 cm.
  3. With L & C as centres draw arcs of radii 6 cm & 7.5 cm respectively which intersect at E.
  4. Now with L & E as centres draw arcs of radii 7.5 cm each which intersect at U.
  5. Join CE, EU & UL. Hence CLUE is the required rhombus.
Q 7

Construct a quadrilateral BEAR in which BE = 6 cm, EA = 7 cm, RB = RE = 5 cm and BA = 9 cm. Measure the fourth side.

Solution

Rough figure:

Steps of construction:
Step 1. Draw line segment RE = 5cm.
Step 2. With R as centre and radius 5cm, draw an arc below and mark as B
Step 3. With radius as 6cm and centre as E draw an arc intersecting the previous arc at B satisfying SSS congruence. Join RB and EB.
Step 4. With E as centre and radius 7cm, draw an arc above and mark as A
Step 5. With radius as 9cm and centre as B draw an arc intersecting the previous arc at A. Join EA and AB.
Step 6. Join AR and measure.
It measures 4cm.
Required figure:

Q 8

Construct a parallelogram POUR in which PO = 5.5 cm, OU = 7.2 cm and ∠O = 70⁰.

Solution

Rough figure:

Steps of construction:
Step 1. Draw line segment OU = 7.2cm.
Step 2. With O as centre and radius 5.5cm, draw an arc above and mark as P
Step 3. On U construct angle XUO=70⁰
Step 4. As opposite sides of parallelogram are same draw an arc of radius 5.5cm on angle XUO.
Step 5. With radius as 7.2cm and centre as R draw an arc intersecting the previous arc at P.
Hence, we get the required quadrilateral.
Required figure:

Q 9

Draw a circle of radius 3 cm and draw its diameter and label it as AC. Construct its perpendicular bisector and let it intersect the circle at B and D. What type of quadrilateral is ABCD? Justify your answer.

Solution


Steps of Construction
Step I Taking centre OC = 3 cm, draw a circle.
Step II Join A to C and draw a perpendicular bisector of AC that cuts the circumference of circle at B and D.
Step IlI Join B and D.
Step IV Thus, ABCD is a cyclic quadrilateral.
Justification
In cyclic quadrilateral,
{tex}\angle{/tex}B = {tex}\angle{/tex}D= 90° [angle in a semi-circle]
{tex}\angle{/tex}A = {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 90°
{tex}\angle{/tex}B + {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 180°
and {tex}\angle{/tex}A + {tex}\angle{/tex}C = 180° [opposite angles are supplementary]
Since, opposite angles are supplementary, thus quadrilateral is a cyclic quadrilateral.

Q 10

Construct a parallelogram HOME with HO = 6 cm, HE = 4 cm and OE = 3 cm.

Solution
  1. Opposite sides of a parallelogram are equal so, HO = EM = 6 cm and HE = OE = 4 cm
  2. Draw a line segment HO = 6cm.
  3. With H & O as centres draw two arcs of radii 4 cm & 3 cm respectively which meet at E.
  4. Now with, E & O as centres draw two arcs of radii 6 cm & 4 cm respectively which at M.
  5. Join HE, EM & MO. Hence HOME is the required parallelogram.
Q 11

Is it possible to construct a quadrilateral ABCD in which AB = 3 cm, BC = 4 cm, CD = 5.4 cm, DA = 5.9 cm and diagonal AC = 8 cm? If not, why?

Solution

No,
Given measures are AB = 3 cm,BC = 4 cm,CD = 5.4cm,
DA = 5.9 cm and AC = 8 cm
Here, we observe that AB + BC = 3 + 4 = 7 cm and AC = 8 cm
i.e. the sum of two sides of a triangle is less than the third side, which is absurd.
Hence, we cannot construct such a quadrilateral.

Q 12

Is it possible to construct a quadrilateral ROAM in which RO = 4cm, OA = 5cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}O = 120o, {tex}\angle{/tex}R = 105o and {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 135o? If not, why?

Solution

The total of angles = 120o + 105o + 135o = 360o
According to the angle sum property of quadrilateral
Sum of four angles = 360o
Hence, it cannot be constructed because it already fulfills the angle sum property of quadrilateral.

Q 13

Construct a square in which each diagonal is 5 cm long.

Solution
  1. Diagonals of a square bisect each other at 90°
  2. Draw a line segment AC = 5 cm.
  3. Draw the perpendicular bisector of AC which gives O as mid-point of AC.
  4. With O as centre cut two arcs of 2.5 cm each which intersect the perpendicular bisector at points B & D.
  5. Join AB, BC, CD & DA. Hence ABCD is required square.
Q 14

Construct a quadrilateral NEWS in which NE = 7cm, EW = 6cm, {tex}\angle{/tex}N = 60o, {tex}\angle{/tex}E = 110o and {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 85o.

Solution

Rough figure:

Steps of construction:
Step 1. Draw line segment NE = 7cm.
Step 2. Taking N as centre construct an angle of 60o namely YNE=60o.
Step 3. Taking E as centre draw an angle of 110o namely XEN.
Step 4. With E as centre and radius 6cm, draw an arc on the angle XEN.
Step 5. Using angle sum property, find the angle measure of W i.e 105o.
Step 6. Construct angle W as 105o i.e ZWE.
Step 7. Angles YNE and ZWE would intersect at a point. Name it as point S.
Hence, we get the required figure i.e NEWS.
Required figure:

Q 15

Construct a parallelogram when one of its side is 4 cm and its two diagonals are 5.6 and 7 cm respectively. Measure the other side.

Solution

Rough figure:

Steps of construction:
Step 1. Draw line segment AC = 7cm.
Step 2. Since diagonals of parallelogram bisect each other, taking mid point of AC , draw an arc for 2.8cm upward as D and downward as B.
Step 3. With A as centre and radius 4cm, draw an arc intersecting the previous arc at B
Step 4. Similarly, taking centre as C draw an arc intersecting the above arc at D.
Step 5. Join AD and BC.
We get the required quadrilateral.
Required Figure:

Q 16

Construct a trapezium RISK in which RI || KS, RI = 7 cm, IS = 5 cm, RK = 6.5 cm and {tex}\angle{/tex}I = 60o.

Solution


{tex}\angle{/tex}I + {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 180°
60° + {tex}\angle{/tex}S = 180° [cointerior angles]
{tex}\angle{/tex}S =120°
Steps of Construction
StepI: Draw an arc RI = 7 cm.
Step II: Make {tex}\angle{/tex}RIX = 60°.
Step III: With I as centre and radius 5 cm, draw an arc cutting IX atS.
Step IV: Make {tex}\angle{/tex}ISY = 120°
Step V: With R as centre and radius 6.5 cm, draw an arc cutting SY at K.
Step VI: Join KR.
Thus, RISK is the required trapezium.

Q 17

Construct a trapezium ABCD, where AB || CD, AD = BC = 3.2 cm, AB = 6.4 cm and CD = 9.6cm. Measure {tex}\angle{/tex}B and {tex}\angle{/tex}A..

[Hint: Difference of two parallel sides gives an equilateral triangle.]

Solution


Steps of Construction
StepI: Draw a line segment DC = 9.6 cm.
StepII: With D as center, draw an angle measure 60°. Now, cut-off it with an arc 3.2 cm called point A.
StepIII: Now, draw a parallel line AB to CD.
StepIV: Talking C as centre, cut an arc B measure 3.2 cm on the previous parallel line.
StepV: Draw a line segment BE = 3.2 cm from arc B.
StepVI: Join B to E and C.
Thus, we have required trapezium ABCD in which {tex}\angle{/tex}A = 120° and {tex}\angle{/tex}B = 60°

A pie chart is also called a pictograph.

Q 14

The adjoining pie chart gives the marks scored in an examination by a student in Hindi, English, Mathematics, Social Science and Science. If the total marks obtained by the students were 540, answer the question.

In which subject did the students score 105 marks? (Hint: for 540 marks, the central angle = 360°. So, for 105 marks, what is the central angle?)

Solution

From the graph it is clear that  For 540 marks, the central angle = 360°
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For 105 marks, the central angle {tex} = \frac{{360^\circ }}{{540 }} \times 105 = 70^\circ {/tex}, hence, the student scored 105 marks in Hindi.

Q 15

The adjoining pie chart gives the marks scored in an examination by a student in Hindi, English, Mathematics, Social Science and Science. If the total marks obtained by the students were 540, answer the question.

How many more marks were obtained by the student in Mathematics than in Hindi?

Solution

We have,
Marks obtained by the students in Mathematics
{tex} = \frac{{90^\circ }}{{360^\circ }} \times 540 {/tex}
{tex} = 135{/tex}
Marks obtained by the students in Hindi {tex} = \frac{{70^\circ }}{{360^\circ }} \times 540 {/tex}
= 105
Therefore, the student obtained 135 – 105 = 30 marks more in Mathematics than in Hindi.

  2. How many workers earn ₹ 850 and more?
  3. How many workers earn less than ₹ 850?
  4. How many workers earn ₹ 820 or more but less than ₹ 880?
Solution

  1. Group 830–840, has the maximum number of workers.
  2. 1 + 3 + 1 + 1 + 3= 9, workers earn Rs. 850 and more.
  3. 3 + 2 + 1 +10+4 = 20, workers earn less than Rs. 850.

  4. What is the upper limit of the fifth class?
  5. What is the lower limit of the last class? 
Solution

  2. 75 – 85

Q 19

Given below is a pie chart depicting the reason given by people who had injured their lower back. Study the pie chart and find the number of people who injured their back while either bending and lifting. A total of 600 people were surveyed.

  1. Lifting
  2. Twisting
  3. Bending
  4. Pulling
  5. Others
Solution

To find
The total number of people can be found by finding (18% + 49%) = 67% of 600.
Solve
Total percentage of people who injured their back while bending and lifting = 18% + 49% = 67%.
Number of people who injured their back while bending and lifting = 67% of 600
{tex}=\frac{67}{100} \times 600{/tex}
= 402
Hence, 402 people injured their back while bending and lifting.
Revise
You can check the answer by finding the total number of people who injured their backs for reasons other than bending and lifting and then subtracting the answer from the total number of people. Total percentage of people who injured their backs for reasons other than bending and lifting = 12% + 12% + 9% = 33%
Number of such people = 33% of 600 = {tex}\frac{33}{100} \times 600{/tex} 
= 198
So, 600 –198 should give us the answer to our original question, and 600 - 198 = 402 which is the same as our answer. Hence our answer is correct.

Q 84

Given below is a pie chart showing the time spend by a group of 350 children in different games. Observe it and answer the questions that follow.

  1. How many children spend atleast one hour in playing games?
  2. How many children spend more than 2 h in playing games?
  3. How many children spend 3 or lesser hours in playing games?
  4. Which is greater, number of children who spend 2 hours or more per day or number of children who play for less than one hour?
Solution
  1. Number of children who spend atleast 1 h in playing games i.e. the number of children playing 1 h or more than 1 h
    = (Total number of children) - (Number of children spend less than 1 h)
    = 350 -6 % of 350
    {tex}=350-\frac{6}{100} \times 350{/tex}
    = 350-21 = 329
  2. Number of children who spend more than 2 h in playing games
    = (34 + 10 + 4)% of the total number of students
    = 48% of 350
    {tex}=\frac{48}{100} \times 350=168{/tex}
  3. Number of children who spend 3 or lesser hours in playing games
    = (34 + 30 + 16 + 6)%of total number of students
    = 86% of 350
    {tex}=\frac{86}{100} \times 350=301{/tex}
  4. Number of children who spend 2 h or more per day in playing games
    = (30 + 34 + 10 + 4)%of total number of students
    = 78% of total number of students
    Number of children who spend less than one hour = 6% of total number of students Clearly, number of children who play for 2 h or more per day is greater than the number of children who play for less than 1 h.
Q 85

The pie chart given below shows the result of a survey carried out to find the modes of travel used by the children to go to school. Study the pie chart and answer the questions that follow.

  1. What is the most common mode of transport?
  2. What fraction of children travel by car?
  3. If 18 children travel by car, how many children took part in the survey?
  4. How many children use taxi to travel to school?
  5. By which two modes of transport are an equal number of children travelling?
Solution
  1. The central angle is maximum for bus, hence bus is the most common mode of transport.
  2. The fraction of children traveled by car = {tex}\frac{\text { Central angle }}{360^{\circ}}=\frac{90^{\circ}}{360^{\circ}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
  3. We know that a fraction of children travel by car ={tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    Hence, the total number of children traveled by car = {tex}\frac{1}{4}\times{/tex} Total number of children
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 18=\frac{1}{4} \times{/tex} Total number of children
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total number of children = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 72
  4. The fraction of children travel by car is 30o = {tex}30 \over 360{/tex} = {tex}1 \over 12{/tex}
    The no. of children using transport is unknown
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} No. of children using car is 0
  5. The central angle made up the sectors representing cycle and walk are the same. Hence, the cycle and walk are two modes of transport, by which an equal number of children are travelling.
Q 86

A die is rolled once. What is the probability that the number on top will be

  1. odd
  2. greater than 5
  3. a multiple of 3
  4. less than 1
  5. A factor of 36
  6. A factor of 6
Solution

When a die is rolled once, then the possible outcomes are 1,2, 3, 4, 5, and 6.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Probability that number on top will be

  1. odd = {tex}\frac{3}{6}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  2. greater than 5 = {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
  3. a multiple of 3 = {tex}\frac{2}{6}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
  4. less than 1 = 0
  5. a factor of 36 = {tex}5 \over 6{/tex}
  6. a factor of 6 = {tex}\frac{4}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
Q 87

Classify the following statements under appropriate headings.

  1. Getting the sum of angles of a triangle is 180o.
  2. India winning a cricket match against Pakistan.
  3. Sun setting in the evening.
  4. Getting 7 when a die is thrown.
  5. Sun rising from the west.
  6. Winning a racing competition by you.
Certain to happen Impossible to happen May or may not happen
     
     
Solution
  1. Certain to happen, because the sum of the angles of a triangle is 180o.
  2. May or may not happen, as the result of the match is unpredictable.
  3. Certain to happen, as the Sun always set in the evening.
  4. Impossible to happen, as there are only 6 possible outcomes on throwing a die, i.e. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6.
  5. Impossible to happen, as the Sunrise from East.
  6. May or may not happen, as the winning of the competition is unpredictable.

Q 89

Ritwik draws a ball from a bag that contains white and yellow balls. The probability of choosing a white ball is {tex}\frac {2}{9}{/tex}. If the total number of balls in the bag is 36, then find the number of yellow balls.

Solution

​​The probability of choosing a white ball = {tex}\frac {2}{9}{/tex}
Then, the probability of choosing a yellow ball = 1 - {tex}\frac{2}{9}=\frac{9-2}{9}=\frac{7}{9}{/tex}
Given, total number of balls in the bag = 36
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of yellow balls = {tex}\frac {7}{9}\times{/tex}36 = 28

Q 90

Look at the histogram below and answer the questions that follow.

  1. How many students have height more than or equal to 135 cm, but less than 150 cm?
  2. Which class interval has the least number of students?
  3. What is class size?
  4. How many students have height less than 140 cm?
Solution
  1. Number of students who have height more than or equal to 135 cm, but less than 150 cm = 14+ 18+ 10 = 42
  2. The class interval 150-155 has the least number of students, i.e. 4.
  3. We know, class size = Upper class limit - Lower class limit. Consider any class, say (125-130), then class size = 130-125 = 5 Hence, the class size is 5.
  4. Number of students who have height less than 140 cm = 6 + 8 + 14 = 28

Q 96

Shoes of the following brands are sold in November 2007 at a shoe store. Construct a pie chart for the given data.

Brand Number of pairs of shoes sold

B 120
C 90
D 40

Solution

Total number of pairs of shoes sold = (130 + 120 + 90 + 40 + 20) = 400
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Central angle of pie chart representing the brands:

  1. {tex}A=\frac{130}{400} \times 360^{\circ}=117^{\circ}{/tex} (as total central angle =360o)
  2. {tex}B=\frac{120}{400} \times 360^{\circ}=108^{\circ}{/tex}
  3. {tex}C=\frac{90}{40} \times 360^{\circ}=81^{\circ}{/tex}
  4. {tex}D=\frac{40}{400} \times 360^{\circ}=36^{\circ}{/tex}
  5. {tex}E=\frac{20}{400} \times 360^{\circ}=18^{\circ}{/tex}
Q 97

The following pie chart depicts the expenditure of a state government under different heads:

  1. If the total spending is 10 crore, how much money was spent on roads?
  2. How many times is the amount of money spents on education compared to the amount spent on roads?
  3. What fraction of the total expenditure is spents on both roads and public welfare together?
Solution
  1. Money spent on roads = 10%of total spending = {tex}\frac{10}{100} \times 10 \;\mathrm{crore}{/tex} = 1 crore
  2. Money spent on education = 25% of total spending = {tex}\frac{25}{100} \times {/tex} Total spending
    Money spent on roads = 10% of total spending = {tex}\frac{10}{100} \times{/tex} Total spending
    Now, {tex}\frac{\text { money spent on education }}{\text { money spent on roads }}=\frac{25}{10}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Money spent on education = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} Money spent on roads
  3. Fraction of the total expenditure spent on both roads and public welfare
    = 10% + 20% = {tex}\frac{10}{100}+\frac{20}{100}=\frac{(10+20)}{100}=\frac{30}{100}=\frac{3}{10}{/tex}
Q 98

The following data represents the different number of animals in a zoo. Prepare a pie chart for the given data.

Animals Number of animals

Q 99

Playing cards

  1. From a pack of cards, the following cards are kept face down:

    Suhail wins if he picks up a face card. Find the probability of Suhail winning?
  2. Now, the following cards are added to the above cards:

    What is the probability of Suhail winning now? Reshma wins if she picks up a 4. What is the probability of Reshma winning? [Queen, King and Jack cards are called face cards.]
Solution

From the given information, it is clear that 

  1. p (Suhail winning) = {tex}\frac {1}{7}{/tex} [as there is only one face card]
  2. P (Suhail winning now) = {tex}\frac {4}{14}{/tex}= {tex}\frac {2}{7}{/tex} [as there are 4 face cards]
    P (Reshma winning) = {tex}\frac {4}{14}{/tex}= {tex}\frac {2}{7}{/tex} [as there are four 4-number cards]

Q 104

  3. In which age group, are literate females the least?
  4. In which age group, is the number of literate females the highest?
Solution
  1. As we know that, the age group of 10 yr to 40 yr is to be divided into classes of equal width, starting with 10. Then, the classes of equal width can be written as 10-15, 15-20, 20-25, 25-30, 30-35, 35-40

Q 111

At a birthday party, the children spin a wheel to get a gift.

Find the probability of:

  1. getting a ball
  2. getting a toy car
  3. getting any toy except a chocolate
Solution
  1. The probability of getting a ball = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events of getting a ball }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{2}{8}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
  2. The probability of getting a toy car = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events of getting a toy car }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{3}{8}{/tex}
  3. The probability of getting any gift except a chocolate = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events of getting any gift except a chocolate }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{7}{8}{/tex}
Q 112

Sonia picks up a card from the given cards

Find the probability of getting

  1. an odd number
  2. a Y card
  3. a G card
  4. a B card bearing number greater than 7
Solution

From the given information, it is clear that: 

  1. The probability of getting an odd number = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events getting an odd number }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{5}{10}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
  2. The probability of getting a Y card = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events getting a Y card }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{3}{10}{/tex}
  3. The probability of getting a G card = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events getting a G card }}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{2}{10}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
  4. The probability of getting a B card bearing number greater than 7 = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of events getting a B card bearing number greater than } 7}{\text { Total number of events }}=\frac{0}{10}{/tex} = 0

Thus,

Q 116

Following is a pie chart showing the amount spent (in ₹ thousands) by a company on various modes of advertising for a product. Now, answer the following questions:

Solution

Q 17

Check whether 90 is a perfect square or not by using prime factorisation.

Solution

Prime factorisation of 90 is

90 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
The prime factors 2 and 5 do not occur in pairs. Therefore, 90 is not a perfect square.

Q 18

Using distributive law, find the square of 43.

Solution

We have, 432 = (40 + 3)2
= (40 + 3) (40 + 3) 
= 40(40 + 3) + 3(40 + 3)
= 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 + 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
= 1600 + 240 + 9
= 1849

Q 19

Write a Pythagorean triplet whose smallest number is 6.

Solution

We have pythagorean triplet: 2m, m2 + 1, m2 - 1
Smallest number is 6
2m = 6 or m = 3
m2 + 1 = 32 + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10
m2 - 1 = 32 - 1 = 9 - 1 = 8
So, the Pythagorean triplet is 6, 8, 10.

Q 20

Evaluate the square root of 22.09 by long division method.

Solution

The square root of 22.09 by long division method 

Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{22.09}{/tex} = 4.7

Q 21

Find the smallest perfect square divisible by 3, 4, 5 and 6.

Solution

The least number that is divisible by 3, 4, 5 and 6 is their LCM. The LCM of 3, 4, 5 and 6 is 60. Now, 60 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.
We see that prime factors 5 and 3 are not in pairs. Therefore 60 is not a perfect square. So, 60 should be multiplied by 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 15 to get a perfect square.
Thus, the required least square number that is divisible by 3, 4, 5 and 6 = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 900

Q 22

A ladder 10m long rests against a vertical wall. If the foot of the ladder is 6m away from the wall and the ladder just reaches the top of the wall, how high is the wall?

Solution


Let AC be the ladder.
Therefore, AC = 10m
and BC be the distance between the foot of the ladder and the wall.
Therefore, BC = 6m
{tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC is a right-angled triangle, right-angled at B.
AC2 = AB2 + BC2[By Pythagoras theorem]
102 = AB2 + 62
or AB2 = 102 - 62
= 100 - 36
= 64
or AB = {tex}\sqrt{64}{/tex} 
= 8m
Hence, the wall is 8m high.

Q 23

Find the length of a diagonal of a rectangle with dimensions 20m by 15m.

Solution

Using Pythagoras theorem, we have
Length of diagonal of the rectangle ={tex}\sqrt{\left(l^{2}+b^{2}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\sqrt{\left(20^{2}+15^{2}\right)}{/tex} m
= {tex}\sqrt{400+225}{/tex} m
= {tex}\sqrt{625}{/tex} m
= 25 m
Hence, the length of the diagonal is 25 m

Q 24

The area of a rectangular field whose length is twice its breadth is 2450 m2. Find the perimeter of the field.

Solution

Let the breadth of the field be x metres. Then the length of the field is 2x metres.
Therefore, area of the rectangular field = length × breadth
= (2x)(x)
= 2x2 m2
Given that area is 2450 m2.
Therefore, 2x2 = 2450
x2 = {tex}\frac{2450}{2}{/tex} 
x = {tex}\sqrt{1225}{/tex} 
or x = 35 m
Hence, breadth = 35m
and length = 35 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 70m
Perimeter of the rectangular field = 2(l + b)
= 2(70 + 35)m
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 105m
= 210m

Q 25

During a mass drill exercise, 6250 students of different schools are arranged in rows such that the number of students in each row is equal to the number of rows. In doing so, the instructor finds out that 9 children are left out. Find the number of children in each row of the square.

Solution

We have given that the total number of students = 6250
Number of students forming a square = 6250 - 9 = 6241
Thus, 6241 students form a big square which has number of rows equal to the number of students in each row.
Let the number of students in each row be x, then the number of rows = x
Therefore, x {tex}\times{/tex} x = 6241
or x = {tex}\sqrt{6241}{/tex} = 79
Hence, there are 79 students in each row of the square formed.

Q 26

Find the least number that must be added to 1500 so as to get a perfect square. Also find the square root of the perfect square.

Solution

We have,

We observe that 382 < 1500 < 392
Hence, the number to be added = 392 - 1500
= 1521 - 1500
= 21
Therefore, the perfect square is 1500 + 21 = 1521
{tex}\sqrt{1521}{/tex} = 39
Therefore, the required number is 21 and the square root is 39.

Q 27

Find the smallest number by which 1620 must be divided to get a perfect square.

Solution

The prime factorisation of 1620 is 1620 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
We see that prime factor 5 has no pair. So, if we divide 1620 by 5, then we get

1620 {tex}\div{/tex} 5 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
Now each factor has a pair. Therefore, {tex}\frac{1620}{5}{/tex} = 324 is a perfect square.
Thus the required smallest number is 5.

Q 55

Show that 500 is not a perfect square.

Solution

Resolving 500 into prime factors, we have

500 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
Grouping the factors into pairs of equal factors, we get
500 = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) {tex}\times{/tex} (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 ) {tex}\times{/tex}5
Clearly, by grouping into pairs of equal factors, we are left with one factor 5, which cannot be paired.
Hence, 500 is not a perfect square.

After grouping, there is no unpaired factor left over.

So, 484 is a perfect square.

Using prime factorisation, find whether 11250 is a perfect square or not.

We have,

As grouping, 2 has no pair.
So, 11250 is not a perfect square.

Using prime factorisation, find that 841 is a perfect square.
