Q 139

The expression x2 - 6x + 8 is the area of a rectangle. Find the possible length and breadth of this rectangle. 

Solution

Given, area of a rectangle = x2 - 6x + 8
Now, we have to find the possible length and breadth of the rectangle.
So, we factorise the given expression. i.e. x2 - 6x + 8 = x2 - (4 + 2) x + 8 = x2 - 4x - 2x + 8
= x(x - 4) - 2(x - 4) = (x - 4)(x - 2)
Since, area of a rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth. Hence, the possible length and breadth are (x - 4) and (x - 2).

Q 140

The expression x2 - 3x + 2 is the area of a rectangle. Find the possible length and breadth of this rectangle.

Solution

We have,
Area of rectangle = x2 - 3x + 2
= x2 - (2 + 1)x + 2 = x2 - 2x - x + 2
= x(x - 2) -1(x - 2) = (x - 2)(x - 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The possible length and breadth are (x - 2) and (x -1).

Q 141

The expression x2 - 7x + 10 is the area of a rectangle. Find the possible length and breadth of this rectangle.

Solution

We have,
Area of rectangle = x2 - 7x + 10
= x2 - (5 + 2)x + 10 = x2 - 5x - 2x + 10
= x(x - 5) - 2(x - 5) = (x - 5)(x - 2)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The possible length and breadth are (x - 5) and (x - 2).

Q 142

The expression x2 + 19x - 20 is the area of a rectangle. Find the possible length and breadth of this rectangle.

Solution

We have,
Area of rectangle = x2 + 19x - 20
= x2 + (20 - 1)x - 20 = x2 + 20x - x - 20
= x(x + 20) - 1(x + 20) = (x + 20) (x - 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The possible length and breadth are (x + 20) and (x - 1)

Q 143

The expression x2 + 9x + 20 is the area of rectangle. Find the possible length and breadth of this rectangle.

Solution

We have,
Area of rectangle = x2 + 9x + 20
 x2 + 9x + 20 = x2 + (5 + 4)x + 20
= x2 + 5x + 4x + 20
= x(x + 5) + 4(x + 5)
= (x + 5)(x + 4)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The possible length and breadth are (x + 5) and (x + 4)

Q 144

The area of a square is given by 4x2 + 12xy + 9y2. Find the side of the square.

Solution

We have, Area of square = 4x2 + 12xy + 9y2
So, we factorise the given expression.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 4x2 + 12xy + 9y2 = (2x)2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2x {tex}\times{/tex} 3y + (3y)2  [{tex}\because{/tex} a2 + 2ab+ b2 = (a + b)2]
= (2x + 3y)2
Since, area of a square having side length a is a2. Hence, side of the given square is 2x + 3y.

Q 145

The area of a square is 9x2 + 24xy + 16y2. Find the side of the square.

Solution

We have,
Area of a square = 9x2 + 24xy + 16y2 = (3x)2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3x {tex}\times{/tex} 4y + (4y)2 [{tex}\because{/tex} a2 + 2ab + b2 = (a + b)2]
= (3x + 4y)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The side of the square = 3x + 4y [{tex}\because{/tex} area of square = (side)2]

Q 146

The area of a rectangle is x2 + 7x + 12. If its breadth is (x + 3), then find its length.

Solution

Let the length of the rectangle be I,
Given, area of a rectangle = x2 + 7x + 12
and breadth = x + 3
We know that,
Area of rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 7x + 12 = l {tex}\times{/tex} (x + 3)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+7 x+12}{x+3}=\frac{x^{2}+4 x+3 x+12}{x+3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x(x+4)+3(x+4)}{x+3}=\frac{(x+4)(x+3)}{x+3}{/tex} = x + 4
Hence, the length of rectangle = x + 4

Q 147

The curved surface area of a cylinder is 2{tex}\pi{/tex} (y2 - 7y + 12) and its radius is (y - 3). Find the height of the cylinder (CSA of cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh)

Solution

Let the height of cylinder be h,
Given, the curved surface area of a cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(y2 - 7 y + 12) and radius of cylinder = y - 3
We know that, Curved surface area of cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(y2 - 7y + 12)
 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(y2 - 4y - 3y+ 12) using middle term splitting
= 2{tex}\pi{/tex}[y(y- 4 ) - 3 ( y - 4)]
= 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(y - 3) (y - 4)
2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r(y - 4) [{tex}\because{/tex} r = (y - 3), given]
On comparing the both sides, we get h = y - 4
Hence, the height of the cylinder is y - 4

Q 148

The area of a circle is given by the expression {tex}\pi{/tex}x2 + 6{tex}\pi{/tex}x + 9{tex}\pi{/tex}. Find the radius of the circle.

Solution

We have, Area of a circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}x2 + 6{tex}\pi{/tex}x + 9{tex}\pi{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}(x2 + 6x + 9)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}(x2 + 3x + 3x + 9) [{tex}\because{/tex} area of a circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2, where r is the radius]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}[x(x + 3) + 3(x + 3 )]= {tex}\pi{/tex}(x + 3)(x + 3) = {tex}\pi{/tex}(x + 3)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex}r2 = {tex}\pi{/tex}(x + 3)2
On comparing both sides, r2 = (x + 3)2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = x + 3
Hence, the radius of circle is x + 3

Q 149

The sum of first n natural numbers is given by the expression {tex}\frac{n^{2}}{2}+\frac{n}{2}{/tex} Factorise this expression.

Solution

We have, the sum of first n natural numbers = {tex}\frac{n^{2}}{2}+\frac{n}{2}{/tex}
Factorisation of given expression {tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left(n^{2}+n\right)=\frac{1}{2} n(n+1){/tex} [taking n as common]

Q 150

The sum of (x + 5) observations is x4 - 625. Find the mean of the observations.

Solution

We have, the sum of (x + 5) observations = x4 - 625
We know that, the mean of the n observations x1, x2, ... xn is given by {tex}\frac{x_{1}+x_{2}+\ldots x_{n}}{n}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The mean of (x + 5 ) observations {tex}=\frac{\text { Sum of }(x+5) \text { observations }}{x+5}=\frac{x^{4}-625}{x+5}=\frac{\left(x^{2}\right)^{2}-(25)^{2}}{x+5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+25\right)\left(x^{2}-25\right)}{x+5}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} a2 - b2 = (a -b)(a + b)]
= {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+25\right)\left[(x)^{2}-(5)^{2}\right]}{x+5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+25\right)(x+5)(x-5)}{(x+5)}{/tex} = (x2 + 25)(x - 5)

Q 151

The height of a triangle is x4 + y4 and its base is 14xy. Find the area of the triangle.

Solution

Given, the height of a triangle and its base are x4 + y4 and 14xy, respectively.
We know that, the area of a triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\times{/tex}Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\times{/tex}14xy {tex}\times{/tex} (x4 + y4)
= 7xy(x4 + y4)

Q 152

The cost of a chocolate is ₹(x + 4) and Rohit bought (x + 4) chocolates. Find the total amount paid by him in terms of x. If x = 10.

Solution

Given, cost of a chocolate = ₹ (x + 4)
Rohit bought (x + 4) chocolates
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The cost of (x + 4 )chocolates = Cost of one chocolate {tex}\times{/tex} Number of chocolates = (x + 4)(x + 4 )= (x + 4)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The total amount paid by Rohit = ₹(x2 + 8x + 16) [{tex}\because{/tex} (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]
Now, if x = 10. Then, the amount paid by Rohit = 102 + 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10+ 16 = 100 + 80 + 16 = ₹196

Q 153

The base of a parallelogram is (2x + 3) units and the corresponding height is (2x - 3) units. Find the area of the parallelogram in terms of x. What will be the area of a parallelogram of x = 10 units?

Solution

The base and the corresponding height of a parallelogram are (2x + 3) units and (2x - 3) units, respectively.
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of a parallelogram = Base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= (2x + 3) {tex}\times{/tex} (2x - 3)
= (2x)2 - (3)2 [{tex}\because{/tex} (a + b)(a - b) = a2 - b2]
= (4x2 - 9)sq.units
Now, at x =10 
Therefore, the area of the parallelogram = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (10)2 - 9
= 400 - 9
= 391 sq. units

Q 154

The radius of a circle is 7ab - 7bc - 14ac. Find the circumference of the circle, use {tex}\left(\pi=\frac{22}{7}\right){/tex}.

Solution

We have, radius of the circle = 7ab - 7bc - 14ac = r
We know that,
{tex}\because{/tex} The circumference of the circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r [say]
= 2 {tex}\times \frac{22}{7}\times{/tex} (7ab - 7bc - 14ac) =  2 {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{22}{7}\times{/tex} 7 (ab - bc - 2ac) = 44[ab - c(b + 2a)]

Q 155

If p + q = 12 and pq = 22, then find p2 + q2

Solution

Given, p + q = 12 and pq = 22
Since, (p + q)2 = p2 + q2 + 2pq [using the identity, (a + b)2 = a2 + b2 + 2ab]
(12)2 = p2+ q2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 22
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 + q2 = (12 )2 - 44
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p2 + q2 = 144 - 44 = 100

Q 156

If a + b = 25 and a2 + b2 = 225, then find ab.

Solution

Given, a + b = 25 and a2 + b2 = 225
We know that, (a + b)2 = a2 + b2 + 2ab [an algebraic identity]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (25)2 = 225 + 2ab
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ab = (25)2 - 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ab = 625 - 225
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2ab = 400
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ab = {tex}\frac{400}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} ab = 200

Q 157

If x - y = 13 and xy = 28, then find x2 + y2

Solution

Given, x - y = 13 and xy = 28
Since, (x - y)2 = x2 + y2 - 2xy [an algebraic identity] (a - b)2 = a2 + b2 - 2ab]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (13)2 = x 2 + y2 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 28
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = (13)2 + 56
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 169 + 56
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + y2 = 225

Q 158

If m - n = 16 and m2 + n2 = 400, then find mn.

Solution

Given, m - n = 16 and m2 + n2 = 400
Since, (m - n)2 = m2 + n2 - 2mn [using the identity, (a - b)2 = a2 + b2 - 2ab]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (16)2 = 400 - 2mn
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2mn = 400 - (16)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2mn = 400 - 256
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2mn = 144
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mn = {tex}\frac {144}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} mn = 72

Q 159

If a2 + b2 = 74 and ab = 35, then find a + b.

Solution

Given, a2 + b2 = 74 and ab = 35
Since, (a + b)2 = a2 + b2 + 2ab [using the identity, (a + b)2 = a2 + b2 + 2ab]
(a + b)2 = 74 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a + b)2 = 74 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a + b)2 = 144
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = {tex}\sqrt{144}{/tex} [taking square root]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = 12 [rejecting -ve sign]

Q 160

Verify that (ab + bc)(ab - bc) + (bc + ca)(bc - ca) + (ca + ab)(ca - ab) = 0

Solution

Taking LHS = (ab + bc)(ab - bc) + (bc + ca)(bc - ca) + (ca + ab)(ca - ab) = 0
= [(ab)2 - (bc)2] + [(bc)2 - (ca)2] + [(ca)2 - (ab)2] [using the identity, (a + b)(a - b) = a2 - b2]
= a2b2 - b2c2 + b2c2 - c2a2 + c2a2 - a2b2 = 0
= RHS [cancelling the like terms having opposite sings]

Q 161

Verify that (a + b + c)(a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca) = a3 + b3 + c3 - 3abc

Solution

Taking LHS = (a + b + c)(a2 + b2 + c 2 - ab - bc - ca) [distributive law]
= a(a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca) + b(a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca) + c(a2 + b2 + c2 - ab - bc - ca) [distributive law]
= a3 + ab2 + ac2 - a2b - abc - a2c + ba2 + b3 + bc2 - b2a - b2c - bca + ca2 + cb2+ c3 - cab - c2b - c2a
= a3 + b3 + c3 - 3abc = RHS

Q 162

Verify that (p - q) (p2 + pq + q2) = p3 - q3.

Solution

Taking LHS = (p - q) (p2 + pq + q2)
= p(p2 + pq + q2) - q(p2 + pq + q2)
= p3 + p2q + pq2 - qp2 - pq2 - q3 = p3 - q3 = RHS

Q 163

Verify the (m + n)(m2 - mn + n2) = m3 + n3

Solution

Taking LHS = (m + n)(m2 - mn +n2)
= m(m2 - mn + n2) + n(m2 - mn +n2)
= m3 - m2n + mn2 + nm2 - mn2 + n3
m3 + n3 = RHS

Q 164

Verify that (a + b)(a + b)(a + b) = a3 + 3a2b + 3ab2 + b3

Solution

Taking LHS = (a + b)(a + b)(a + b)
= (a + b)(a + b)2
= (a + b)(a2 + b2+2ab)
= a(a2 + 2ab + b2) + b (a2 + 2ab + b2)
= a3 + 2a2b + ab2 + ba2 + 2ab2 + b3
= a3 + 3a2b + 3ab2 + b3 [adding like terms]
= RHS

Q 165

Verify that (a - b)(a - b)(a - b) = a3 - 3a2b + 3ab2 - b3

Solution

Taking LHS = (a - b)(a - b)(a - b)
= (a -b )(a -b)2
= (a - b)(a2 - 2ab + b2)
= a(a2 - 2ab + b2) - b(a2 - 2ab + b2)
= a3 - 2a2b + ab2 - ba2 + 2ab2 - b3
= a3 - 3a2b + 3ab2 - b3 [adding like terms]
= RHS

Q 166

Verify that (a2 - b2)(a2 + b2) + (b2 - c2)(b2 + c2)+ (c2 - a2) (c2 + a2)= 0

Solution

Taking LHS = (a2 - b2)(a2 + b2) + (b2 - c2)(b2 + c2) + (c2 - a2)(c2 + a2)
= (a4 - b4 + b4 - c4 + c4 - a4) [using identity, (a - b)(a + b) = a2 - b2]
= 0 = RHS

Q 167

Verify that (5x + 8)2 - 160x = (5x -8)2

Solution

Taking LHS = (5x + 8)2 - 160x
= (5x)2 + (8)2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5x {tex}\times{/tex} 8 - 160x [using the identity, (a + b)2 = a2 + 2ab + b2]
= (5x)2 + (8)2 + 80x - 160x
= (5x)2 + (8)2 - 80x
= (5x)2 + (8)2 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5x {tex}\times{/tex} 8
= (5x - 8)2
= RHS

Q 168

Verify that (7p - 13q)2 + 364pq = (7p + 13q)2.

Solution

Taking L.H.S. = (7p - 13q)2 + 364pq
= (7p)2 + (13q)2 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7p {tex}\times{/tex} 13q + 364pq
= (7p)2 + (13q)2 - 182pq + 364pq
= (7p)2 + (13q)2 + 182pq
= (7p)2 + (13q)2 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7p {tex}\times{/tex} 13q  [using identity a2 +b2 + 2ab = (a +b)2]
= (7p + 13q)2 = R.H.S. 
Hence verified.

Q 169

verify that {tex}\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{3 p}{7}-\frac{7}{6 p}\right)^{2}{/tex}= 2

Solution

​​Taking LHS = {tex}\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{3 p}{7}-\frac{7}{6 p}\right)^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}\right)+\left(\frac{3 p}{7}-\frac{7}{6 p}\right)\right]\left[\left(\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}\right)-\left(\frac{3 p}{7}-\frac{7}{6 p}\right)\right]\right.{/tex} [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a - b)]
= {tex}\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}+\frac{3 p}{7}-\frac{7}{6 p}\right)\left(\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}-\frac{3 p}{7}+\frac{7}{6 p}\right)=\frac{6 p}{7} \times \frac{14}{6 p}{/tex} = 2 = RHS

Q 170

Find the value of a, if 8a = 352 - 272

Solution

We have, 8a = 352 - 272
 8a = 352 - 272
= (35 + 27)(35 - 27) [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a - b)]
 8a = 62 {tex}\times{/tex} 8
a = {tex}\frac{62 \times 8}{8}{/tex}
a = 62

Q 171

Find the value of a, if 9a = (76)2 - (67)2

9a = (76 + 67)(76 - 67) [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a - b)]
9a = 143 {tex}\times{/tex} 9
 a = {tex}\frac{143 \times 9}{9}{/tex}
 a = 143

Q 172

Find the value of a, if pqa = (3p + q)2 - (3p - q)2

Solution

We have, pqa = (3p + q)2 - (3p - q)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pqa = [(3p + q) + (3p - q)][(3p + q) - (3p - q) ] [using the identity, a2 - b2(a + b)(a - b)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pqa = [(3p + q + 3p - q)] [3p + q - 3p + q]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pqa = 6p {tex}\times{/tex} 2q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{6 p \times 2 q}{p q}=\frac{(6 \times 2) p q}{p q}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 12

Q 173

Find the value of a, if pq2a = (4pq + 3q)2 - (4pq - 3q)2

Solution

We have, pq2a = (4pq + 3q)2 - (4pq - 3q)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = [(4pq + 3q )+ (4pq - 3q)][(4pq + 3q) - (4pq - 3p)] [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a-b)]
= (4pq + 3q + 4pq - 3q)(4pq + 3q - 4pq + 3q)
= 8pq {tex}\times{/tex} 6q
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} pq2a = 48pq2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{48 p q^{2}}{p q^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 48

Q 174

What should be added to 4c(-a + b + c) to obtain 3a(a + b + c) - 2b (a - b + c)?

Solution

Let x be added to the given expression
4c (-a + b + c) to obtain 3a(a + b + c) -2b(a - b + c)
i.e. x + 4c(-a + b + c ) = 3a(a + b + c) -2b(a - b + c)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3a(a + b + c) -2b(a - b + c) - 4c(- a + b + c)
= 3a2 + 3ab + 3ac -2ba + 2b2 - 2bc + 4ca - 4cb - 4c2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3a2 + ab + 7ac + 2b2 - 6bc - 4c2 [adding the like terms]

Q 175

Subtract b(b2 + b - 7) + 5 from 3b2 - 8 and find the value of expression obtained for b = -3.

Solution

Required difference = (3b2 - 8) - [b(b2 + b - 7) + 5]
= 3b2 - 8 - b(b2 + b - 7 ) - 5
= 3b2 - 8 - b3 - b2 + 7b - 5 = - b3 + 2b2 + 7b - 13
Now, if b = -3
The value of above expression = -(-3)3 + 2(-3)2 + 7(-3) - 13
= -(-27) + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 - 21 - 13
= 27 + 18 - 21 - 13
= 45 - 34 = 11

Q 176

If {tex}x-\frac{1}{x}=7{/tex}, then find the value of {tex}x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}x-\frac{1}{x}=7{/tex}
Since, {tex}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}=x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}-2 \cdot x \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using the identity, (a - b)2 = a2 + b2 - 2ab]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}7^{2}=x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}-2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 49 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{2}+\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 51

Q 177

Find the value of {tex}\frac{6.25 \times 6.25-1.75 \times 1.75}{4.5}{/tex}.

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{6.25 \times 6.25-1.75 \times 1.75}{4.5}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(6.25)^{2}-(1.75)^{2}}{4.5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(6.25+1.75)(6.25-1.75)}{4.5}{/tex} [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b)]
= {tex}\frac{8 \times 4.5}{4.5}=8{/tex}

Q 178

Find the value of {tex} \frac{198 \times 198-102 \times 102}{96}{/tex} .

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{198 \times 198-102 \times 102}{96}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(198)^{2}-(102)^{2}}{96}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(198+102)(198-102)}{96}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{300 \times 96}{96}{/tex} = 300 [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a - b)(a + b)]

Q 179

The product of two expressions is x5 + x3 + x. If one of them is x2 + x +1, find the other.

Solution

We have, product of two expressions x5 + x3 + x and one is x2 + x + 1
Let the other expression be A. Then, A{tex}\cdot{/tex}(x2 + x + 1) = x5 + x3 + x
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{x^{5}+x^{3}+x}{x^{2}+x+1}=\frac{x\left(x^{4}+x^{2}+1\right)}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A=\frac{x\left(x^{4}+2 x^{2}-x^{2}+1\right)}{x^{2}+x+1}=\frac{x\left(x^{4}+2 x^{2}+1-x^{2}\right)}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} [adding and subtracting x2 in numerator term
= {tex}\frac{x\left[\left(x^{4}+2 x^{2}+1\right)-x^{2}\right]}{x^{2}+x+1}=\frac{x\left[\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2}-x^{2}\right]}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x\left(x^{2}+1+x\right)\left(x^{2}+1-x\right)}{x^{2}+x+1}{/tex} [using the identity, a2 - b2 = (a + b)(a - b)]
= x(x2 + 1 - x)
Hence, the other expression is x(x2 - x + 1)

Q 180

Find the length of the side of the given square, if the area of the square is 625sq units and then find the value of x.

Solution

We have, a square having length of a side (4x + 5) units and the area is 625 sq units.
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of a square = (Side)2
(4x + 5)2 = 625
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (4x + 5)2 = (25)2 [taking square root both sides and neglecting (-ve) sign]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x + 5 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 25 - 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x = 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5
Hence, side(length) = 4x + 5 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + 5 = 25units

Q 181

Take suitable number of cards given in the adjoining diagram [G(x {tex}\times{/tex} x) representing x2, R (x {tex}\times{/tex} 1) representing x and Y (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) representing 1] to factorise the following expressions, by arranging the cards in the form of rectangles:

  1. 2x2 + 6x + 4
  2. x2 + 4x + 4.

Factorise 2x2 + 6x + 4 by using the figure.

Calculate the area of figure.

Solution

Students do it by yourself.

Q 182

The figure shows the dimensions of a wall having a window and a door of a room. Write an algebraic expression for the area of the wall to be painted.

Solution

We have a wall of dimension 5x {tex}\times{/tex} (5x + 2) having a window and a door of dimension (2x {tex}\times{/tex} x) and (3x {tex}\times{/tex} x), respectively.
Then, area of the window = 2x {tex}\times{/tex} x = 2x2 sq. units
Area of the door = 3x {tex}\times{/tex} x = 3x2 sq. units
and area of wall = (5x + 2) {tex}\times{/tex} 5x = (25x2 + 10x) sq. units
Now, area of the required part of the wall to be painted = Area of the wall - (Area of the window + Area of the door)
= 25x2 + 10x - (2x2 + 3x2)
= 25x2 + 10x - 5x2
= 25x2 + 10x - 5x2 = 20x2 + 10x
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} x + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} x
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} x(2x + 1) = 10x(2x + 1)sq. units

Q 183

Match the expressions of column I with that of column II:

Column I Column II
(a) (21x + 13y)2 (i) 441x2 - 169y2
(b) (21x - 13y)2 (ii) 441x2 + 169y2 + 546 xy
(c) (21x - 13y) (21x + 13y) (iii) 441x2 + 169y2 - 546xy
  (iv) 441x2 – 169y2 + 546xy
Solution

The correct matching is as follows:
(a) - (ii), (b) - (iii), (c) - (i)

Q 13

A polyhedron has 7 faces and 10 vertices. How many edges does the polyhedron have?

Solution

For any polyhedron,
Using Euler's formula:
F + V - E = 2
Here, F = 7, V = 10, E = ?
Using above formula,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 10 – E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 17 - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 17 - 2 = E
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ε = 15

Q 14

Find the number of vertices in a polyhedron which has 30 edges and 12 faces.

Solution

For any polyhedron,
Using Euler's formula: F + V –  E = 2
Here, F = 12,
V = ?, E = 30
Using above formula,
12 + V - 30 = 2
V - 18 = 2
V = 2 + 18
V = 20

Q 15

The distance between City A and City B on a map is given as 6 cm. If the scale represents 1 cm = 200 km, then find the actual distance between City A and City B.

Solution

We have, Actual distance represented by 1cm = 200 km
Actual distance represented by 6 cm = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 km
= 1200 km
So, the actual distance between City A and City B = 1200 km

Q 16

Height of a building is 9 m and this building is represented by 9 cm on a map. What is the scale used for the map?

Solution

We have, Scale of map = {tex}\frac{\text { Size drawn }}{\text { Actual size }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9 \mathrm{cm}}{900 \mathrm{cm}}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} 9 m = 900 cm)
{tex}=\frac{1}{100}{/tex}
Thus, the scale is 1 : 100

Q 17

The scale on a map is 1 mm : 4 m. Find the distance on the map for an actual distance of 52 m.

Solution

We have, Distance on the map for an actual distance of 4 m = 1 mm
The distance on the map for actual distance of 52 m {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times 52{/tex}
= 13 mm
Thus, the distance on the map for the actual distance of 52 m = 13 mm

Q 18

Application of problem solving strategy
Determine the number of edges, vertices and in the figure:

Solution

Understand and Explore the problem

  • What information is given in the question?
    A cube with one of its corner cut.
  • What are you trying to find?
    The no. of edges, vertices and faces.
  • Is there any information that is not needed?
    The measures of edges are not needed.
    Plan a strategy
  • Think of the definitions of an edge, vertex and faces and try to co-relate them to the figure given above.
    Solve
  • The polygonal regions are called faces, hence there are 7 faces.
  • The line segment formed by the intersection of two faces is called edges, hence there are 15 edges.
  • Edges meet at vertices which are points, hence there are 10 vertices.
    Revise
  • The above answer is verified using Euler’s Formula.
    F + V = E + 2
    For the above problem, F = 7, V = 10, E = 15
    F + V = 7 + 10 = 17
    E + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17
    Hence F + V = E + 2 is verified.

Q 70

Look at the shapes given below and state which of these are polyhedra using Euler’s formula.

Solution
  1. In the given figure, we have Faces F = 5, Vertices V = 6 and edges E = 9 
    On putting these values in Euler's formula, we get
    F + V - E = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + 6 - 9 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 - 9 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = 2
    Hence, these values satisfies the Euler's formula. So, it is a polyhedra.
  2. In the given figure, we have
    Faces F = 6, vertices (V) = 8 and edges (E) = 12
     On putting these values in Euler's formula, we get
    F + V - E = 2 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}6 + 8 - 12 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}14 - 12 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2 = 2
    Hence, these values satisfy Euler's formula. So, it is a polyhedra.
  3. In the given figure, we have
    Faces (F) = 3, vertices (V) = 0 and edges (E) = 2
    On putting these values in Euler's formula, we get
    F + V - E = 2 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + 0 - 2 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}1 \neq 2{/tex}
    Hence, these values do not satisfy the Euler's formula. So, it is not a polyhedra.
  4. In the given figure, we have
    Faces (F) = 7, vertices (V) = 10 and edges (E) = 15
     On putting these values in Euler's formula, we get
    F + V - E = 2 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 10 - 15 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 17 - 15 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2  = 2
    Hence, these values satisfies the Euler's formula. So, it is a polyhedra.
  5. In the given figure, we have
    Faces (F) = 5, vertices (V) = 6 and edges (E) = 9 
    On putting these values in Euler's formula, we get
    F + V - E = 2 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + 6 - 9 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11 - 9 = 2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = 2
    Hence, these values satisfies the Euler's formula. So, it is a polyhedra.

    F + V - E = 2 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + 0 - 2 = 2

    Hence, these values do not satisfy the Euler's formula. So, it is not a polyhedra.

Q 73

Using Euler’s formula, find the value of unknown x, y, z, p, q, r, in the following table.

  (i) (ii) (iii) (iv) (v) (vi)
Faces 7 y 9 p 6 8
Vertices 10 12 z 6 q 11
Edges x 18 16 12 12 r
Solution

By using Euler's formula for polyhedron
From (i), F = 7, V = 10 and E = x
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 10 - x = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 17 - x = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 17 - 2 = x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15.
From (ii), F = 12, V = 18 and F = y
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y + 12 -18 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 6 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 8.
From (iii), F = 9, V = 16 and V = z
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 9 + z -16 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z - 7 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 2 + 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} z = 9.
From (iv), F = 6, V = 12 and F =p
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p + 6 -12 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p - 6 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 2 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 8.

From (v), F = 6, V = 12 and V = q
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6 + q -12 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q - 6 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 2 + 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} q = 8.
From (vi), F = 8, V = 11 and E = r
So,
F + V - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 + 11 - r = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 19 - r = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 19 - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r = 17.

Q 75

Check whether a polyhedron can have V = 12, E = 6 and F = 8.

Solution

By using Euler’s formula for polyhedron,
F + V - E = 2 [where, F = faces, V = vertices, E = edges] 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 + 12 - 6 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20 - 6 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 14 {tex}\ne{/tex} 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Given values do not satisfy the Euler’s formula. It means that this type of polyhedron cannot be possible.

Solution

By using Euler’s formula for polyhedron,
F + V - E = 2 [where, F = faces, V = vertices, E = edges]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  F + 40 - 60 = 2 [{tex}\because{/tex}E = 60 and V = 40, given]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F - 20 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = 2 + 20
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F = 22
Hence, the number of faces are 22.

Q 78

A polyhedron has 20 faces and 12 vertices. Find the edges of the polyhedron.

Solution

By using Euler’s formula for polyhedron,
F + V - E = 2 [where, F = faces, V = vertices, E = edges]
Given, faces (E) = 20, vertices (V) = 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 20 + 12 - E = 2
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}32 - E = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}E = 32 - 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E = 30
Hence, the edges of the polyhedron are 30.

Q 79

A solid has forty faces and sixty edges. Find the number of vertices of the solid.

Solution

By using Euler’s formula for polyhedron
F + V - E = 2
Given, faces (F) = 40, edges (E) = 60
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 40 + V - 60 = 2 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V -20 = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = 2 + 20 = 22
Hence, the vertices of the solid are 22.

Q 89

How many faces, edges and vertices does a pyramid have with n sided polygon as its base?

Solution

We know that in a pyramid, the number of vertices is 1 more than the number of sides of the polygon base, i.e. vertices = n + 1
Also, the number of faces is 1 more than the number of sides of the polygonal base, i.e. faces = n + 1
But the number of edges is 2 times the number of sides of the polygonal base
Therefore, edges = 2n

Q 99

A photographer uses a computer program to enlarge a photograph. What is the scale according to which the width has enlarged in the given graph?

Solution

By the given graph, we have width before editing = 2 units
Width after editing = 4 units
We know that, Scale = {tex}\frac { Size \;before\; editing }{ Size \;after\; editing}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Scale = {tex}\frac{2}{4}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1:2
Hence, scale used to enlarge the photograph is 1 : 2.

Q 100

The side of a square board is 50 cm. A student has to draw its image in her notebook. If the drawing of the square board in the notebook has perimeter of 40 cm, then by which scale the figure has been drawn?

Solution

Given, the side of a square board is 50 cm.
So, perimeter of the square board = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Side = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 = 200 cm
On drawing in the notebook, the perimeter of a square board = 40 cm
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { Scale }=\frac{\text { Size of actual square board }}{\text { Size in notebook }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{200}{40}=5: 1{/tex}
Hence, the used scale is 5 :1.

Q 101

The distance between school and house of a girl is given by 5 cm in a picture, using the scale 1cm: 5 km. Find the actual distance between the two places?

Solution

Given scale = 1 cm : 5 km, i.e. 1 cm in picture = 5 km of actual distance
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 5 cm in picture = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 km
Hence, the actual distance between the two places is 25 km.

Q 102

Use a ruler to measure the distance in cm between the places joined by dotted lines. If the map has been drawn using the scale, 1 cm : 10 km, find the actual distances between

  1. School and Library
  2. College and Complex
  3. House and School
Solution

Given scale is 1 cm : 10 km, i.e. 1 cm in a picture = 10 km of actual distance

  1. The distance between the school and libarary in the picture = 6 cm Hence, the actual distance between the school and library = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 60 km
  2. Distance between the college and complex in the picture = 2 cm Hence, the actual distance between the college and complex = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 20 km
  3. Distance between the house and school in the picture = 3.5 cm Hence, the actual distance between the house and school = 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 35 km
Q 103

The actual length of a painting was 2m. What is the length in the photograph if the scale used is 1 mm : 20 cm.

Solution

​​The actual length of the painting was 2 m or 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 200 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m = 100 cm]
Scale used in the painting = 1 mm : 20 cm
Hence, length of painting in photograph = Scale {tex}\times{/tex} Actual Size {tex}\left[\because \text { scale }=\frac{\text { size drawn }}{\text { actual size }}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{20} \times 200{/tex} = 10 mm

Q 104

Find the scale,

  1. Actual size 12 m Drawing size 3 cm
  2. Actual size 45 feet Drawing size 5 inches Size drawn
Solution
  1. By using, scale = {tex}\frac{\text { Size drawn }}{\text { Actual size }}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{3 \mathrm{cm}}{12 \mathrm{m}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1 \mathrm{cm}}{4 \mathrm{m}}{/tex} = 1 cm: 4m
    Hence, scale = 1 cm : 4 m
  2. By using, scale = {tex}\frac{\text { Size drawn }}{\text { Actual size }}=\frac{5 \text { inches }}{45 \text { feet }}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1 \text { inch }}{9 \text { feet }}{/tex} = 1 inch : 9 feet
    Hence, scale = 1 inch : 9 feet
Q 105

In a town, an ice-cream parlour has displayed an ice-cream sculpture of height 360 cm. The parlour claims that these ice-creams and the sculpture are in the scale 1:30. What is the height of the ice-creams served?

Solution

Given, height of ice-cream sculpture = 360 cm
Scale used for ice-cream and sculpture = 1:30
The height of the ice-creams served = Scale {tex}\times{/tex} Actual size {tex}\left[\because \text { scale }=\frac{\text { size drawn }}{\text { actual size }}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1 \times 360}{30}{/tex} = 12 cm
Hence, the height of the ice-cream served is 12 cm.

Q 6

Amount of region occupied by a solid is called its surface area.

Q 7

160 m3 of water is to be used to irrigate a rectangular field whose area is 800 m2. What will be the height of the water level in the field?

Solution

We have, Volume of water = 160 m3
Area of rectangular field = 800 m2
Let h be the height of the water level in the field.
Now, the volume of water = volume of cuboid formed on the field by water.
160 = Area of base × height
= 800 {tex}\times{/tex} h
or h = {tex}\frac{160}{800}{/tex} = 0.2
So, required height = 0.2 m

Q 8

Find the area of a rhombus whose one side measures 5 cm and one diagonal as 8 cm.

Solution

Let ABCD be the rhombus as shown below.

DO = OB = 4 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors of each other
Now in {tex}\triangle \mathrm{AOB}{/tex},
AO2 + OB2 = AB2 [using Pythagoras theorem]
AO = {tex}\sqrt{\mathrm{AB}^{2}-\mathrm{OB}^{2}}=\sqrt{5^{2}-4^{2}}{/tex} = 3 cm
So, AC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} AO 
AC = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6 cm
Thus, the area of the rhombus = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times d_{1} \times d_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 6{/tex}
= 24 cm2

Q 9

The parallel sides of a trapezium are 40 cm and 20 cm. If its non-parallel sides are both equal, each being 26 cm, find the area of the trapezium.

Solution

Let ABCD be the trapezium such that AB = 40 cm, CD = 20 cm
and AD = BC = 26 cm.

Now, draw CL || AD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ALCD is a parallelogram
So, AL = CD = 20 cm and CL = AD = 26 cm.
In {tex}∆{/tex}CLB, we have
CL = CB = 26 cm
Therefore, {tex}∆{/tex} CLB is an isosceles triangle.
Draw altitude CM of {tex}∆{/tex}CLB.
Since, {tex}∆{/tex} CLB is an isosceles triangle.
So, CM is also the median.
Then, LM = MB
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} B L=\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} 20 cm
= 10 cm [{tex}\because{/tex}BL = AB – AL = (40 – 20) cm = 20 cm]
Now, Applying Pythagoras theorem in {tex}∆{/tex}CLM, we have
CL2 = CM2 + LM2
262 = CM2 + 102
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}CM2 = 262 – 102
= (26 – 10) (26 + 10)
= 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 36
= 576
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}CM = {tex}\sqrt{576}{/tex}
{tex}=24 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Therefore, the area of the trapezium = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(sum of parallel sides) {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (20 + 40) {tex}\times{/tex} 24
= 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 24
= 720 cm2.

Q 10

Find the area of polygon ABCDEF, if AD = 18cm, AQ = 14 cm, AP = 12 cm, AN = 8 cm, AM = 4 cm, and FM, EP, QC and BN are perpendiculars to diagonal AD.

Solution

We have given,

In the figure
MP = AP – AM = (12 – 4) cm = 8 cm
PD = AD – AP = (18 – 12) cm = 6 cm
NQ = AQ – AN = (14 – 8) cm = 6 cm
QD = AD – AQ = (18 – 14) cm = 4 cm
Therfore,
Area of the polygon ABCDEF = area of {tex}∆{/tex}AFM + area of trapezium FMPE + area of {tex}∆{/tex}EPD + area of {tex}∆{/tex}ANB + area of trapezium NBCQ + area of {tex}∆{/tex}QCD
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} AM {tex}\times{/tex} FM + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}(FM + EP) {tex}\times{/tex} MP + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} PD {tex}\times{/tex} EP + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} AN {tex}\times{/tex} NB + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}(NB + CQ) {tex}\times{/tex} NQ + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}  QD {tex}\times{/tex} CQ
=  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 +  {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} (5 + 6) {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}(5 + 4) {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
= 10 +  44 + 18 + 20 + 27 + 8
= 127 cm2

200 cm2

= 200cm2
Again, for cylinder B, {tex}r=\frac{5}{\pi}{/tex} cm and h = 20cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Curved surface area of B =  {tex}2 \pi rh=2 \pi \times \frac{5}{\pi} \times20{/tex} 
= 200cm2
Therefore, the curved surface area of both the cylinders is same.

= 48 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= Breadth = 8 m
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ACD, {tex}\angle{/tex}D = 90° [{tex}\because{/tex} in a rectangle, all angles are of 90°]
So, it is a right-angled triangle.
By using Pythagoras theorem, we have
(AC)2 = (AD)2 + (DC)2 
(AC)2 = (6)2 + (8)2
(AC)2 = 36 + 64
AC = {tex}\sqrt{100}{/tex}
AC = 10m
Time taken by lady to cross the field diagonally at rate of 20 m/min = {tex}\frac{Distance}{Speed}=\frac{10}{20}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} min or 30 s

Q 63

The circumference of the front wheel of a cart is 3 m long and that of the back wheel is 4 m long. What is the distance traveled by the cart, when the front wheel makes five more revolutions than the rear wheel?

Solution

Given, circumference of front wheel = 3 m
Now, distance covered by front wheel of the cart in 1 revolution = Circumference of front wheel
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance covered by front wheel in 5 revolutions = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 15 m
Hence, the distance covered by the cart is 15 m.

Q 64

Four horses are tethered with equal ropes at 4 corners of a square field of side 70 m so that they just can reach one another. Find the area left ungrazed by the horses.

Solution

Given, side of a square = 70 m Also, four horses are tethered with equal ropes at 4 corners of the square field.
Hence, each horse can graze upto 35 m of distance along the side.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the square field = Side {tex}\times{/tex} Side
= 70 {tex}\times{/tex} 70
= 4900 m2
The grazed area is making a complete circle by taking all the four grazed parts. So, area of grazed part = {tex}\pi r^2{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 35 \times 35{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} r = 35 m]
= 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 35
= 3850 m2
Area left ungrazed by the horses = Area of square field - Area of grazed part
= 4900 - 3850
= 1050m2

Q 65

The walls and ceiling of a room are to be plastered. The length, breadth and height of the room are 4.5 m, 3m and 350 cm, respectively. Find the cost of plastering at the rate of Rs. 8 per m2

Solution

Given, length of the room (l) = 4.5 m
Breadth of the room (b) = 3 m
Height of the room (h) = 350 cm = 3.5 m [{tex}\because{/tex} 100 cm = 1 m]
and the cost of plastering = ₹ 8 per m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the walls = 2h (l + b)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 (4.5 + 3)
= 7 {tex}\times{/tex} (7.5)= 52 .5 m2
Area of the ceiling = lb = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 13.5 m2
Area of the room = 52.5 + 13.5 = 66 m2
Hence, the cost of plastering = 66 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = ₹ 528

Q 66

Most of the sailboats have two sails, the jib and the mainsail. Assume that the sails are triangles. Find the total area of each sail of the sailboats to the nearest tenth.

Solution

We have,
In the sailboat (i),
Area of a triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \text { Base } \times \text { Height }{/tex}

AC = Base
= 22 + 20
= 42 m
BD = Height
= 22.3 m

{tex}\therefore \text { Area of } \Delta \mathrm{ABC}=\frac{1}{2} \times 42 \times 22.3{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{9366}{2}=4683 \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=4683 \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex}
In another triangular part,
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ACE,
EF = Height
= 16.8 m
AC = Base
= 22 + 20
= 42 m

{tex}\therefore \text { Area of } \Delta A C E=\frac{1}{2} \times 42 \times 168{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7056}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=352.8 \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex}
Area of sailboat (i) = 468.3 + 352.8
= 821.1 m2
In sailboat (ii),
Area of a triangle = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \text { Base } \times \text { Height }{/tex}
In {tex}\Delta{/tex}ABC, {tex}\angle B=90^{\circ}{/tex},

and height (AB) = 19.5 m

{tex}=\frac{212.55}{2}{/tex}

In another triangular part,

= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 239 \times 86{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{20554}{2}{/tex}
= {tex}102.77 \mathrm{m}^{2}{/tex}
therefore, Area of sailboat (ii) = 106.275 + 102.77

In sailboat (iii),

AB = 8.9 m
and BC = 3 m.

Q 67

The area of a trapezium with equal non-parallel sides is 168 m2. If the lengths of the parallel sides are 36 m and 20 m, then find the length of the non-parallel sides.

Solution

Length of the parallel sides are 36 m and 20 m.
Area of a trapezium = 168 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a trapezium = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times[\text { Sum of parallel sides }] \times \text { Height }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 168=\frac{1}{2} \times[36+20] \times \text { Height }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \text { Height }=\frac{168 \times 2}{56}=\frac{336}{56}=6 \mathrm{m}{/tex}

In {tex}\triangle{/tex}ACB, using Pythagoras theorem, (AB)2 = (BC)2 + (AC)2
= (AB)2 = (8)2 + (6)2 
= (AB)2 = 64 + 36
= (AB)2 = 100
{tex}\therefore \quad A B=\sqrt{100}{/tex} = 10 m
Hence, length of the non-parallel side is 10 m.

Q 68

Mukesh walks around a circular track of radius 14 m with a speed of 4 km/h. If he takes 20 rounds of the track, for how long does he walk.

Solution

Radius of the circular track = 14 m
Circumference of the circular track = {tex}2 \pi r=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14{/tex}
= 44 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 88 m
Total distance cover in 20 rounds = 88 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1760 m
Speed of Mukesh on the circular track = 4 km/h
= {tex}\frac{4 \times 1000}{60}=\frac{2 \times 100}{3}=\frac{200}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
Time taken by Mukesh = {tex}\frac{1760}{200 / 3}=\frac{1760 \times 3}{200}=\frac{176 \times 3}{20}=26.4 \mathrm{min}{/tex}
26.4 min

Q 69

The areas of two circles are in the ratio 49 : 64. Find the ratio of their circumferences.

Solution

Given, the area of two circles are in the ratio 49 : 64.
Area of a circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
Let area of the first circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r12
and area of the second circle = {tex}\pi{/tex}r22
According to the question,{tex}\frac{49}{64}=\frac{\pi r_{1}^{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{49}{64}=\frac{r^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(7)^{2}}{(8)^{2}}=\frac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{7}{8}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{r_{1}}{r_2}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r_{1}=7 \text { and } r_{2}=8{/tex}
The ratio of circumferences of these two circles
= {tex}\frac{2 \pi r_{1}}{2 \pi r_{2}}=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{7}{8}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}circumference of circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r]
Hence, the required ratio is 7 : 8.

Q 70

There is a circular pond and a footpath runs along its boundary. A person walks around it, exactly once keeping close to the edge. If his step is 66 cm long and he takes exactly 400 steps to go around the pond, then find the diameter of the pond.

Solution

Let the radius of the pond be r. Then, diameter of the pond, d = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} r [{tex}\because{/tex}diameter = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} radius]
Since, a person takes exactly 400 steps with 66 cm long each step to go round the pond.

Hence, the circumference of the pond = 66 {tex}\times{/tex} 400
= 26400cm
= {tex}\frac{26400}{100} \mathrm{m}{/tex} = 264 m [{tex}\because{/tex} 100 c m = 1 m]
We know that, circumference of a circle is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r=264{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{264}{2} \times \frac{7}{22}=\frac{264 \times 7}{44}{/tex} = 42m
Hence, radius of the pond = 42 m
So, diameter of the pond = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 = 84 m

Q 71

A running track has 2 semi-circular ends of radius 63 m and two straight lengths. The perimeter of the track is 1000 m. Find each straight length.

Solution

Radius of semi-circular track = 63 m
Perimeter of 2 semi-circles = Perimeter of 1 circle
Perimeter of a circular track = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
Perimeter of circular track = {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 63{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 9
= 44 {tex}\times{/tex} 9
= 396 m
{tex}\because{/tex} The perimeter of the total track is 1000 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Length of two straight lengths track = 1000 - 396 = 604 m
Length of 1 straight length track = {tex}\frac{604}{2}=302 \mathrm{m}{/tex}

Q 72

Find the perimeter of the given figure.

Solution

Radius of the given figure = 6.3 m
Two sections in figure form a semi-circle.
Perimeter of semi-circular figure = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{2}+2 r=\pi r+2 r{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 6.3+2 \times 6.3{/tex}
= {tex}22 \times 0.9+2 \times 6.3{/tex}
= = 19.8+ 12.6 = 32.4 m

Q 73

A bicycle wheel makes 500 revolutions in moving 1 km. Find the diameter of the wheel.

Solution

A bicycle wheel makes 500 revolutions in moving 1 km.
In 1 revolution, the bicycle wheel covers = {tex}\frac{1}{500} \mathrm{km}=\frac{1000}{500} \mathrm{m}{/tex} = 2m [{tex}\because{/tex}1 km = 1000 m]
1 revolution distance = Circumference(Perimeter) of the wheel
= 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times r=2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{2 \times 7}{2 \times 22}=\frac{7}{22}{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex}  circumference of a circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Diameter (d) = {tex}2 r=\frac{7}{22} \times 2=\frac{7}{11}{/tex} = 0.636m

Q 74

A boy is cycling such that the wheels of the cycle are making 140 revolutions per hour. If the diameter of the wheel is 60 cm, then calculate the speed (in km/h) with which the boy is cycling.

Solution

The cycle makes 140 revolutions per hour.
Diameter of the wheel = 60 cm
Radius of the wheel = 30 cm
Circumference of a circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
= {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 30{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{44 \times 30}{7}{/tex}
= 188.57 cm
Distance cover in 140 revolutions
= 140 {tex}\times{/tex} 188.57
= 26400 cm
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { Speed }=\frac{26400}{100000} \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h}{/tex}
= 0.264 km/h [{tex}\because{/tex}1 km = 100000 cm]

Find the length of the largest pole, that can be placed in a room of dimensions 12 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4m {tex}\times{/tex} 3m.

AF = {tex}\sqrt{144+16+9}{/tex}

 {tex}\because{/tex} Volume of each of the given figure = Base area {tex}\times{/tex} Height

Q 87

A cube of side 5 cm is cut into as many 1 cm cubes as possible. What is the ratio of the surface areas of the original cube to that of the sum of the surface areas of the small cubes?

Solution

As we know that the volume of the cube is equal to the side cube.
So the volume V1 of the bigger cube is (5)3 = 125 cm3
Now we cut a 1 cm cube from the 5 cm cube so the volume (V2) of the smaller cube = (1)3  = 1 cm3.
Now let there are (n) such possible cubes.
So n times the volume of a smaller cube is equal to the volume of a bigger cube.
{tex}\Rightarrow n V_{2}=V_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 125
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 125
So there are 125 such cubes possible.
Now, the surface area of the bigger cube is {tex}(S . A)_{1}=6(5)^{2} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}{/tex}
And the surface area of the smaller cube is (S.A)2 = 6(1)2 = 6 cm2
So the surface area of 125 smaller cubes =(S.A)3 = 125 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 cm2
So the ratio of the surface area of the larger cube to that of the sum of the surface areas of the smaller cubes is
{tex}\frac{(S . A)_{1}}{(S . A)_{3}}=\frac{6(5)^{2}}{125 \times 6}=\frac{25}{125}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
So this is the required ratio.

Q 88

A square sheet of paper is converted into a cylinder by rolling it along its side. What is the ratio of the base radius to the side of the square?

Solution

Let the sides of a square paper be a.

A cylinder is formed by rolling the paper along its side.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Base of the cylinder is circle, so the circumference of the circle is equal to the length of each side of the square sheet.
{tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi r = a{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} circumference of circle = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r]
{tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{a}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio = {tex}\frac{a}{2 \pi}: a=\frac{1}{2 \pi}: 1=1: 2 \pi{/tex}
Hence, the ratio of the base radius to the side of the square is 1 : 2{tex}\pi{/tex}

Q 89

How many cubic meters of Earth must be dug to construct a well 7 m deep and of diameter 2.8 m

Solution

A well is in the form of cylindrical form. Earth must be dug to construct a well 7 m deep and diameter 2.8 m is equal to the volume of a cylinder with 7 m height and diameter 2.8 m. Volume of a cylinder = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2h
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{2.8}{2} \times \frac{2.8}{2} \times 7=11 \times 2.8 \times 1.4{/tex} = 43.12 cm2

Q 90

The radius and height of cylinder are in the ratio of 3:2 and its volume is 19,404 cm3. Find the radius and height.

Solution

Let the radius be 3x  and height be 2x.
Volume of cylinder {tex}=\pi {r^2}h{/tex}
19404 = {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex}(3x)(3x)(2x)
19404 = {tex}\frac{(66x)(6x^2)}{7}{/tex}

19404{tex}\times 7{/tex} = 396x3
x3 = 343
{tex}x = \sqrt[3]{{343}}{/tex}
x = 7cm
Therefore,
Radius = 3x = 3(7) = 21cm
Height = 2x = 2(7) = 14cm

Q 91

The thickness of a hollow metallic cylinder is 2 cm. It is 70 cm long with an outer radius of 14 cm. Find the volume of the metal used in making the cylinder, assuming that it is open at both ends. Also, find its weight if the metal weighs 8 g per cm3.

Solution

The thickness of the hollow metallic cylinder is 2 cm.
Height of the cylinder = 70 cm
Outer radius of the cylinder = 14 cm
Inner radius of the cylinder = 14 - 2 = 12 cm

Volume of the metal used in making the cylinder = Volume of the hollow cylinder
= {tex}\pi\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) \times h{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times\left[(14)^{2}-(12)^{2}\right] \times 70{/tex}
= 22 {tex}\times{/tex} [196 - 144] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 52 {tex}\times{/tex} 10
= 11440 cm3
Weight of 11440 cm3, if metal is 8 g per cm3 = 11440 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 91520 g

Q 92

Radius of a cylinder is r and the height is h. Find the change in the volume if the

  1. height is doubled.
  2. height is doubled and the radius is halved.
  3. height remains same and the radius is halved.
Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} We know that Volum e of a cylinder = {tex}\pi r^{2} h{/tex}
where, h is height and r is the radius of the base of the cylinder.

  1. If height is double i.e. h = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} h
    = 2h
    Then, its volum e = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 h
    = {tex}2\pi r^{2} h{/tex}
    Hence, volume becames double of original volume.
  2. If height is doubled and the radius is halved,
    i.e. h = 2h and r = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \text { Volume }=\pi \times\left(\frac{r}{2}\right) \times\left(\frac{r}{2}\right) \times 2 h{/tex}
    {tex}=\pi \times \frac{r^{2}}{4} \times 2 h{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\pi r^{2} h}{2}{/tex}
    Hence, volume becames half of the original volume.
  3. If height remains same and radius is halved,
    i.e. h = h and r = {tex}\frac{r}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \text { Volume }=\pi \times \frac{r}{2} \times \frac{r}{2} \times h{/tex}
    {tex}\text{Volume}=\pi \times \frac{r^{2}}{4} \times h{/tex}
    Hence, volume becames {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}th of the original volume.
Q 93

If the length of each edge of a cube is tripled, what will be the change in its volume?

Solution

Let the edge of a cube be a
If edge of the cube became tripled i.e. a' = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} a = 3a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cube = a3
{tex}\because{/tex} Volume of the cube with edge tripled = (a')3 = (3a)3 = 27a3
Hence, the volume is 27 times of the original volume.

Q 94

A carpenter makes a box that has a volume of 13400 cm3. The base has an area of 670 cm2. What is the height of the box?

Solution

Let the height of the box be h.
Volume of the box = 13400 cm2
Area of base of the box = 670 cm2
{tex}\because{/tex} Volume of a box = Area of base {tex}\times{/tex} Height
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 13400 = 670 {tex}\times{/tex} h
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{13400}{670}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{1340}{67}=20 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Hence, the height of the box is 20 cm

Q 95

A cuboidal tin box opened at the top has dimensions 20 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 16 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 14 cm. What is the total area of metal sheet required to make 10 such boxes?

Solution

Dimensions of the cuboidal tin box are 20 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 16 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 14 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of metal sheet for 1 box = Surface area of cuboid
= 2 (lb + bh + hl)
= 2 (20 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 + 16 {tex}\times{/tex} 14 + 14 {tex}\times{/tex} 20)
= 2(320 + 224 + 280)
= 2(824) = 1648cm2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of metal sheet required to make 10 such boxes = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 1648= 16480 cm2

Q 96

Find the capacity of the water tank, in liters, whose dimensions are 4.2 m, 3 m and 1.8 m?

Solution

It is given that the dimensions of the water tank are 4.2 m, 3 m and 1.8 m
Capacity of water tank = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth {tex}\times{/tex} Height
= 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.8 = 22{tex}\cdot{/tex}68 m3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacity of the water tank in litres = 22.68 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 22680 L [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m3 = 1000 L]

Q 97

How many cubes each of side 0.5 cm are required to build a cube of volume 8 cm3?

Solution

The volume of a cube = (Side)3
{tex}\because{/tex} Side of cube = 0.5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of the cube = (0.5)3 = 0.125 cm3
Therefore the number of cubes required to make volume of 8 cm3 
{tex}=\frac{8}{0.125}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8000}{125}{/tex} = 64 cubes

Q 98

A wooden box (including the lid) has external dimensions 40 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 34 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 30 cm. If the wood is 1 cm thick, how many cm3 of wood is used in it?

Solution

External dimensions of wooden box are 40 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 34 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 30 cm.

Since the wood is 1 cm thick, it means the internal dimensions will be (40 - 2) cm {tex}\times{/tex} (34 - 2)cm {tex}\times{/tex} (30 - 2)cm = 38 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 32 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 28 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Wood used for the box = Volume of the wooden box with external dimensions - Volume of the wooden box with internal dimensions
= 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 34 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 - 38 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 {tex}\times{/tex} 28
= 40800 - 34048
= 6752 cm3

Q 99

A river 2 m deep and 45 m wide is flowing at the rate of 3 km per hour. Find the amount of water in cubic meters that run into the sea per minute.

Solution

Depth of the river = 2 m
Width of the river = 45 m
Flowing rate of the water = 3 km /h
{tex}=3 \times \frac{1000}{60}=\frac{3000}{60}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}1 km = 1000 m and 1 h = 60 min]
= {tex}\frac{300}{6}=50 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
The amount of water into sea per minute
= Depth {tex}\times{/tex} Width {tex}\times{/tex} Length of water of 1 min
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 {tex}\times{/tex} 50
= 4500 m3/m

Q 100

Find the area to be painted in the following block with a circular hole. Given that, the length is 15cm, width 12cm, height 20cm, and radius of the hole 2.8 cm.

Solution

We have given that,
Length = 15 cm
Width = 12 cm
Height = 20 cm
Radius of the hole = 2.8 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The area to be painted = Surface area of the figure - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} Area of the circular hole
= 2 (lb + bh + lh) - {tex}2 \pi r^{2}{/tex}
= 2 (15 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 + 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 20) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{22}{7}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.8
= 2(180 + 240 + 300) - {tex}\frac{44 \times 2.8 \times 2.8}{7}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 720 - 49.28 = 1440 - 49.28
= 1390.72 cm2

Q 101

A truck carrying 7.8 m3 concrete arrives at a job site. A platform of width 5 m and height 2 m is being constructed at the site. Find the length of the platform, constructed from the amount of concrete on the truck?

Solution

Total volume of concrete = 7.8 m3 Width of the platform = 5 m
Height of the platform = 2 m
Let the length of the platform = xm
According to the question, Volume of concrete = Volume used to make platform 7.8 m3
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7.8 = 10x [{tex}\because{/tex} volume = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth {tex}\times{/tex} height]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x = 7.8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.78 m
Hence, the length of the platform is 0.78 m

Q 102

A hollow garden roller of 42 cm diameter and length 152 cm is made of cast iron 2 cm thick. Find the volume of iron used in the roller.

Solution

Diameter of the hollow garden roller = 42 cm

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Inner radius = {tex}\frac{42}{2}{/tex} = 21 cm
{tex}\because{/tex} Thickness of cast iron = 2 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Outer radius = 21 + 2 = 23 cm
Volume of hollow cylinder = {tex}\pi{/tex} (R2 - r2) {tex}\times{/tex} h [where, R is outer-radius and r is inner-radius]
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times\left[(23)^{2}-(21)^{2}\right] \times 152{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times(529-441) \times 152{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 88 \times 152=\frac{22 \times 88 \times 152}{7}=\frac{294272}{7}{/tex}
= 42038.85 cm3

Q 103

Three cubes each of side 10 cm are joined end to end. Find the surface area of the resultant figure.

Solution

If three cubes each of side 10 cm are joined, then a cuboid will be formed of dimensions 30 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10cm

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Surface area of the cuboid = 2 [lb + bh + hl]
= 2 [30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 + 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10]
= 2 [300 + 100 + 300] = 2 [700] = 1400 cm2

Q 104

Below are the drawings of cross-sections of two different pipes used to fill swimming pools. Figure A is a combination of 2 pipes each having a radius of 8 cm. Figure B is a pipe having a radius of 15 cm. If the force of the flow of water coming out of the pipes is the same in both the cases, which will fill the swimming pool faster?

Solution

In figure A, 2 pipes each having a radius of 8 cm.
{tex}\because{/tex} Area of a circle = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of one pipe = {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 8 \times 8{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22 \times 8 \times 8}{7}=\frac{22 \times 64}{7} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Area of 2 pipes = {tex}\frac{2 \times 1408}{7}=\frac{2816}{7} \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex} = 402.28 cm2
figure B, a pipe having radius of 15 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the pipe = {tex}\pi r^{2}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 15 \times 15=\frac{22}{7} \times 225{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{4950}{7}=707.14 \mathrm{cm}^{2}{/tex}
Clearly, the surface area of pipe B is greater. So, pipe B fill the swimming pool faster.

Q 105

A swimming pool is 200 m {tex}\times{/tex} 50 m and has an average depth of 2 m. By the end of the summer day, the water level drops by 2 cm. How many cubic meters of water are lost at the end of the day?

Solution

Dimensions of swimming pool are 200 m {tex}\times{/tex} 50 m.
Average depth of the swimming pool = 2 m
At the end of summer day the water level drops by 2 cm.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of water in swimming pool = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth {tex}\times{/tex} Depth = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20000 m3
If water level drops by 2 cm, it means new level of water
= {tex}\left(2-\frac{2}{100}\right) m=1.98 m \quad\left[\because 1 \mathrm{cm}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{m}\right]{/tex}
Volume of water after summer day = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.98 = 19800 m3
So, water in cubic metres was lost on that day = Initial volume - Volume after summer day
= 20000 -19800 = 200 m3

Q 106

A housing society consisting of 5500 people, needs 100 L of water per person per day. The cylindrical supply tank is 7 m high and has a diameter of 10 m. For how many days will the water in the tank last for the society?

Solution

Total number of people = 5500
Water required per person per day = 100 L
Total requirement of water by 5500 people = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 5500 = 550000 L
Height of the cylindrical tank = 7m
Diameter of the cylindrical tank = 10 m
Radius = 5 m {tex}\left[\because \frac{\text { diameter }}{2}=\text { radius }\right]{/tex}
Volume of cylinder = {tex}\pi r^{2} h=\frac{22}{7} \times 5 \times 5 \times 7{/tex}
= 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 25 = 550 m3
diameter = radius = 550 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000= 550000 L [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m3 = 1000 L]
Hence, for 1 day the water in the tank lost for the society and in one day society needs 550000 L of water.

Q 107

Metallic discs of radius 0.75 cm and thickness 0.2 cm are melted to obtain 508.68 cm3 of metal. Find the number of discs to be melted. (use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

We have given that,
Radius of metallic disc = 0.75 cm
Thickness of disc = 0.2 cm i.e. h = 0.2 cm
The total volume of material which will be used in forming by melting of disc = 50868 cm3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Material required for one disc = Volume of cylinder  [{tex}\because{/tex}the shape of a disc is a cylinder] 
{tex}=\pi r^{2} h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{22}{7} \times 0.75 \times 0.75 \times 0.2{/tex}
= 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.75 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2
= 0.35325 cm3
Number of discs can be melted = {tex}\mathrm{Total\space volume\space of \space metal \space obtained \space after \space melting}\over\mathrm{Volume \space of\space one\space disc}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{50868}{0.35325}{/tex} 
= 1440 discs

Q 108

The ratio of the radius and height of a cylinder is 2 : 3. If its volume is 12936 cm3, find the total surface area of the cylinder.

Solution

The ratio of the radius and height of a cylinder = 2 : 3
Let the radius of the cylinder be 2x and the height of the cylinder be 3x.
Volume of the cylinder = 12936 cm3
Volume of a cylinder = {tex}\pi r^{2} h{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad 12936=\frac{22}{7} \times(2 x)^{2} \times 3 x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 12936=\frac{22}{7} \times 4 x^{2} \times 3 x{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 12936=\frac{264}{7} x^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{3}=\frac{12936 \times 7}{264}=49 \times 7{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{3}=7 \times 7 \times 7=(7)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{3}=(7)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  x = 7
So, radius = 2x = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 14 cm and height = 3x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 21 cm
The total surface area of the cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r(r + h)
= {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 14(14+21){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{44 \times 14}{7} \times 35=44 \times 14 \times5{/tex} = 3080 cm2

Q 109

External dimensions of a closed wooden box are in the ratio 5:4:3. If the cost of painting its outer surface at the rate of Rs. 5 per dm2 is Rs. 11750, find the dimensions of the box.

Solution

We have,
External dimensions of a closed wooden box are in the ratio 5 : 4 : 3.
Let the external dimensions of the closed wooden box be 5x, 4x and 3x.
The cost of painting = Rs. 5 per dm2
The total cost of painting = Rs. 11750
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total surface area = {tex}\frac{\text { Total cost of painting }}{\text { cost of painting per } \mathrm{dm}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{11750}{5}{/tex} 
= 2350 cm2
Total surface area of a cuboid = 2 (lb + bh + hl)
= 2(5x {tex}\times{/tex} 4x + 4x {tex}\times{/tex} 3x + 3x {tex}\times{/tex} 5x)
=2(20x2 + 12x2 + 15x2)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 47x2 = 94x2
Since, total surface area = 2350 dm2
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 94 x^{2}=2350{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x^{2}=\frac{2350}{94}{/tex}= 25
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  x = 5
Therefore, dimensions of the box are 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 25 dm,
4 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 20 dm
and 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 15 dm

Q 110

The capacity of a closed cylindrical vessel of height 1 m is 15.4 L. How many square metres of metal sheet would be needed to make it?

Solution

Height of cylindrical vessel = 1 m
Capacity of the cylindrical vessel = 15.4 L
In metre cube = {tex}\frac{15.4}{1000}=.0154 \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m3 = 1000L]
Volume of a cylinder = {tex}\pi r^2h{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{22}{7} \times r^{2} \times 1{/tex} = 0.0154
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r^{2}=\frac{0.0154}{3.14}{/tex} = 0.0049
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r=\sqrt{0.0049}{/tex} = 0.07
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Metal of sheet required = {tex}2 \pi r h=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 0.07 \times 1{/tex} = 0.4396 = 0.44 m2

Q 111

What will happen to the volume of the cube, if its edge

  1. Tripled
  2. Reduced to one-fourth?
Solution

Let each side of the cube be a, then its volume = a3 [{tex}\because{/tex} volume of a cube = (side)3]

  1. If side became triple, then volume will be = (3a)3 = 27 a3
    Hence, the new volume of the cube will be 27 times of original volume of the cube.
  2. If side reduced to one fourth = {tex}a \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{a}{4}{/tex}
    Now, its volume = {tex}\left(\frac{a}{4}\right)^{3}=\frac{a^{3}}{64}{/tex}
    Hence, new volume {tex}\frac{1}{64}{/tex} times of original volume.
Q 112

A rectangular sheet of dimensions 25 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 7 cm is rotated about its longer side. Find the volume and the whole surface area of the solid thus generated.

Solution

A rectangular sheet of dimensions 25 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 7 cm is rotated about its longer side which makes a cylinder with base 25 cm /and height 7 cm.

Surface area of a base = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore \quad 2 \pi r=25 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{25 \times 7}{2 \times 22}=\frac{175}{44} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}

Volume of a cylinder = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2h
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{175}{44} \times \frac{175}{44} \times 7{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{175 \times 175}{2 \times 44}=\frac{30625}{88}{/tex}
= 348.011 cm3
Surface area = {tex}2 \pi r h=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times \frac{175}{44} \times 7{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{44}{44} \times 175{/tex}
= 175 cm2

Q 113

From a pipe of inner radius 0.75 cm, water flows at the rate of 7 m per second. Find the volume in litres of water delivered by the pipe in 1 h. [{tex}\therefore{/tex}1 cm = {tex}\frac{1}{100} m{/tex}]

Solution

We have, Radius of pipe = r = 0 .75 cm
= 0.0075 m {tex}\left[\because 1 \mathrm{cm}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{m}\right]{/tex}
The rate of water flow = 7 m /s
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of water in 1 sec = 7 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of water flow in 1 hour = {tex}60 \times 60 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 0.0075 \times 0.0075 \times 7{/tex}
= 3600 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.00005625 {tex}\times{/tex} 7
= 11304 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.00005625
= 79128 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.00005625
= 4.45095 m3
= 4.45000 m3
= 4450000 cm3
=  4450 L [{tex}\because{/tex}1000cm3 = 1L]

Q 114

Four times the area of the curved surface of a cylinder is equal to 6 times the sum of the areas of its bases. If its height is 12 cm, find its curved surface area.

Solution

Let the radius and height of the cylinder be r and h, respectively.
Curved surface area of cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh
Area of base = {tex}\pi{/tex}r2
Sum of areas of bases = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
According to the question, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Curved surface area = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} Sum of areas of bases
4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r2
= 8{tex}\pi{/tex}rh = 12{tex}\pi{/tex}r2 
= 2h = 3r
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{2}{3} h{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{2}{3} \times 12{/tex} = 8cm [{tex}\because{/tex} h = 12 cm, given]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Curved surface area of the cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh
= {tex}2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 8 \times 12=\frac{44 \times 8 \times 12}{7}{/tex}
= 603.428 cm2

Q 115

A cylindrical tank has a radius of 154 cm. It is filled with water to a height of 3 m. If water to a height of 4.5 m is poured into it, what will be the increase in the volume of water in kL?

Solution

Radius of cylindrical tank = 154 cm
Initial height of water tank = 3m = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 cm [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m = 100 cm]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Volume of water = {tex}\pi r^{2} h=\frac{22}{7} \times 154 \times 154 \times 3 \times 100{/tex}
= 22360800 cm2
If height of water 4.5 m is poured into it, then volume of water
= {tex}\frac{22}{7} \times 154 \times 154 \times 4.5 \times 100{/tex}
= 33541200 cm3
Increase in volume = 33541200 - 22360800
= 11180400 cm3
= 11180.4 L [1000 cm3 = 1L]
= 11.1804 kL [1 kL = 1000 L]

Q 116

The length, breadth, and height of a cuboidal reservoir are 7m, 6m and 15 m respectively. 8400 L of water is pumped out from the reservoir. Find a fall in the water level in the reservoir.

Solution

Length of a cuboidal reservoir = 7 m
Breadth of a cuboidal reservoir = 6 m
Height of a cuboidal reservoir = 15 m
Capacity of cuboidal reservoir = l {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h =7 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 630 m3
If 8400 L of water is pumped out.
In meter cubic = {tex}\frac{8400}{1000}=8.4 \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex}
Fall in water level = 630 - 8.4= 621.6 m3
= 621.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 [{tex}\because{/tex} 1m3 = 1000 L)
= 621600 L

Q 117

How many bricks of size 22 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 7 cm are required to construct a wall 11 m long, 3.5 m high and 40 cm thick if the cement and sand used in the construction occupy (1/10)th part of the wall?

Solution

Volume of each brick = 22 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 7 cm
= 1540 cm3 = 0.00154 m3
Volume of wall = l {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h = 11 m {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 m {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{40}{100} m{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 1 m = 100 cm]
= 11 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.4 = 15.4 m3
If 1/10th part of the wall used in cement and sand, then part of wall used by cement and sand  = {tex}\frac{15.4}{10} m^{3}=1.54 m^{3}{/tex}
Remaining part = 15.4 - 1.54 = 13.86 m3
Number of bricks {tex}=\frac{\text { Volume of wall to be construct }}{\text { Volume of each brick }}=\frac{13.86}{0.00154}{/tex} = 9000

Q 118

A rectangular examination hall having seats for 500 candidates has to be built so, as to allow 4 cubic metres of air and 0.5 square metres of floor area per candidate. If the length of hall is 25 m, find the height and breadth of the hall.

Solution

Total number of seats in rectangular examination hall = 500
Length of the hall = 25 m
Cubic metres of air for per candidates = 4 m3
Square metres of floor area for per candidate = 0.5 m2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Height of the hall = {tex}\frac{\text { Volume of air per candidate }}{\text { Square metre of floor area for one candidate }}=\frac{4}{0.5}=\frac{40}{5}{/tex} = 8 m
Total capacity of the hall = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 2000 m3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Breadth of the hall = {tex}\frac{2000}{25 \times 8}=\frac{80}{8}{/tex} = 10 m

Q 119

The ratio between the curved surface area and the total surface area of a right circular cylinder is 1 : 2. Find the ratio between the height and radius of the cylinder.

Solution

Curved surface area of a cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}rh
Total surface area of a cylinder = 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r(r+h)
The ratio between the curved surface area and the total surface area of a cylinder is 1 : 2
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2}=\frac{2 \pi r h}{2 \pi r(r+h)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}=\frac{h}{(r+h)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \quad 2 h=(r+h){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 h-h=r \Rightarrow h=r{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ratio of height and radius = 1:1.

Q 120

A birthday cake has two tiers as shown in the figure below. Find the volume of the cake.

Solution

Volume of the cake = Volume of lower cuboid + Volume of upper cuboid
= 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 [{tex}\because{/tex} volume of cuboid = I {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h]
= 6000 + 500 = 6500 cm3

Q 121

Work out the surface area of the shape:(Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

Surface area of the figure = TSA of upper block + TSA of lower block - area of contacted part
Surface area of the figure = 2 [3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1] + 2 [4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1] - [1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1]
= 2 [3 + 1 + 3] + 2[4 + 1 + 4 ] - [1 + 1]
= 2 [7] + 2 [9] - [2]
= 14 + 18 - 2
= 32 - 2
= 30 cm2

Q 122

Work out the surface area of shape: (Use {tex}\pi{/tex} = 3.14)

Solution

In the given figure, there are two cuboids are joined and one face is joined.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total surface area of the given solid = 2 (24 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 24) + 2 (12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 + 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 ) - 2 (12 {tex}\times{/tex}12)
= 1440 + 864 - 288
= 2016 cm2

Q 123

Solution

To find the total surface area, we draw the figure as given below.

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Upper surface area
= 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 18

Q 124

Work out the surface area of the shape: ( Use {tex} \pi=\mathbf{3} . \mathbf{1 4}{/tex})

Solution

From the given figure, it is clear that,
There is a cube of side 5 cm and a cylinder of height 20 cm and radius is 2 cm.
So, total surface area = Surface area of the cube + Curved surface area of cylinder
= 6(5)2 + 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}(2) {tex}\times{/tex} 20  [ {tex}\therefore{/tex} Surface area of cube = 6a2 and Surface area of cylinder = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex}rh]
= 150 + 80{tex}\pi{/tex}
Total surface area = 150 + 251.2 = 401.2 cm2
Note In this question assume that cylinder is at end open.

Q 125

Water flows from a tank with a rectangular base measuring 80 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 70 cm into another tank with a square base of side 60 cm. If the water in the first tank is 45 cm deep, how deep will it be in the second tank?

Solution

Dimensions of the rectangular base tank are 80 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 70 cm
Height of rectangle base tank = 45 cm
Each side of square base tank = 60 cm
Let h be the height of the square base tank.
Volume of rectangular base tank = Volume of a square base tank
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 80 {tex}\times{/tex} 70 {tex}\times{/tex} 45 = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 {tex}\times{/tex} h [{tex}\because{/tex} volume of the tank = volume of cuboid = l {tex}\times{/tex} b {tex}\times{/tex} h]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{80 \times 70 \times 45}{60 \times 60}=h{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} h = 70 cm
Hence, water in the second tank will be 70 cm deep.

Q 126

A rectangular sheet of paper is rolled in two different ways to form two different cylinders. Find the volume of cylinders in each case if the sheet measures 44 cm{tex}\times{/tex} 33 cm.

Solution

In 1st case:
If the sheet rolled along 44 cm

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=44 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{44}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex} r=\frac{44}{2} \times \frac{7}{22}{/tex}
{tex} r=7 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
In 2nd case:
If the sheet rolled along 33 cm

{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \pi r=33 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad r=\frac{33}{2 \pi}{/tex}
{tex}r=\frac{33 \times 7}{2 \times 22}{/tex}
{tex}r=\frac{21}{4} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
Volume in 1st case:
Volume of cylinder = {tex}\pi \times r^{2} \times h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{22}{7} \times 7 \times 7 \times 33{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} r = 7 cm and h = 33 cm]
= 22 {tex}\times{/tex} 33 {tex}\times{/tex} 7
​​​​​= 5082 cm3
Volume in 2nd case:
Volume of cylinder ={tex}\pi r^{2} h{/tex}
{tex}\frac{22}{7} \times \frac{21}{4} \times \frac{21}{4} \times 44{/tex}{tex}\left[\because r=\frac{21}{4} \mathrm{cm} \text { and } h=44 \mathrm{cm}\right]{/tex}
= 3811.5 cm3

Q 8

Simplify {tex} \frac{(-2)^{3} \times(-2)^{7}}{3 \times 4^{6}}{/tex}

Solution

we have {tex}\frac{(-2)^{3} \times(-2)^{7}}{3 \times 4^{6}}=\frac{(-2)^{3+7}}{3 \times\left(2^{2}\right)^{6}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}am {tex}\times{/tex} an = am+n]
{tex}=\frac{(-2)^{10}}{3 \times 2^{12}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2^{10-12}}{3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}am {tex}\div{/tex} an = am–n, (-2)10 = 210]
{tex}=\frac{2^{-2}}{3}=\frac{1}{3 \times 2^{2}}=\frac{1}{12}{/tex}

Q 9

Find the value of x so that (-5)x+1{tex}\times{/tex} (-5)5 = (-5)7

Solution

We have (-5)x+1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-5)5 = (-5)7
(-5)x+1 {tex}\times{/tex} (-5)5 = (-5)7
(-5)x+6 = (-5)7  [am {tex}\times{/tex} an = am+n]
On comparing both sides, if powers have the same base, then their exponents must be equal.
Therefore, x + 6 = 7
x = 7 - 6 = 1
x = 1
