Q 92

Solve: {tex}2^{-2} \times 2^{-3}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}2^{-2} \times 2^{-3}{/tex} = {tex}(2)^{-2-3}=(2)^{-5}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because a^{m} \times a^{n}=(a)^{m+n}\right]{/tex}

Q 93

Solve: {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-2} \div\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-3}{/tex}

Solution

{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-2} \div\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-3}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-2+3}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because a^{m}\div a^{n}=(a)^{m-n}\right]{/tex}

Q 94

Express 3-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3-4 as a power of 3 with positive exponent.

Solution

Using laws of exponents, am {tex}\times{/tex} an = (a)m + n and {tex}a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore \quad 3^{-5} \times 3^{-4}=(3)^{-5-4}=(3)^{-9}=\frac{1}{3^{9}}{/tex}

Q 95

Express 16-2 as a power with the base 2.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 16 = 24
{tex}\therefore \quad 16^{-2}=\left(2^{4}\right)^{-2}=(2)^{4 \times(-2)}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\left(a^{m}\right)^{n}=(a)^{m n}\right]{/tex}
= (2)-8

Q 96

Express {tex}\frac{27}{64}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-27}{64}{/tex} as powers of a rational number.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} 27 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 33, (-27) = (-3) {tex}\times{/tex} (-3) {tex}\times{/tex} (-3) = (-3)3
and 64 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 43
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{27}{64}=\frac{3^{3}}{4^{3}}=\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{3}{/tex} and {tex} \frac{-27}{64}=\frac{(-3)^{3}}{(4)^{3}}=\left(\frac{-3}{4}\right)^{3}{/tex}

Q 97

Express {tex}\frac{16}{81}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-16}{81}{/tex} as powers of a rational number.

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} 16=4 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 42
and 81 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 92
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{16}{81}=\frac{(4)^{2}}{(9)^{2}}=\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)^{2}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{-16}{81}=\frac{-(4)^{2}}{(9)^{2}}=-\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)^{2}{/tex}

Q 98

Express {tex}\left(\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-2}\right)^{-3}{/tex}as a power of a rational number with negative exponent.

Solution

Using laws of exponents, (am)n = (a)m {tex}\times{/tex} n and {tex}a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore\left(\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-2}\right)^{-3}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-2 \times(-3)}=\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{6}=\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{-6}{/tex}

Q 99

Express {tex}\left(2^{5} \div 2^{8}\right) \times 2^{-7}{/tex} as a power of a rational number with a negative exponent.

Solution

Using laws of exponents, am {tex}\div{/tex} an = am - n
and am {tex}\times{/tex} an = am+n [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (25 {tex}\times{/tex}28) {tex}\times{/tex} 2-7 = (25 - 8) {tex}\times{/tex} 2-7 = 2-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-7 = 2-3 - 7 = 2-10

Q 100

Find the product of the cube of (-2) and the square of (+4).

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} Cube of (-2) = (-2)3
and square of (+4) = (+4)2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The product = (-2)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (4)2 = (-8) {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = -128

Q 101

Simplify: {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{-2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-2}+\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{-2}{/tex}

Solution

Using law of exponents, {tex}a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{-2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-2}+\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{-2}{/tex} = (4)2 + (2)2 + (3)2 = 16 + 4 + 9 = 29

Q 102

Simplify: {tex}\left(\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)^{-2}\right)^{3} \times\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{-4} \times 3^{-1} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex}

Solution

Using laws of exponents, (am)n = (a)m {tex}\times{/tex} n, {tex}a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex}, am {tex}\times{/tex}an = am+n and am {tex}\div{/tex} an = am - n [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore\left(\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)^{-2}\right)^{3} \times\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{-4} \times 3^{-1} \times \frac{1}{6}=\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)^{(-2) \times 3} \times(3)^{4} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{-2}{3}\right)^{-6} \times 3^{4} \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{2 \times 3}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 6 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3]
= {tex}\left(\frac{3}{-2}\right)^{6} \times 3^{4} \times \frac{1}{3 \times 2 \times 3}=\frac{(3)^{6}}{(-2)^{6}} \times 3^{4} \times \frac{1}{2^{1} \times 3^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(3)^{6+4}}{(2)^{6+1} \times 3^{2}}{/tex} [(-am) = am, if m is an even number]
= {tex}\frac{(3)^{10}}{2^{7} \times 3^{2}}=\frac{3^{10-2}}{2^{7}}=\frac{3^{8}}{2^{7}}{/tex}

Q 103

Simplify: {tex}\frac{49 \times z^{-3}}{7^{-3} \times 10 \times z^{-5}}(z \neq 0){/tex}

Solution

{tex}\frac{49 \times z^{-3}}{7^{-3} \times 10 \times z^{-5}}=\frac{(7)^{2} \times z^{-3}}{7^{-3} \times 10 \times z^{-5}}=\frac{(7)^{2+3} \times z^{-3+5}}{10}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} am {tex}\div{/tex} an = (a)m - n]
= {tex}\frac{(7)^{5} z^{2}}{10}=\frac{7^{5}}{10} z^{2}{/tex}

Q 104

Solution

Using laws of exponents, am {tex}\div{/tex} an = (a)m - n and am {tex}\times{/tex} an = (a)m+n [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (25 {tex}\div{/tex} 28) {tex}\times{/tex} (2)-7 = (2)5 - 8 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)-7 = (2)-3 {tex}\times{/tex} (2)-7
= (2)-3 - 7 = (2)-10 = {tex}\frac{1}{2^{10}}=\frac{1}{1024} \quad\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}

Q 105

Solution

We have, {tex}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-2} \times\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-14}=\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{8 x}{/tex}
Using law of exponents, am {tex}\times{/tex} an = (a)m + n [{tex}\because{/tex} a is non-zero integer]
Then, {tex}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-2} \times\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-14}=\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{8x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-2-14}=\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{8 x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{-16}=\left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^{8 x}{/tex}
On comparing both sides, we get -16 = 8x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2

Q 106

Q 107

Find the value of x, so that (2-1 + 4-1 + 6-1 + 8-1)x = 1

Solution

We have, (2-1 + 4-1 + 6-1 + 8-1)x = 1

This can be possible only if x = 0, {Since, a0 = 1, (law of exponents)}

Q 111

Q 112

Find the multiplicative inverse of (-7)-2 {tex}\div{/tex} (90)-1

Solution

a is called multiplicative inverse of b, if a {tex}\times{/tex} b = 1
We have, (-7)-2 {tex}\div{/tex} (90)-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{(7)^{2}}\div\frac{1}{(90)^{1}}=\frac{1}{49}\div\frac{1}{90}=\frac{1}{49} \times \frac{90}{1}=\frac{90}{49}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} (-a)m = am, if m is an even number and a-m = {tex}\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex}]
put {tex}b=\frac{90}{49}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \text { a } \times \frac{90}{49}=1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{49}{90}{/tex}

Q 132

By what number should {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-3}{/tex} be divided so that the quotient becomes {tex}\left(\frac{4}{27}\right)^{-2}{/tex}?

so, {tex}\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-3}\div \frac{x}{1}=\left(\frac{4}{27}\right)^{-2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-3}\times \frac{1}{x}=\left(\frac{4}{27}\right)^{-2}{/tex}[using cross multiplication]
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-3}\times\left(\frac{3^{3}}{2^{2}}\right)^{-2}{/tex}
{tex}x=\left(\frac{-3}{2}\right)^{-3}\times\frac{(3)^{-6}}{(2)^{-4}}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{(-3)^{-3} \times(3)^{-6}}{2^{-3} \times 2^{-4}}{/tex}{tex}\left[\because a^{m} \times a^{n}=a^{m+n}\right]{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{-3^{-9}}{2^{-7}}{/tex}
{tex}x=\frac{-2^{7}}{3^{9}} \quad\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}} \right]{/tex}

Q 154

A half-life is the amount of time that it takes for a radioactive substance to decay one-half of its original quantity. Suppose radioactive decay causes 300 grams of a substance to decrease 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-3 grams after 3 half-lives. Evaluate 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-3 to determine how many grams of the substance is left. Explain why the expression 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-n can be used to find the amount of the substance that remains after half-lives.

Solution

A half life is the amount of time that it takes for a radioactive substance to decay to one half of its original quantity. Since, 300 g of a substance decreases to {tex}\frac{300}{2}=300 \times 2^{-1}{/tex} g after one-half life.
So, the amount left after one half-life will be (original weight-decayed weight) = {tex}300-\frac{300}{2}=\frac{300}{2}{/tex} g
So, for 2nd half life its initial weight will be {tex}\frac{300}{2}{/tex} g = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-1 g
So, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{300}{2}=\frac{300}{2^{2}}=300 \times 2^{-2}{/tex} g will be decayed or decreased in 2nd half-life.
So, the amount remaining after two half-life will be (original weight at the start of second half life-decayed weight in second half life) = {tex}\frac{300}{2}-\frac{300}{4}=\frac{300}{4} g=300 \times 2^{-2}{/tex} g
Now, for 3rd half life its initial weight will be {tex} \frac{300}{4} g=300 \times 2^{-2}{/tex} g So, {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{300}{4}=\frac{300}{2^{3}}=300 \times 2^{-3}{/tex} g will be decayed or decreased in 3rd half-life.
So, amount remaining after 3 half lives will be (original weight at the start of third half life-decayed weight in third half-life) = {tex}\frac{300}{4}-\frac{300}{8}=\frac{300}{8} g=300 \times 2^{-3}{/tex} g
So, after 3 half-lives 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-3 g of substance is remaining, which is equal to {tex}\frac{300}{8}{/tex}=37.5 g
We, can observe from above all 3 half lives that after nth half-life, amount of substance remaining is 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-n
So, it is clear that expression 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 2-n can be used to find the amount of the substance that remains after n half-lives.

Q 155

Consider a quantity of a radioactive substance. The fraction of this quantity that remains after t half-lives can be found by using the expression 3-t.

  1.  What fraction of the substance remains after 7 half-lives?
  2. After how many half-lives will the fraction be {tex}\frac{1}{243}{/tex} of the original?
Solution
  1. Since 3-t expression is used for finding the fraction of the quantity that remains after t half-lives.
    Hence, the fraction of substance remains after 7 half-lives will be equal to 3-7, i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{3^{7}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2187}{/tex} 
  2. Given, t half-lives = 3-t 
    So, {tex}\frac{1}{243}=3^{-t}{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{3^{5}}=\frac{1}{3^{t}}{/tex}  [{tex}\because{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 243] and [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}a^{(-m)}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}{/tex}]
    On comparing both sides, we get t = 5 half-lives.

Q 181

Shikha has an order from a golf course designer to put palm trees through a ({tex}\times{/tex}23) machine and then through a ({tex}\times{/tex}33) machine. She thinks that she can do the job with a single repeater machine. What single repeater machine she should use?

Let W1 be the work done by ({tex}\times{/tex}23)machine.
Let W2 be the work done by ({tex}\times{/tex}33) machine.
Work done by both the machines is Wt = W1{tex}\times{/tex}W2
Wt = 23{tex}\times{/tex}33
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Wt = 2{tex}\times{/tex}2{tex}\times{/tex}2{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3 = 8{tex}\times{/tex}27 = 216
If a single repeater has to be used, then the machine is of the form ({tex}\times{/tex} Am) where A and m are both natural numbers.
216 = 6{tex}\times{/tex}6{tex}\times{/tex}6 = 63
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A = 6, m = 3.
Therefore, Shikha should use a ({tex}\times{/tex}63) single repeater machine.

Q 191

The diameter of the Sun is 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 109m and the diameter of the Earth is 1.2756 {tex}\times{/tex} 107m. Compare their diameters by division.

Solution

Diameter of the Sun = 1.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 109m
Diameter of the Earth = 1.2756 {tex}\times{/tex} 107m
For comparison, we have to change both diameter in same powers of 10 i.e. 1.2756 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 = 0.012756 {tex}\times{/tex} 109
Hence, if we divide the diameter of the Sun by diameter of Earth, we get, {tex}\frac{1.4 \times 10^{9}}{0.012756 \times 10^{9}}=110{/tex}
So, the diameter of the Sun is 110 times the diameter of Earth.

Solution

= 1.496 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 m = 1496 {tex}\times{/tex} 108m
The distance between the Earth and the Moon is 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m
The distance between the Moon and the Sun at a particular time (solar eclipse) = (1496 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 - 3.84 {tex}\times{/tex} 108)m
= 1492.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 108m

Q 194

Since, (-5) {tex}\times{/tex} (-5) {tex}\times{/tex} (-5) = - 125 = (-5)3 and 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 343 = (7)3
Exponential form of {tex}\frac{-125}{343}=\frac{(-5)^{3}}{(7)^{3}}=\left(\frac{-5}{7}\right)^{3}{/tex}

Q 208

Express {tex}\frac{400}{3969}{/tex} in exponential form.

Solution

Given, {tex}\frac{400}{3969}{/tex}
Since, 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 400 = (20)2 and 63 {tex}\times{/tex} 63 = 3969 = (63)2
Exponential form of {tex}\frac{400}{3969}=\frac{(20)^{2}}{(63)^{2}}=\left(\frac{20}{63}\right)^{2}{/tex}

Q 209

Express {tex}\frac{-625}{10000}{/tex} in exponential form.

Solution

Given, {tex}\frac{-625}{10000}{/tex}
Since, 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 625 = (5)4 and 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10000 = (10)4
Exponential form of {tex}\frac{-625}{10000}=\frac{-(5)^{4}}{(10)^{4}}=-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{4}{/tex}

Q 210

Simplify: {tex}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{3}\right]^{-1} \times 2^{-3}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}\left[\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{3}\right]^{-1} \times 2^{-3}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{64}\right)^{-1} \times 2^{-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{16-1}{64}\right)^{-1} \times 2^{-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\frac{15}{64}\right)^{-1} \times 2^{-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{64}{15} \times \frac{1}{8} \quad \quad\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{8}{15}{/tex}

Q 211

Simplify: {tex}[(\frac{4}{3})^{-2}-(\frac{3}{4})^{2}]^{(-2)}{/tex}

Solution

Given, {tex}[(\frac{4}{3})^{-2}-(\frac{3}{4})^{2}]^{(-2)} = \left[(\frac{3}{4})^2- (\frac{3}{4})^2\right]^{-2}{/tex} = [0]-2 = 0

Q 212

Simplify: {tex}\left(\frac{4}{13}\right)^{4} \times\left(\frac{13}{7}\right)^{2} \times\left(\frac{7}{4}\right)^{3}{/tex}

Solution

we have , {tex}\left(\frac{4}{13}\right)^{4} \times\left(\frac{13}{7}\right)^{2} \times\left(\frac{7}{4}\right)^{3}{/tex} 
{tex}\left(\frac{4}{13}\right)^{4} \times\left(\frac{13}{7}\right)^{2} \times\left(\frac{7}{4}\right)^{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(4)^{4}}{(13)^{4}} \times \frac{(13)^{2}}{(7)^{2}} \times \frac{(7)^{3}}{(4)^{3}}{/tex}
= (4)4 {tex}\times{/tex} (4)-3 {tex}\times{/tex} (13)2 {tex}\times{/tex} (13)-4 {tex}\times{/tex} (7)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (7)-2  {tex}\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
= (4)4-3 {tex}\times{/tex} (13)2-4  {tex}\times{/tex} (7)3-2   {tex}\left[\because a^{m} \times a^{n}=a^{m-n}\right]{/tex}
= (4)1 {tex}\times{/tex} (13)-2  {tex}\times{/tex} (7)1
= {tex}4 \times \frac{1}{169} \times 7{/tex}  {tex}\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{28}{169}{/tex}

Q 213

Simplify: {tex}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{45} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-60}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{+28} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-43}{/tex}
 

Solution

We have, {tex}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{45} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-60}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{+28} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-43}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{45} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-60}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{+28} \times\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{-43}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{(5)^{45}} \times \frac{1}{(5)^{-60}}-\frac{1}{(5)^{28}} \times \frac{1}{(5)^{-43}}{/tex}
 = {tex}\frac{1}{(5)^{{45}-60}}-\frac{1}{(5)^{{28}-43}}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\because a^{m} \times a^{n}=(a)^{m+n}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{(5)^{-15}}-\frac{1}{(5)^{-15}}=(5)^{15}-(5)^{15}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
= 0

Q 214

Simplify: {tex}\frac{(9)^{3} \times 27 \times t^{4}}{(3)^{-2} \times(3)^{4} \times t^{2}}{/tex}

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{(9)^{3} \times 27 \times t^{4}}{(3)^{-2} \times(3)^{4} \times t^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{\left(3^{2}\right)^{3} \times(3)^{3} \times t^{4}}{(3)^{-2} \times(3)^{4} \times t^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{(3)^{6} \times(3)^{3} \times t^{4}}{(3)^{-2} \times(3)^{4} \times t^{2}}{/tex}
= (3)6 {tex}\times{/tex} (3)3 {tex}\times{/tex} (3)2 {tex}\times{/tex} (3)-4 {tex}\times{/tex}  t4 {tex}\times{/tex}  t-2  {tex}\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
= (3)11-4 {tex}\times{/tex} t4-2 {tex}\left[\because a^{m} \times a^{n}=a^{m+n}\right]{/tex}
= 37 {tex}\times{/tex} t2

Q 215

Simplify: {tex}\frac{\left(3^{-2}\right)^{2} \times\left(5^{2}\right)^{-3} \times\left(t^{-3}\right)^{2}}{\left(3^{-2}\right)^{5} \times\left(5^{3}\right)^{-2} \times\left(t^{-4}\right)^{3}}{/tex}

Solution

We have, {tex}\frac{\left(3^{-2}\right)^{2} \times\left(5^{2}\right)^{-3} \times\left(t^{-3}\right)^{2}}{\left(3^{-2}\right)^{5} \times\left(5^{3}\right)^{-2} \times\left(t^{-4}\right)^{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3)^{-4} \times(5)^{-6} \times(t)^{-6}}{(3)^{-10} \times(5)^{-6} \times(t)^{-12}}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\because\left(a^{m}\right)^{n}=(a)^{m n}\right]{/tex}
= (3)-4 {tex}\times{/tex} (3)10 {tex}\times{/tex} (5)-6 {tex}\times{/tex} (5)6 {tex}\times{/tex} (t)-6{tex}\times{/tex} (t)12
=  (3)-4+10 {tex}\times{/tex} (5)-6+6 {tex}\times{/tex} (t)-6+12  {tex}\left[\because a^{-m}=\frac{1}{a^{m}}\right]{/tex}
=  (3)6 {tex}\times{/tex} 50 {tex}\times{/tex} (t)6 = (3t)6

Q 2

Q 3

Q 4

Amrita takes 18 hours to travel 720 kilometres. Time taken by her to travel 360 kilometres is ________hours.

Q 5

If x and y are inversely proportional then ________ = k where k is positive constant.

Q 6

Side of a rhombus and its perimeter are in ________ proportion.

Q 7

When two quantities x and y are in inverse proportion, then {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} is a constant.

Q 8

If the cost of 10 pencils is Rs.90, then the cost of 19 pencils is Rs.171.

Q 9

If 5 persons can finish a job in 10 days then one person will finish it in 2 days.

Q 10

In a scout camp, there is food provision for 300 cadets for 42 days. If 50 more persons join the camp, for how many days will the provision last?

Solution

As we know that more the number of persons, the sooner would be the provision exhausted.
So, this is a case of inverse proportion.
Let the required number of days be x.
Hence, 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 = (300 + 50) {tex}\times{/tex} x
300 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 = 350 {tex}\times{/tex} x
{tex}\frac{300 \times 42}{350}=x{/tex}
x = 36

Q 11

If two cardboard boxes occupy 500 cubic centimetres space, then how much space is required to keep 200 such boxes?

Solution

As we know that the number of boxes increases, the space required to keep them also increases.
So, this is a case of direct proportion.

Number of boxes 2 200
Space occupied (in cubic centimeters) 500 x

{tex}\text { So } \therefore \frac{2}{500}=\frac{200}{x}{/tex} 
 2x = 500 {tex}\times{/tex} 200 
x = {tex}x=\frac{500 \times 200}{2}{/tex}
x = 50,000. 
Thus, the required space is 50,000 cubic centimeters.

Q 12

Under the condition that the temperature remains constant, the volume of gas is inversely proportional to its pressure. If the volume of gas is 630 cubic centimetres at a pressure of 360 mm of mercury, then what will be the pressure of the gas if its volume is 720 cubic centimetres at the same temperature?

Solution

As it is clearly given that, at a constant temperature, pressure and volume of a gas are inversely proportional. Let the required pressure be x.

Volume of gas (in cubic centimeters) 630 720
Pressure of gas (in mm) 360 x

Then, 630 {tex}\times{/tex} 360 = 720 {tex}\times{/tex} x
{tex}\frac{630 \times 360}{720}=x{/tex}
x = 315
Therefore, the required pressure is 315 mm of mercury.

Q 21

Q 24

Q 26

x ________ 60
y 2 10
Solution

since x varies inversely as y-
{tex}\Rightarrow x \propto \frac{1}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x y=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}60 \times 10{/tex} = k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 600
For y = 2
{tex}x=\frac{k}{y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{600}{2}{/tex} = 300

Q 27

x 12 6
y 48 _________
Q 28

Q 31

Q 54

When two quantities are related in such a manner that, if one increases, the other also increases, then they always vary directly.

Q 55

When two quantities are related in such a manner that if one increases and the other decreases, then they always vary inversely.

Q 56

If x varies inversely as y and when x = 6, y = 8, then for x = 8, the value of y is 10.

Q 57

The number of workers and the time to complete a job is a case of direct proportion.

Q 58

For fixed time period and rate of interest, the simple interest is directly proportional to the principal.

Q 59

The area of cultivated land and the crop harvested is a case of direct proportion.

Q 60

Which of the following vary directly and which vary inversely with each other and which are neither of the two?

  1. The time taken by a train to cover a fixed distance and the speed of the train.
  2. The distance travelled by CNG bus and the amount of CNG used.
  3. The number of people working and the time to complete a given work.
  4. Income tax and the income.
  5. Distance travelled by an auto-rickshaw and time taken.
Solution
  1. The time taken by a train to cover a fixed distance and the speed of the train are inversely proportional. e.g Let a train cover 100 km in 1 h with speed 100 km/h. Then, the same train cover 100 km in 30 min with speed 200 km/h.
  2. The distance travelled by CNG bus and the amount of CNG used are directly proportional. e.g. Let a CNG bus can travel 10 km in 1 kg of CNG. Then, the same CNG bus travelled 20 km in 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2 kg of CNG.
  3. The number of people working and the time to complete a given work are inversely proportional to each other. e.g. Let 20 workers can complete a work in 1day. Then, 10 workers can complete the same work in 2 days.
  4. Income tax and the income are directly proportional to each other, e.g. Let Mr. X have 4.5 lakh annual income. Then, he pay 10% income tax on his income. But if Mr. X have 5.5 lakh annual income, then he has to pay 30% income tax on his salary/income.
  5. Distance travelled by an auto-rickshaw and time taken are directly proportional to each other. e.g. Let an auto-rickshaw takes 2 h to travel 10 km. Then, it will take 4 h to travel 20 km.
Q 61

Which of the following vary directly and which vary inversely with each other and which are neither of the two?

  1. Number of students in a hostel and consumption of food.
  2. Area of the walls of a room and the cost of whitewashing the walls.
  3. The number of people working and the quantity of work.
  4. Simple interest on a given sum and the rate of interest.
  5. Compound interest on a given sum and the sum invested.
Solution
  1. Number of students in a hostel and consumption of food are directly proportional to each other. e.g. Let 200 students in a hostel can consume 100 kg of rice in a month. Then, 400 students in a hostel can consume 200 kg of rice in a month.
  2. Area of the walls of a room and the cost of whitewashing the walls are directly proportional to each other. e.g. Let ₹1000 required for whitewashing a room with (12 {tex}\times{/tex} 8) m size. Then, ₹2000 required for whitewashing a room with (12 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) m size.
  3. The number of people working and the quantity of work are directly proportional to each other. e.g. Let 20 workers can complete 20% of a work. Then, 40 workers can complete 40% of the same work.
  4. Simple interest on a given sum and the rate of interest are directly proportional to each other.
    e.g. Let P = ₹1000, R = 10% and T = 1 yr
    {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{SI}=\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}=\frac{1000 \times 10 \times 1}{100}{/tex} = ₹ 100
    But if P = 1000, R = 20% and T = 1 yr
    {tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm{SI}=\frac{P \times R \times T}{100}=\frac{1000 \times 20 \times 1}{100}{/tex} = ₹200
  5. Compound interest on a given sum and the sum invested are neither depend directly nor inversely.
Q 62

which of the following vary directly and which vary inversely with each other and which are neither of the two?

  1. The quantity of rice and its cost.
  2. The height of a tree and the number of years.
  3. Increase in cost and number of shirts that can be purchased, if the budget remains the same.
  4.  Area of land and its cost.
  5. Sales tax and the amount of the bill.
Solution
  1. The quantity of rice and its cost are directly proportional to each other, e.g. Let 1 kg of rice price = ₹40 Then, 2 kg of rice price = ₹2 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = ₹80
  2. The height of a tree and the number of years are neither directly nor inversely proportional to each other.
  3. Increase in cost and number of shirts that can be purchased, if the budget remains the same are inversely proportional to each other.
    e.g. Let 2 shirts price = ₹800
    After increasing in price of each shirt,
    1 shirt price became ₹800
    where budget = ₹800
  4. Area of land and its cost are directly proportional to each other,
    e.g. Let 200 m2 land cost = ₹5000
    Then, 400 m2 land cost = ₹10000
  5. Sales tax and the amount of the bill are directly proportional to each other,
    e.g. Let bill amount = ₹1000
    Sales tax = 10%
    Then, sales tax = {tex}\frac{10}{100} \times 1000{/tex} = ₹100
    But if, bill amount = 2000
    Sales tax = 10%
    Then, sales tax {tex}\frac{10}{100} \times 2000=10 \times 20{/tex} = ₹200
Q 63

If x varies inversely as y and x = 20 when y = 600, find y when x = 400.

Solution

If x varies inversely as y.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy = k(constant) ...... (i)
If x =20 and y = 600
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 = 12000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 12000
When x = 400, then from Eq. (i),
y {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} 400 = 12000 [putting the value of k]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y=\frac{12000}{400}=30{/tex}

Q 64

The variable x varies directly as y and x = 80 when y is 160. What is y when x is 64?

Solution

If x varies directly as y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{y} = k{/tex} (constant)
If x = 80 and y = 160
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{y}=\frac{80}{160}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow k=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
When x = 64, then from Eq. (i),
{tex}\frac{64}{y}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 64 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 128 [putting the value of k]

Q 65

l varies directly as m and l = 5, when m = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}. Find l when m = {tex}\frac{16}{3}{/tex}.

Solution

If l varies directly as m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{l}{m}{/tex} = k = (constant)..........(i)
If l = 5 and m = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{l}{m}=\frac{5}{2 / 3}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5}{1} \times \frac{3}{2}=\frac{15}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad k=\frac{15}{2}{/tex}
When m= {tex}\frac{16}{3}{/tex} then from Eq. (i),
{tex}\frac{l}{16 / 3}=\frac{15}{2}{/tex} [putting the value of k]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad l=\frac{15}{2} \times \frac{16}{3}=40{/tex}

Q 66

If x varies inversely as y and y = 60 when x = 1.5. Find x, when y = 4.5.

Solution

If x varies inversely as y.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy = k (constant) .....(i)
If xy = 1.5 and y = 60
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 90
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 90
When y = 4.5, then from Eq. (i),
4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} x = k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} x = 90 [putting the value of k]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{90}{4.5}=20{/tex}

Q 67

In a camp, there is enough flour for 300 persons for 42 days. How long will the flour last, if 20 more persons join the camp?

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} For 300 persons flour is enough for 42 days.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For 1 person flour will be enough for = 300 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 = 12600 days
Now, 20 more persons join the camp.
So, total persons = 300 + 20 = 320
{tex}\therefore{/tex} For 320 persons flour enough = {tex}\frac{12600}{320}=\frac{315}{8} =39\frac{3}{8} \text { days }{/tex}

Q 68

A contractor undertook a contract to complete a part of a stadium in 9 months with a team of 560 persons. Later on, it was required to complete the job in 5 months. How many extra persons should he employ to complete the work?

Solution

{tex}\because{/tex} In 9 months, a part of the stadium can complete by 560 persons
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In 1 month, the work can be completed by 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 560= 5040persons 5040
{tex}\because{/tex} In 5 months, the work can be completed by {tex}\frac{5040}{5}{/tex} = 1008 persons
Now, the number of extra persons required to complete the work in 5 months = 1008 - 560 = 448

Q 69

Sobi types 108 words in 6 minutes. How many words would she type in half an hour?

Solution

Sobi can types 108 words in 6 min
In 1 min, she can type = {tex}\frac{108}{6}{/tex} = 18 words
Thus, in 30 min, she can type = 18 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 540 words

Q 70

A car covers a distance in 40 minutes with an average speed of 60 km/h. What should be the average speed to cover the same distance in 25 minutes?

Solution

A car covers a distance in 40 min with an average speed = 60 km/h = {tex}\frac{60 \times 1000}{60} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
In 1 min, the same distance can be cover with speed = {tex}\frac{60 \times 1000 \times 40}{60}{/tex} = 40000 m/min
In 25 min, the same distance can be cover with speed = {tex}\frac{40000}{25}{/tex} = 1600 m/min
= {tex}\frac{1600}{1000} \times 60=16 \times 6{/tex} = 96km/h {tex}\left[\because 1 \mathrm{m}=\frac{1}{1000} \mathrm{km} \text { and } 1 \mathrm{min}=\frac{1}{60} \mathrm{h}\right]{/tex}

Q 71

It is given that l varies directly as m.

  1. Write an equation that relates to l and m.
  2. Find the constant of proportion (k), when l is 6, then m is 18.
  3. Find l, when m is 33.
  4. Find m, when l is 8.
Solution

It is given that l varies directly as m. 

  1. Equation related to l and m is {tex}\frac{l}{m}=k{/tex} (constant)
  2. If l = 6 and m = 18, then k = {tex}\frac{6}{18}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
  3. If m - 33, then
    {tex}\frac{l}{m}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{l}{33}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow l=\frac{33}{3}=11{/tex}
  4. If l = 8
    {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{l}{m}=k \Rightarrow \frac{8}{m}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because k=\frac{1}{3}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow m=8 \times 3=24{/tex}
Q 72

If a deposit of ₹2000 earns an interest of ₹500 in 3 years, how much interest would a deposit of ₹36000 earn in 3 years with the same rate of simple interest?

Solution

If a deposit of ₹2000 earns in 3 yrs with an interest = ₹500
Then, a deposit of ₹1000 earns in 3 yrs with an interest = ₹250
Similarly, deposit of ₹3600 i.e.₹36 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 earns in 3 yrs with an interest = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 36= ₹9000

Q 73

The mass of an aluminium rod varies directly with its length. If a 16 cm long rod has a mass of 192g, find the length of the rod whose mass is 105g.

Solution

According to the question, the mass (m) of an aluminium rod varies directly with its length
(l). Here, we use the direct proportion.
In direct proportion, {tex}\frac{m}{l}=k{/tex} (constant)
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{{m}}{l}=\frac{192}{16}=12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 12

If mass of the rod = 105 g
Then, {tex}\frac{m}{l}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{105}{l}=12{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}l=\frac{105}{12}=8.75 \mathrm{cm}{/tex}

Q 74

Find the values of x and y, if a and b are in inverse proportion.

a 12 x 8
b 30 5 y
Solution

In an inverse proportion, When we multiply the first quantity by any constant k, then another quantity is divided by the same constant k. Given,

a 12 x 8
b 30 5 y

Here, we see that in part, (b) when we divide 30 by 6, we will get 5.
So in part (a), we will get the value of x,
i.e. x = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 = 72
Similarly, in part (a), when we divide x i.e., 72 by 9, we will get 8.
So in part (b), we will get the value of y.
i.e. y = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 45

{tex}\because{/tex} Naresh walks 250 steps to cover distance = 200 m

{tex}\because{/tex} in 350 steps, he covers = {tex}\frac{200}{250} \times 350=\frac{20 \times 350}{25}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{7000}{25}=280 \mathrm{m}{/tex}

 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} p = 108  [putting the value of k]

Q 84

Q 88

Campus and Welfare Committee of the school is planning to develop a blue shade for painting the entire school building. For this purpose, various shades are tried by mixing containers of blue paint and white paint. In each of the following mixtures, decide which is a lighter shade of blue and also find the lightest blue shade among all of them.

If one container has one-litre paint and the building requires 105 litres for painting, how many containers of each type is required to paint the building by darkest blue shade?

Solution
  1. In the mixture A,
    The number of blue containers = 3
    The number of white containers = 4
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{3}{4}=0.75{/tex}
  2. In the mixture B,
    The number of blue containers = 3
    The number of white containers = 3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{3}{3}=1{/tex}
    Clearly, mixture A would be the lighter shade.
  3. In the mixture C
    The number of blue containers = 3
    The number of white containers = 3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{3}{3}=1{/tex}
  4. In the mixture D,
    The number of blue containers = 2
    The number of white containers = 5
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{2}{5}=0.4{/tex}
    Clearly, mixture (D) would be a lighter shade, since for lighter shade white containers should be more than blue containers.
  5. In the mixture E
    The number of blue containers = 6
    The number of white containers = 1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{6}{1}=6{/tex}
  6. In the mixture F,
    The number of blue containers = 4
    The number of white containers = 2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{4}{2}=2{/tex}
    Clearly, mixture (F) would be a lighter shade, since for lighter shade white containers should be more than blue containers.
  7. In the mixture G
    The number of blue containers = 3
    The number of white containers = 3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{3}{3}=1{/tex}
  8. In the mixture H,
    The number of blue containers = 4
    The number of white containers = 3
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The ratio of blue and white = {tex}\frac{4}{3}=1.33{/tex}
    It is clear that mixture (G) would be a lighter shade since for lighter shade white containers should be more than blue containers.

From the above all mixtures, mixture D is the lightest among them.
It is given that the total number of containers required for painting = 105 L.
Therefore,  the number of blue containers required for painting = {tex}\frac{2}{7} \times 105{/tex}
= {tex}2 \times 15=30{/tex} lt.
and the number of white containers required for painting = {tex}\frac{5}{7} \times 105{/tex}
= {tex}5 \times 15=75{/tex} lt.

Q 91

44 cows can graze a field in 9 days. How many less/more cows will graze the same field in 12 days?

Q 93

Shabnam takes 20 minutes to reach her school, if she goes at a speed of 6 km/h. If she wants to reach school in 24 minutes, what should be her speed?

Solution

Shabnam’s speed = 6 km/h = {tex}\frac{6 \times 1000}{60} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total distance covered by Shabnam in 20 min = {tex}\frac{6 \times 1000}{60} \times 20{/tex} [{tex}\therefore{/tex} distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time]
= {tex}\frac{1000}{10} \times 20=100 \times 20{/tex}
= 2000m
If she want to reach the school in 24 min, then she should maintain the speed = {tex}\frac{2000}{24}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1000}{12}=\frac{500}{6} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{min}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{500 \times 60}{1000 \times 6}=5 \mathrm{km} / \mathrm{h} \quad\left[\because 1 \mathrm{km} \frac{1}{1000} \text { and } 1 \mathrm{h}=60 \mathrm{min}\right]{/tex}

Q 94

Ravi starts for his school at 8:20 am on his bicycle. If he travels at a speed of 10 km/h, then he reaches his school late by 8 minutes but on travelling at 16 km/h, he reaches the school 10 minutes early. At what time does school start?

Solution

Let the total distance = x km
Let the time taken by Ravi to reach the school at sharp time = t min
If the speed of the bicycle is 10 km/h, then he reach his school late by 8 min.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{x}{10}=t+\frac{8}{60}{/tex} {tex}\left[\because 1 \min =\frac{1}{60} h\right]{/tex} .....(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{10}=t+\frac{2}{15}{/tex}
If the speed of the bicycle is 16 km/h, then he reaches his school 10 min early.
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{x}{16}=t-\frac{10}{60}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{16}=t-\frac{1}{6}{/tex} .....(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{x}{10}-\frac{x}{16}=\frac{2}{15}+\frac{1}{6}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{8 x-5 x}{80}=\frac{4+5}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{3 x}{80}=\frac{9}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{9 \times 80}{30 \times 3}=8 \mathrm{km}{/tex} [by cross-multiplication]
Now, put x = 8 in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac{8}{10}=t+\frac{2}{15}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{8}{10}-\frac{2}{15}=\frac{24-4}{30}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{20}{30}=\frac{2}{3} h{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2}{3} \times 60=40 \mathrm{min}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} 1h = 60 min]
Hence, the starting time of school is 8:20 + 40 min i.e. 9: 00 am.

Q 99

The table shows the time four elevators take to travel various distances. Find, which elevator is fastest and which is slowest.

  Distance (in m) Time (in s)
Elevator A 435 29
Elevator B 448 28
Elevator C 130 10
Elevator D 85 5

How much distance will be travelled elevators B and C separately in 140 sec? Who travelled more and by how much?

Solution

On the basis of given table,
Elevator A takes 29 s to cover 435 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance covered by elevator A in 1 s = {tex}\frac{435}{29}{/tex}= 15 m
Elevator B takes 28 s to cover 448 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance covered by elevator A in 1 s = {tex}\frac{448}{28}{/tex}= 16 m
Elevator B takes 28 s to cover 130 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance covered by elevator A in 1 s = {tex}\frac{130}{13}{/tex}= 13 m
Elevator B takes 28 s to cover 85 m.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Distance covered by elevator A in 1 s = {tex}\frac{85}{5}{/tex}= 17 m
Hence, in 1s, elevator D covers more distance as compare to elevators A, B and C. So, elevator D is fastest, while elevator C covers least. Hence, elevator C is slowest.
Now, elevator B covers distance in 140 s = 140 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 2240 m [distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time]
Elevator C covers distance in 140s = 140 {tex}\times{/tex} 13 = 1820m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Elevator B covers more distance than C = 2240 -1820 = 420m

= {tex}2 \times 1000{/tex}
= 2000 m/min
Now, speed in m/s = {tex}\frac{2000}{60}=\frac{200}{6}=\frac{100}{3} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex}1min=60 s]
So, 20 m can be cover in {tex}\frac{20}{\frac {100}{3}}=\frac{60}{100}{/tex} = 0.6s {tex}\left[\because \text { time }=\frac{\text { distance }}{\text { speed }}\right]{/tex}

Q 104

The variable x is inversely proportional to y. If increases by p%, then by what per cent will y decrease?

Solution

The variable x is inversely proportional to y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xy = k (constant)
Since we know that two quantities x and y are said to be in inverse proportion if an increase in x causes a proportional decrease in y and vice-versa.
So, we can say y decrease by p%.

Q 105

Here is a keyboard of a harmonium.

  1. Find the ratio of white keys to black keys on the keyboard.
  2.  What is the ratio of black keys to all keys on the given keyboard?
  3. This pattern of keys is repeated on larger keyboard. How many black keys would you expect to find on a keyboard with 14 such patterns?
Solution

According to the given figure in the question,

  1. The total number of black keys = 7
    The total number of white keys = 10
    Hence, the ratio of white keys to black keys on the keyboard = {tex}\frac {10}{7}{/tex}
  2. The total number of keys = 10 + 7 = 17
    The ratio of black keys to total keys on the given keyboard = {tex}\frac {7}{17}{/tex}
  3. Black keys in 1 keyboard = 7
    Black keys in 14 such keyboards = 14 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 98 keys
Q 106

The following table shows the distance travelled by one of the new eco-friendly energy-efficient car travelled on gas.

Litres of gas 1 0.5 2 2.5 3 5
Distance (in km) 15 7.5 30 37.5 45 75

Which type of properties are indicated by the table? How much distance will be covered by the car in 8 litres of gas?

Solution

On the basis of the given information in the table, the distance travelled by one of the new eco-friendly energy-efficient cars travelled on gas.
The car travelled 15 km in 1 L of gas. The car travelled 7.5 km in 0.5 L of gas.
The car travelled 30 km in 2 L of gas.
This rate shows a direct proportion between litres of gas and the distance covered
The car can cover the distance in 8 L of gas = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 120km

Q 107

Kritika is following this recipe for bread. She realises her sister used most of sugar syrup for her breakfast. Kritika has only {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} cup of syrup, so she decides to make a small size of bread. How much of each ingredient shall she use?
Bread recipe
1 cup quick cooking oats
2 cups bread flour
{tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} cup sugar syrup
1 tablespoon cooking oil
{tex}1 \frac{1}{3}{/tex} cups water
3 tablespoons yeast
1 teaspoon salt.

Solution

According to the information given, After used of most of sugar syrup for her breakfast, the remaining sugar is {tex}\frac{1}{6} {/tex}  cup of sugar syrup. Thus, it means {tex}1-\frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{6}{/tex} has been used. She need {tex}\frac{1}{3} {/tex} cup of sugar syrup for one piece of bread. So, new quantity of ingredients will be in proportion of  {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} .
Now, the bread recipe will look like
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} cup quick-cooking oats
1 cups bread flour
{tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} cup sugar syrup
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} tablespoon cooking oil
{tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} cups water
{tex}\frac{3}{2}{/tex} tablespoons yeast
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} teaspoon salt.

Q 108

Many schools have a recommended students-teachers ratio as 35:1. Next year, school expects an increase in enrollment by 280 students. How many new teachers will they to appoint to maintain the students-teachers ratio?

Solution

Students-teachers ratio = 35:1
It show every 35 students-one teacher should available in the school.
In the school, number of students increases = 280 students
The number of teachers required for 280 students = {tex}\frac{280}{35}{/tex} = 8 teachers

Q 109

Kusum always forgets how to convert miles to kilometres and back again. However, she remembers that her car's speedometer shows both miles and kilometres. She knows that travelling 50 miles per hour is same as travelling 80 kilometres per hour. To cover a distance of 200 km, how many miles Kusum would have to go?

Solution

50 miles per hour is the same as travelling 80 km per hour.
So, 1 km covers distance in 1 h to {tex}\frac{50}{80}{/tex} miles
To cover a distance of 200 km = {tex}\frac{50}{80} \times 200=\frac{5 \times 200}{8}{/tex} = 125 miles 80
Hence, 125 miles Kusum has to go for 200 km.

Q 110

The student of Anju's class sold posters to raise money. Anju wanted to create a ratio for finding the amount of money, her class would make for different numbers of posters sold. She knew they could raise ₹250 for every 60 posters sold.

  1. How much money would Anju's class make for selling 102 posters?
  2. Could Anju's class raise exactly ₹2000? If so, how many posters would they need to sell? If not, why?
Solution
  1. Every 60 posters, Anju’s class students raise = ₹250
    So, from 1 poster, Anju’s class students raise = {tex}\frac{250}{60}{/tex} 
    = ₹{tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex}
    If Anju sell 102 posters, then they raise = {tex}\frac{25}{6} \times 102{/tex}
    = 17 {tex}\times{/tex} 25
    = ₹425
    Hence, Anju’s class make ₹425 for selling of 102 posters.
  2. Since, by selling 1 poster, Anju’s class raise = ₹{tex}\frac{25}{6}{/tex}
    For raise exactly ₹ 2000, they needs to sell = {tex}2000 \div \frac{25}{6}{/tex}
    = {tex}2000 \times \frac{6}{25}{/tex}
    = 480 posters

Q 5

Factorise : 21x2y3 + 27x3y2.

Solution

We have 21x2y3 + 27x3y2
21x2y3 + 27x3y2 = 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 7 {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} y {tex}\times {/tex} y {tex}\times {/tex} y + 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 {tex}\times {/tex} 3 {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} y {tex}\times {/tex} y
 = 3 {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} x {tex}\times {/tex} y {tex}\times {/tex} y (7y + 9x)      [Using ab + ac = a (b + c)]
= 3x2y2 (7y + 9x)

Q 6

Factorise : a3 - 4a2 + 12 - 3a

Solution

We have given that a3 - 4a2 + 12 - 3a
= a2(a - 4) - 3a + 12
= a2(a - 4) - 3 (a - 4)
= (a - 4) (a2 - 3)    [by using identity a2 – b2 = (a + b) (a – b) ]
{tex}=(a-4)(a+\sqrt3)(a-\sqrt3){/tex}

Q 7

Factorise : 4x2 - 20x + 25

Solution

We have to factorise 4x2 - 20x + 25
= {tex}(2x)^2-2\times2x\times5+(5)^2{/tex}
= (2x - 5)2     [Since a2 - 2ab + b2 = (a - b)2 ]
= (2x - 5) (2x - 5)

Q 8

Factorise : {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{9}-9{/tex}.

Solution

We have {tex}\frac{y^{2}}{9}-9{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{y^{2}}{9}-9{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{y}{3}\right)^{2}-(3)^{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\left(\frac{y}{3}+3\right)\left(\frac{y}{3}-3\right){/tex} [Since a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b)]

Q 9

Factorise : x4 - 256

Solution

x4 - 256
= (x2)2 - (16)2
= (x2 + 16) (x2 - 16) [using a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b)]
= (x2 + 16) (x2 - 42)
= (x2 + 16) (x + 4) (x - 4) [again using a2 - b2 = (a + b) (a - b)]

Q 10

The area of a rectangle is x2 + 12xy + 27y2 and its length is (x + 9y). Find the breadth of the rectangle.

Solution

As we know that  Area of rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth
so, {tex}\text{breadth}=\frac{\text { Area }}{\text { length }}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{x^{2}+12 x y+27 y^{2}}{(x+9 y)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x^{2}+9 x y+3 x y+27 y^{2}}{(x+9 y)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x(x+9 y)+3 y(x+9 y)}{x+9 y}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(x+9 y)(x+3 y)}{(x+9 y)}{/tex}
= {tex}(x+3 y){/tex}

Q 11

Divide: 15 (y + 3) (y2 - 16) by 5 (y2 - y - 12)

Solution

At first Factorising 15 (y + 3) (y2 - 16),
we get  {tex}5\times3\times(y+3)(y-4)(y+4){/tex} 
Now on factorising 5 (y2 - y - 12), we get 5 (y2 - 4y + 3y - 12)
= {tex}5[y(y-4)+3(y-4)]{/tex}
= {tex}5\times(y-4)(y+3){/tex}
Therefore, on dividing the first expression by the second expression, we get {tex}\frac{15(y+3)\left(y^{2}-16\right)}{5\left(y^{2}-y-12\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{5 \times 3 \times(y+3)(y-4)(y+4)}{5 \times(y-4)(y+3)}{/tex}
= {tex}3(y+4){/tex}

Q 28

Write the greatest common factor of the terms: -18a2, 108a

Solution

We have -18a2, 108a
-18a2 = -2{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3 {tex}\times{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} a
108a = 2{tex}\times{/tex}2{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3 {tex}\times{/tex} a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The greatest common factor i.e. GCF = 2{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}a = 18a

Q 29

Write the greatest common factor of the term: 3x2y, 18xy2, - 6xy

Solution

We have, We have, {tex}3x^2y{/tex} = {tex}3\times x\times x \times y{/tex} 
 {tex}18xy^2{/tex} = {tex}2\times3\times3\times x\times y\times y{/tex} 
 {tex}-6xy{/tex} = {tex}-1\times2\times3\times x\times y{/tex}  
 {tex}\therefore{/tex}  {tex}GCF=3x y{/tex}

Q 30

Write the greatest common factor of the term: 2xy, -y2, 2x2y

Solution

We have,

2x2y = {tex}2\times x\times x\times y{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} GCF = y

Q 31

Write the greatest common factor of the terms: l2m2n, lm2n2, l2mn​2

 lm2n2 = {tex}l\times m\times m\times n\times n{/tex}

 {tex}\therefore{/tex} GCF = {tex}lmn{/tex}

Q 32

Write the greatest common factor of the terms: 21pqr, -7p2q2r2, 49p2qr

Solution

We have, 21pqr = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} r
-7p2q2r2 = -7 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} r
49p2qr = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} r
The greatest common factor of the terms 21pqr, -7p2q2r2 and 49p2qr = 7pqr

pryz = p {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} z
rxyz = r {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} z
The greatest common factor of the terms = ry

Q 34

Write the greatest common factor of the terms: 3x3y2z, –6xy3z2, 12x2yz3

Solution

We have, 3x3y2z = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} z
-6xy3z2 = - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} z {tex}\times{/tex} z
12x2yz3 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} y {tex}\times{/tex} z {tex}\times{/tex} z {tex}\times{/tex} z
The greatest common factor of the terms = 3xyz

Q 35

Write the greatest common factor of the terms: 63p2a2r2s, -9pq2r2s2, 15p2qr2s2, -60p2a2rs2

We have,
63p2a2r2s = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} s
-9pq2r2s2 = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} s {tex}\times{/tex} s
15p2qr2s2 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} q {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} s {tex}\times{/tex} s
-60p2a2rs2 = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} p {tex}\times{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} a {tex}\times{/tex} r {tex}\times{/tex} s {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The greatest common factor of the terms = 3prs

{tex}\therefore{/tex}The greatest common factor of the terms = 1

 = 6b (a + 2c)

- xy - ay = -y(x + a)

l2m2n - lm2n2 - l2mn2 = Imn (Im - mn - In)

Factorise the expression: 3pqr - 6p2q2r2 - 15r2.

Solution

3pqr - 6p2q2r2 - 15r2 
= 3pqr - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2p2q2r2 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 5r2
= 3r(pq - 2p2q2r - 5r)

Q 43

Factorise the expression: x3y2 + x2y3 - xy4 + xy.

Solution
